title: Phase 3 — mBERT attention + persistent homology
description: Main experiment. Extract mBERT attention matrices, convert to distance matrices, compute PH, extract topological features. Per Kushnareva methodology.

title: Emit final-layer mBERT embeddings as sidecar of phase3 attention extraction
description:
## Summary

Piggyback final-layer mBERT contextual embeddings onto the existing
`phase3_thresholds.ipynb` forward pass, so each KWIC occurrence produces both
its attention tensor (Kushnareva path) and its final-layer hidden state
(Draganov-style sidecar input) in a single mBERT call. Save embeddings to
`data/phase3/embeddings/`, alongside a manifest mapping each row to
`(lang, term, kwic_row_id, target_wp_start, target_wp_end)`.

This is **emit only**. The analysis side — kNN cloud expansion in mBERT
space, persistent homology per (lang, color region), blue-region comparison
across en/ru/es — is a separate sibling epic to be planned once the data
exists on disk.

## Why this shape

- The mBERT forward pass is the slow part of phase3 extraction. With
  `output_hidden_states=True` set at model load, `outputs['hidden_states']`
  carries the per-layer hidden states for ~zero extra compute. No
  `register_forward_hook` needed.
- Storing the **final layer, full MAX_LEN=32 sequence** at fp16 keeps the
  pooling-over-target-wordpieces decision live (mean over WP span vs. first
  WP vs. context-window-pooled). Disk cost ≈ 110 MB per (lang, color),
  ~330 MB total across en+ru+es.
- Wordpiece alignment is captured at extraction time. The KWIC CSV's
  `target_idx` is in whitespace-token space; we record the corresponding
  wordpiece span `(target_wp_start, target_wp_end)` per row in the manifest,
  using `BertTokenizerFast.word_ids()`.

## Sibling epics

- `ph-project-1iq` — static fastText version of the Draganov-style sidecar
  (CC-300 + MUSE neighborhoods). This epic is the contextual-mBERT
  counterpart's *upstream data emit*. The contextual analysis epic itself
  will be planned separately once embeddings are on disk.
- `ph-project-mwk` / `ph-project-0cm` — Phase 3 mBERT attention + PH
  (Kushnareva path). This epic shares the threshold-notebook forward pass
  with that work and does not modify the attention output.

## Subtasks

- **`<this-id>.1`** — Add `grab_attention_and_embeddings` to
  `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` + tests. No notebook changes.
- **`<this-id>.2`** — Adapt `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` to also emit
  final-layer embeddings + per-row manifest. Depends on `.1`.

## Layout

- `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` — extended with a new sibling function
  alongside the unchanged `grab_attention_weights`.
- `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` — DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE markers added
  on cells 1 (disk budget), 2 (imports), 10 (paths), 18 (tokenizer), 25
  (model + fast tokenizer load), 26 (extraction loop), plus a new trailing
  `KEEP_EMBEDDINGS` cleanup cell.
- `data/phase3/embeddings/{lang}_{domain}_final_layer_MAX_LEN_{N}_{model}_partXofN.npy`
  — embedding tensors, gitignored (mirrors `data/phase3/attentions/`).
- `data/phase3/embeddings/{lang}_{domain}_manifest.parquet` — per-row
  manifest with target wordpiece spans.

## Out of scope

- Tapping `notebooks/phase3_ripser_templates.ipynb` (currently unadapted —
  that's `mwk.3` territory). Once this epic lands, that notebook stays
  attention-only and consumes the embeddings produced here only if needed.
- Cloud expansion / PH analysis on the emitted embeddings (separate epic, to
  be planned once data is on disk).
- Emotion + kinship domains (paused per CLAUDE.md "Current scope").
- Saving more than the final layer. If a future experiment wants intermediate
  layers (Tenney et al. 2019: lexical info peaks ~layers 7–9), that's a small
  extension to the function and a 13× disk increase — file as follow-up.

## Acceptance

- `tests/test_grab_weights_compat.py` covers the new function (shape, dtype,
  word_ids structure, slow-tokenizer rejection).
- `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` runs end-to-end on `(lang="en", domain="color")` and produces:
  - existing attention `.npy` parts under `data/phase3/attentions/`, byte-identical to a pre-change run for the same input
  - new embedding `.npy` parts under `data/phase3/embeddings/`, shape `(batch, 32, 768)` fp16
  - new `*_manifest.parquet` with row count equal to `len(data)` and `target_wp_start` populated for the vast majority of rows
- `python -m py_compile replication/grab_weights_compat.py` clean.
- All DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE markers added per project convention
  (see auto-memory `feedback_notebook_faithful_phase3.md`).

title: Add grab_attention_and_embeddings to grab_weights_compat.py + tests
description:
## Summary

Add a new function `grab_attention_and_embeddings` to
`replication/grab_weights_compat.py` that runs mBERT's forward pass once and
returns BOTH the attention tensor (existing shape) and the final-layer
hidden-state tensor + per-batch `word_ids` (for downstream wordpiece-target
alignment). No notebook changes in this task — that lands in `16v.2`.

The existing `grab_attention_weights` function is **not modified**. This is
purely additive: a new sibling function with a wider return signature.

## Files to modify

- `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` (extend — add new function alongside
  the existing `grab_attention_weights`, do not modify the existing function)
- `tests/test_grab_weights_compat.py` (extend with new tests for the new
  function; existing tests stay as-is)

## Files to read for context

- `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` — existing shim (file is ~50 lines)
- `reference/grab_weights.py` — original (frozen), shape/return docs
- `tests/test_grab_weights_compat.py` — existing test patterns and fixtures
  (note: existing `mbert` fixture is loaded with only `output_attentions=True`
  and uses a slow tokenizer — DO NOT mutate it; add a new fixture instead)

## Implementation steps

1. **Add new function** to `replication/grab_weights_compat.py`:

```python
def grab_attention_and_embeddings(
    model, tokenizer, sentences, MAX_LEN, device="cuda:0"
):
    """Run mBERT once, return both attention and final-layer embeddings.

    Requirements:
      - model loaded with output_attentions=True AND output_hidden_states=True
      - tokenizer must be a fast tokenizer (BertTokenizerFast or similar) so
        that BatchEncoding.word_ids(batch_index=...) is available.
        A slow tokenizer raises ValueError.

    Returns
    -------
    attention : np.ndarray, dtype float16
        Shape (n_layers, batch, n_heads, MAX_LEN, MAX_LEN). Same as
        grab_attention_weights — bit-for-bit identical for the same input.
    embeddings : np.ndarray, dtype float16
        Shape (batch, MAX_LEN, hidden_size). Final-layer hidden state
        (outputs['hidden_states'][-1]).
    batch_word_ids : list[list[Optional[int]]]
        Length batch; each inner list length MAX_LEN. Each entry is the
        whitespace-word index the wordpiece came from, or None for
        [CLS]/[SEP]/[PAD]. Used downstream to align canon-term `target_idx`
        (whitespace space) to wordpiece span.
    """
```

Implementation:
- At entry, assert `getattr(tokenizer, "is_fast", False)`; if False, raise
  `ValueError("grab_attention_and_embeddings requires a fast tokenizer (e.g. BertTokenizerFast); got <class>")`. The message MUST literally
  contain the substring `BertTokenizerFast` so tests can assert it.
- Tokenize via `encoded = tokenizer([text_preprocessing(s) for s in sentences], return_tensors="pt", add_special_tokens=True, max_length=MAX_LEN, padding="max_length", truncation=True)`.
  Same flags as the existing `grab_attention_weights`.
- Forward pass: `outputs = model(input_ids, attention_mask, token_type_ids)`
  with the three tensors moved to `device` (mirror existing function).
- Pull `attention = outputs["attentions"]` (a tuple of layer tensors). Convert
  to fp16 numpy via `np.asarray([layer.cpu().detach().numpy() for layer in attention], dtype=np.float16)` — same code shape as existing function.
- Pull `last_hidden = outputs["hidden_states"][-1]` — shape `(batch, MAX_LEN, hidden_size)`.
  Convert to fp16 numpy via `last_hidden.cpu().detach().numpy().astype(np.float16)`.
- Build `batch_word_ids = [encoded.word_ids(batch_index=i) for i in range(len(sentences))]`.
- Return `(attention_np, embeddings_np, batch_word_ids)`.

2. **Add tests** to `tests/test_grab_weights_compat.py`. Mirror the existing
   shape/dtype/bounds test pattern. **Do NOT mutate the existing `mbert`
   fixture** — other tests depend on its exact load configuration. Instead,
   add a NEW session-scoped fixture (suggested name: `mbert_with_emb`) that
   loads:
   - `BertModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-multilingual-cased", output_attentions=True, output_hidden_states=True)`
   - `BertTokenizerFast.from_pretrained("bert-base-multilingual-cased", do_lower_case=False)`

   Reuse the existing `_check_mbert_cached` / `PH_RUN_GRAB_WEIGHTS` skip
   gate so the test is opt-in like the existing one. Mark the fixture
   `scope="session"` so the model loads once across the new test cases.

   Test cases to add:
   - `test_returns_three_objects` — return value unpacks to `(attn, emb, wids)` with the right types (`np.ndarray`, `np.ndarray`, `list`).
   - `test_attention_shape_dtype_bounds` — same as existing test, applied to the new function's first return: shape `(12, batch, 12, MAX_LEN, MAX_LEN)`, dtype float16, values in `[0, 1]` along the last axis (per-row softmax).
   - `test_embeddings_shape_dtype_finite` — shape `(batch, MAX_LEN, 768)`, dtype float16, all values finite (`np.isfinite(emb).all()`).
   - `test_word_ids_structure` — `len(wids) == batch`; each inner list has length `MAX_LEN`; index 0 (the [CLS] position) is None; at least one wordpiece in each batch item has a non-None word_id (i.e., real content tokens are aligned).
   - `test_slow_tokenizer_rejected` — load a `BertTokenizer` (slow) locally inside the test; call `grab_attention_and_embeddings` with it; assert a `ValueError` is raised AND the error message contains the substring `"BertTokenizerFast"` (per the contract above).

3. Run `pytest tests/test_grab_weights_compat.py -v` and confirm all green.
4. Run `python -m py_compile replication/grab_weights_compat.py` clean.

## Testing notes

- Tests should fall back to CPU if `cuda:0` is unavailable (mirror the
  existing pattern in `tests/test_grab_weights_compat.py` — check
  `torch.cuda.is_available()` and pass `device="cpu"` if not).
- Use `MAX_LEN=16` and 3 short fixed sentences (mirror the existing fixture).
- `bert-base-multilingual-cased` is already cached locally per ph-project-mwk.1.

## Example

```python
>>> from transformers import BertModel, BertTokenizerFast
>>> from grab_weights_compat import grab_attention_and_embeddings
>>> model = BertModel.from_pretrained(
...     "bert-base-multilingual-cased",
...     output_attentions=True,
...     output_hidden_states=True,
... ).to("cuda:0")
>>> tok = BertTokenizerFast.from_pretrained("bert-base-multilingual-cased", do_lower_case=False)
>>> attn, emb, wids = grab_attention_and_embeddings(
...     model, tok, ["небо синее", "the sky is blue"], MAX_LEN=16
... )
>>> attn.shape, attn.dtype
((12, 2, 12, 16, 16), dtype('float16'))
>>> emb.shape, emb.dtype
((2, 16, 768), dtype('float16'))
>>> wids[0][:5]
[None, 0, 0, 1, None]   # [CLS], wp1-of-небо, wp2-of-небо, синее, [SEP]
```

## Acceptance

- `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` exports `grab_attention_and_embeddings`.
- All new tests pass; existing `grab_attention_weights` tests stay green.
- Existing `grab_attention_weights` function is unchanged (verify with diff).
- Existing `mbert` test fixture is unchanged; new fixture is additive.
- `python -m py_compile replication/grab_weights_compat.py` clean.

## Out of scope

- Notebook integration (subtask `16v.2`).
- Saving manifests / target-wordpiece-span computation (subtask `16v.2`).
- Adapting `notebooks/phase3_ripser_templates.ipynb` (mwk.3 territory).

title: Adapt phase3_thresholds.ipynb to also emit final-layer embeddings + manifest
description:
## Summary

Adapt `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` to also emit final-layer mBERT
embeddings and a per-row manifest in the same forward pass that already
produces attention tensors. Wraps `grab_attention_and_embeddings` (added in
`16v.1`). Mark all changed cells with `DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE` per project
convention (see auto-memory `feedback_notebook_faithful_phase3.md`).

Existing attention output stays equivalent for the same KWIC input — the
embedding extraction is purely additive on the same forward pass.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` (~6 cells edited; NO new cells —
  KEEP_EMBEDDINGS folds into the existing cleanup cell to preserve substrate
  cell count per `feedback_notebook_faithful_phase3.md`).

## Files to read for context

- `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` — current state, focus on cells:
  - cell 1 (markdown — disk budget, DIVERGES marker)
  - cell 2 (imports)
  - cell 7 (parameters)
  - cell 10 (paths/filenames — DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 10)
  - cell 14 (`data = pd.read_csv(...).reset_index(drop=True)` — establishes
    the row-order invariant the manifest relies on)
  - cell 17 (`get_token_length` — uses `tokenizer(...)` and `tokenizer.pad_token_id`; verified compatible with `BertTokenizerFast`, no changes needed in this cell)
  - cell 18 (tokenizer load)
  - cell 24 (batching — `np.array_split(data['sentence'].values, ...)`. Note
    that `np.array_split` produces UNEVEN batch sizes when `len(data) %
    number_of_batches != 0` — this matters for the manifest offset math)
  - cell 25 (model + tokenizer load — DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 25)
  - cell 26 (extraction loop — calls `grab_attention_weights`, dumps DUMP_SIZE-batched .npy parts)
  - last cell (KEEP_ATTENTION cleanup — DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 41)
- `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` — new function `grab_attention_and_embeddings` from `16v.1`
- `replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb` — substrate reference for cell-faithful adaptation
- `data/kwic/en/color.csv` — example KWIC schema (columns: term, labels, sentence, target_idx, corpus_source)
- `CLAUDE.md` — "Hard rules" + "Current scope"
- `~/.claude/projects/-home-anna-ph-project/memory/feedback_notebook_faithful_phase3.md` — DIVERGES marker convention; preserve substrate cell count 1:1

## Implementation steps

1. **Cell 2 (imports):** add `BertTokenizerFast` to the transformers import,
   replace the compat-shim import with the new function, mark with DIVERGES:
   ```python
   # DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 2 — add BertTokenizerFast (needed for word_ids alignment); switch to grab_attention_and_embeddings (16v.1) for joint attention+embedding extraction.
   from transformers import BertTokenizer, BertTokenizerFast, BertModel, BertForSequenceClassification
   ...
   from grab_weights_compat import grab_attention_and_embeddings, text_preprocessing
   ```

2. **Cell 10 (paths):** in the existing DIVERGES block, add embedding paths:
   ```python
   emb_dir = "../data/phase3/embeddings/"
   os.makedirs(emb_dir, exist_ok=True)

   e_file = emb_dir + lang + "_" + domain + "_final_layer_MAX_LEN_" + \
            str(max_tokens_amount) + "_" + model_path.split("/")[-1]
   manifest_file = emb_dir + lang + "_" + domain + "_manifest.parquet"
   ```
   Update the DIVERGES marker comment to mention `emb_dir` / `e_file` /
   `manifest_file`.

3. **Cell 18 (tokenizer load):** switch `BertTokenizer` →
   `BertTokenizerFast`. Update the existing DIVERGES marker:
   `BertTokenizer → BertTokenizerFast (needed for word_ids alignment, 16v)`.
   Cell 17's `get_token_length` was verified compatible with the fast
   tokenizer — no changes needed there.

4. **Cell 25 (model + tokenizer load):** in the existing DIVERGES block:
   - Add `output_hidden_states=True` to `BertModel.from_pretrained(...)`.
   - Switch `BertTokenizer` → `BertTokenizerFast` (consistent with cell 18).
   - Update the DIVERGES marker comment to add: "+ output_hidden_states=True
     for joint attention+embedding extraction (16v); + slow → fast tokenizer
     for word_ids alignment."

5. **Cell 26 (extraction loop):** swap `grab_attention_weights` call →
   `grab_attention_and_embeddings`. Build a parallel embedding accumulator
   and dump pattern that mirrors the attention DUMP_SIZE / part-numbering
   logic. Build manifest rows in the same loop.

   **CRITICAL — manifest offset math.** `np.array_split` produces uneven
   batches when `len(data) % number_of_batches != 0`. Use a per-part
   row-counter that resets at every dump (including the trailing leftover
   dump). DO NOT use `j` (the within-batch index) — that resets every batch,
   not every part. The valid indexing chain is:

   - `data` was `reset_index(drop=True)` in cell 14 → `data.iloc[kwic_row_id]` is well-defined.
   - `np.array_split` preserves order → cumulative batch-length sum gives the global `kwic_row_id`.
   - **Within-part offset** is the global `kwic_row_id` minus the kwic-id at which the current part started.

   Skeleton (pay attention to `part_start_kwic_id` / `embedding_offset`):
   ```python
   # DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 26 — joint attention + embedding extraction (16v); manifest rows recorded inline; per-part offset counter for manifest.
   adj_matricies = []
   adj_filenames = []
   emb_matrices  = []
   emb_filenames = []
   manifest_rows = []

   batch_idx_offset    = 0  # cumulative kwic_row_id at start of next batch
   part_start_kwic_id  = 0  # kwic_row_id at which the CURRENT part begins
   current_part_number = 1

   for i in tqdm(range(number_of_batches), desc="Weights+emb calc"):
       batch_sents = batched_sentences[i]
       attn, emb, wids = grab_attention_and_embeddings(
           model, tokenizer, batch_sents, max_tokens_amount, device
       )
       adj_matricies.append(attn)
       emb_matrices.append(emb)

       # Manifest rows for this batch
       for j in range(len(batch_sents)):
           kwic_row_id = batch_idx_offset + j
           target_idx_ws = int(data['target_idx'].iloc[kwic_row_id])
           wp_positions = [
               k for k, wid in enumerate(wids[j])
               if wid == target_idx_ws
           ]
           target_wp_start = wp_positions[0] if wp_positions else -1
           target_wp_end   = wp_positions[-1] if wp_positions else -1
           manifest_rows.append({
               "kwic_row_id":      kwic_row_id,
               "lang":             lang,
               "domain":           domain,
               "term":             data['term'].iloc[kwic_row_id],
               "target_idx":       target_idx_ws,
               "target_wp_start":  target_wp_start,
               "target_wp_end":    target_wp_end,
               "embedding_part":   current_part_number,
               "embedding_offset": kwic_row_id - part_start_kwic_id,
           })
       batch_idx_offset += len(batch_sents)

       if (i+1) % DUMP_SIZE == 0:
           # --- attention dump (existing logic, unchanged for filename) ---
           adj_arr = np.concatenate(adj_matricies, axis=1)
           adj_arr = np.swapaxes(adj_arr, axis1=0, axis2=1)
           a_filename = r_file + "_part" + str(ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)) + "of" + str(number_of_files) + '.npy'
           np.save(a_filename, adj_arr)
           adj_filenames.append(a_filename)
           adj_matricies = []
           # --- embedding dump (NEW) — MUST use the same filename formula ---
           emb_arr = np.concatenate(emb_matrices, axis=0)
           e_filename = e_file + "_part" + str(ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)) + "of" + str(number_of_files) + '.npy'
           np.save(e_filename, emb_arr)
           emb_filenames.append(e_filename)
           emb_matrices = []
           # advance part bookkeeping
           current_part_number += 1
           part_start_kwic_id = batch_idx_offset

   # Final partial dump (mirror existing leftover branch — same filename formula)
   if adj_matricies:
       adj_arr = np.concatenate(adj_matricies, axis=1)
       adj_arr = np.swapaxes(adj_arr, axis1=0, axis2=1)
       a_filename = r_file + "_part" + str(ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)) + "of" + str(number_of_files) + '.npy'
       np.save(a_filename, adj_arr)
       adj_filenames.append(a_filename)
       emb_arr = np.concatenate(emb_matrices, axis=0)
       e_filename = e_file + "_part" + str(ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)) + "of" + str(number_of_files) + '.npy'
       np.save(e_filename, emb_arr)
       emb_filenames.append(e_filename)

   # Save manifest (after loop, once)
   import pandas as _pd
   _pd.DataFrame(manifest_rows).to_parquet(manifest_file, index=False)
   print(f"Manifest saved: {manifest_file} ({len(manifest_rows)} rows)")
   ```

   **Substrate filename-collision caveat (DO NOT FIX HERE).** The substrate's
   filename formula `_part" + str(ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)) + "of"` collides between
   the periodic-dump branch and the leftover branch in the corner case where
   `number_of_batches == DUMP_SIZE * k + 1` (e.g., 101 batches → leftover
   overwrites part1). For the in-scope inputs this does not bite (en/color
   = 220 batches → parts 1, 2, 3, no collision). This task uses the same
   formula for the embedding filename so attention and embedding part numbers
   stay aligned — fixing the substrate bug is OUT OF SCOPE here. File a
   separate bug if the corner case matters for any future input.

6. **Existing cleanup cell (DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 41):** EXPAND the
   existing cell to also handle `KEEP_EMBEDDINGS`. Do NOT add a new trailing
   cell (preserves substrate cell count per `feedback_notebook_faithful_phase3.md`).

   ```python
   # DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell 41 — log message uses (lang, domain) instead of subset; cleanup logic extended to also gate embedding artifacts (16v).
   # Embeddings are the whole point of the sidecar emit, so default is KEEP_EMBEDDINGS=True (unlike attentions).
   KEEP_ATTENTION  = False
   KEEP_EMBEDDINGS = True

   import os as _os
   if KEEP_ATTENTION:
       print(f"Cleanup: KEEP_ATTENTION=True, retaining {len(adj_filenames)} attention file(s) for ({lang!r}, {domain!r}).")
   else:
       _removed = 0
       for _f in adj_filenames:
           if _os.path.exists(_f):
               _os.remove(_f)
               _removed += 1
       print(f"Cleanup: removed {_removed} attention file(s) for ({lang!r}, {domain!r}).")

   if KEEP_EMBEDDINGS:
       print(f"Cleanup: KEEP_EMBEDDINGS=True, retaining {len(emb_filenames)} embedding file(s) + manifest for ({lang!r}, {domain!r}).")
   else:
       _removed = 0
       for _f in emb_filenames + [manifest_file]:
           if _os.path.exists(_f):
               _os.remove(_f)
               _removed += 1
       print(f"Cleanup: removed {_removed} embedding artifact(s) for ({lang!r}, {domain!r}).")
   ```

7. **Update the disk-budget markdown cell (cell 1).** The current text says
   `~650 MB per (lang, domain)` for attention. Replacement language for the
   cell should add embedding cost explicitly:

   > Per-run estimate with `max_tokens=32` (KWIC contexts ~21 whitespace tokens):
   > - **Attention** is `batch × 12 layers × 12 heads × 32 × 32 × 2 bytes` ≈ 295 KB per sample. en/color has ~2200 samples → **~650 MB per (lang, color)** (deleted at end by default — `KEEP_ATTENTION=False`).
   > - **Embeddings (16v)** are `batch × 32 × 768 × 2 bytes` ≈ 49 KB per sample. en/color → **~110 MB per (lang, color)** (retained by default — `KEEP_EMBEDDINGS=True`, since downstream Draganov-style sidecar will consume them).
   > - **Three runs (en/ru/es)** without attention cleanup ≈ 2 GB; embeddings retained ≈ 330 MB. Plan accordingly.

   Update the existing DIVERGES marker comment for cell 1 to mention the
   embedding budget addition.

8. **Run the notebook on `(lang="en", domain="color")`** end-to-end. Verify:
   - `data/phase3/attentions/en_color_*` parts exist with shape
     `(batch_per_part, 12 layers, 12 heads, 32, 32)` per part.
   - `data/phase3/embeddings/en_color_final_layer_*_partXofN.npy` exist with
     shape `(batch_per_part, 32, 768)` per part, dtype float16.
   - `data/phase3/embeddings/en_color_manifest.parquet` exists; row count
     equals `len(data)` (~2200 for en/color); `target_wp_start` is populated
     for the vast majority of rows (a few -1s OK due to truncation or
     punctuation stickiness — see `bd-5ln`).
   - Spot-check: pick a kwic_row_id, load `data.iloc[kwic_row_id]`, load the
     corresponding part's embedding tensor at `embedding_offset`, confirm
     `target_wp_start` falls inside `[1, tokenizer_length-1]`.

9. **Quality gates:**
   - `jupyter nbconvert --to script notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb --stdout | python -c "import sys; compile(sys.stdin.read(), '<nb>', 'exec')"` clean.
   - `pytest tests/` should remain green (no test changes here).

## Cell-faithfulness notes

Per `feedback_notebook_faithful_phase3.md`: preserve substrate cell count 1:1
where possible. This task adds **zero new cells** — KEEP_EMBEDDINGS is folded
into the existing cell-41 cleanup. All other changes are edits inside existing
cells, marked `DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell <N> — <why>`.

## Acceptance

- Notebook runs end-to-end on `(lang="en", domain="color")` without errors.
- Attention output shape and per-tensor summary stats (`mean`, `std`, `min`,
  `max`) match a pre-change run within fp16 numerical tolerance for the same
  KWIC input. (Byte-identity is not required — CUDA + fp16 are not
  bit-deterministic across runs.)
- Embedding `.npy` parts and the parquet manifest are produced as specified.
- DIVERGES markers updated for cells 1, 2, 10, 18, 25, 26, 41 — and NO new
  cells.
- The diff against the substrate
  (`replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb`) is
  auditable: every divergence has a marker.

## Out of scope

- Running the notebook on `ru` or `es` (the user runs the other two pairs
  manually per the existing per-pair-rerun pattern; verifying `en/color` is
  enough for this task).
- Tapping `notebooks/phase3_ripser_templates.ipynb` (will stay attention-only;
  that's `mwk.3` territory).
- Fixing the substrate filename-collision corner case (separate follow-up).
- Any analysis of the emitted embeddings (separate epic, to be planned once
  the data is on disk).

title: Per-color-region k-NN clouds for sub-baselines B (CC-300) and C (MUSE)
description:
## Summary

The static-embedding **baseline** for the Draganov-style cloud-topology sidecar to Kushnareva. (Contextual mBERT clouds — the *main experiment* within the sidecar — are scoped under a separate epic, not this one.)

Sub-baselines B and C currently run persistent homology on a cloud of just the canon terms themselves — ~11 points per (language, color domain). PH on n=11 produces near-trivial barcodes; H₀ shows a few short bars before everything merges into one component, H₁ rarely forms at all. The Whorfian signal we're after — that синий/голубой are topologically split where blue is not — cannot emerge from a cloud that small.

This epic adds **new sibling notebooks under `draganov/ours/`**, leaving the existing `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` and `notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb` frozen (they are already shipped via `ssa.5` and referenced from `baseline_comparison.ipynb`). The new notebooks adopt a Draganov-style approach: per-(language, color-region) clouds expanded via k-NN.

Specifically:
1. Build per-(language, color-region) clouds via k-NN expansion of canon terms in their respective fastText spaces.
2. For the **Russian blue region**, pool k-NN(синий) ∪ k-NN(голубой) — the experimental structure encoded in `canon-terms/ru/color.yaml`.
3. For all other (language, region) pairs, pool k-NN of the single canonical term.
4. Run the existing `rips_barcode` + `barcode_features` pipeline on each (language, region) cloud.
5. Compare across languages within each region; the blue region is the predicted differentiator, the others act as controls.

Color domain only (per the May 4 scope decision; see CLAUDE.md "Current scope").

## Layout

All Draganov-style work lives under `draganov/ours/` to keep it isolated from the cloned upstream pipeline at `draganov/{A,B,C}-*` (frozen reference). Two file-categories are exceptions:

- **`canon-terms/color_regions.yaml`** stays in `canon-terms/` because it is a cross-language anchor used beyond this sidecar (the contextual mBERT epic will also consume it).
- **`baselines/vectors.py::neighborhood_cloud`** stays in `baselines/` because it is a general fastText vectors utility, not Draganov-specific.

The Draganov-specific artifacts go under `draganov/ours/`:
- `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb`
- `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_C_aligned_neighborhoods.ipynb`
- `draganov/ours/results/baseline_B_neighborhoods_features.csv`
- `draganov/ours/results/baseline_C_neighborhoods_features.csv`

## Region structure (from canon-terms YAMLs)

11 color regions, one per Berlin & Kay anchor:

| Region | en | ru | es |
|---|---|---|---|
| black | black | чёрный | negro |
| white | white | белый | blanco |
| red | red | красный | rojo |
| green | green | зелёный | verde |
| yellow | yellow | жёлтый | amarillo |
| **blue** | blue | **синий + голубой** | azul |
| brown | brown | коричневый | marrón |
| purple | purple | фиолетовый | morado |
| pink | pink | розовый | rosa |
| orange | orange | оранжевый | naranja |
| gray | gray | серый | gris |

## Subtasks

- **`ph-project-1iq.1`** — Foundation: `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml` + `neighborhood_cloud` helper in `baselines/vectors.py` + tests. No notebook work. No dependencies.
- **`ph-project-1iq.2`** — Baseline B neighborhoods notebook at `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb` (CC-300, per-language). Depends on `1iq.1`.
- **`ph-project-1iq.3`** — Baseline C neighborhoods notebook at `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_C_aligned_neighborhoods.ipynb` (MUSE, cross-lingual). Depends on `1iq.1`. Independent of `1iq.2`.

`1iq.2` and `1iq.3` can run in parallel after `1iq.1` lands.

## Key parameters

- **k=20** for k-NN expansion. Reasonable default for fastText; semantic relevance of neighbors drops off fast past k≈30.
- **Cosine distance** throughout (matches fastText training and existing baseline distance code).
- **No filtering** of neighbors — let topology surface structure rather than hand-curating.

## Out of scope

- Wasserstein-style pairwise diagram distances (Draganov stage B). Continue using Kushnareva scalar features.
- Emotion and kinship domains (paused per May 4 decision).
- **Contextual mBERT** version of this analysis. This is the *main* sidecar experiment but lives under a separate epic to be planned in a dedicated `/plan` session that examines Phase 3 substrate.
- Modifying the existing `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` / `notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb` notebooks or their CSV outputs.
- Updating `baseline_comparison.ipynb` to consume the new outputs.

## Acceptance

- Two new notebooks under `draganov/ours/notebooks/` produce per (lang, region) PH features written to distinct CSVs in `draganov/ours/results/`.
- Existing baseline B/C notebooks and outputs are untouched.
- The blue-region comparison surfaces a measurable difference between Russian and English/Spanish that is not present in control regions (or its absence is explicitly reported).
- Tests pass.

title: Foundation: color_regions.yaml + neighborhood_cloud helper
description:
## Summary

Add the cross-language color-region map and the k-NN cloud helper that the two new baseline-neighborhoods notebooks (1iq.2, 1iq.3) will consume. No notebook work in this task — just data file, helper, and tests.

## Files to create

- `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml`
- `baselines/vectors.py` (extend — add `neighborhood_cloud` function)
- `tests/test_vectors_neighborhood.py` OR add to existing `tests/test_vectors.py`

## Files to read for context

- `canon-terms/SCHEMA.md` (canon-terms format conventions)
- `canon-terms/en/color.yaml`
- `canon-terms/ru/color.yaml`
- `canon-terms/es/color.yaml`
- `baselines/__init__.py` (SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES constant)
- `baselines/vectors.py` (existing `load_fasttext`)
- `baselines/distances.py` (existing `extract_term_vectors` + `_warn_oov` pattern)
- `tests/` (existing test conventions)

## Implementation steps

1. **Create `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml`** with all 11 Berlin & Kay color regions mapped to per-language canonical surface forms. The Russian blue region is the only multi-term cell:
   ```yaml
   domain: color
   description: >
     Berlin & Kay color region anchors aligned across en/ru/es. Each region groups
     the canonical surface form(s) used in each language. Russian's blue region
     contains both синий and голубой (the obligatory Russian blue split, per
     Paramei 2005 and Winawer et al. 2007); all other (lang, region) cells contain
     a single term.
   sources:
     - citation: "Berlin, Brent and Paul Kay. 1969. Basic Color Terms..."
       role: anchor
     - citation: "Paramei, Galina V. 2005. Singing the Russian Blues..."
       role: augmentation
       notes: "Licenses the two-term blue region for Russian."
   regions:
     black: { en: ["black"], ru: ["чёрный"], es: ["negro"] }
     white: { en: ["white"], ru: ["белый"], es: ["blanco"] }
     red:   { en: ["red"],   ru: ["красный"], es: ["rojo"] }
     green: { en: ["green"], ru: ["зелёный"], es: ["verde"] }
     yellow: { en: ["yellow"], ru: ["жёлтый"], es: ["amarillo"] }
     blue:  { en: ["blue"],  ru: ["синий", "голубой"], es: ["azul"] }
     brown: { en: ["brown"], ru: ["коричневый"], es: ["marrón"] }
     purple: { en: ["purple"], ru: ["фиолетовый"], es: ["morado"] }
     pink:  { en: ["pink"],  ru: ["розовый"], es: ["rosa"] }
     orange: { en: ["orange"], ru: ["оранжевый"], es: ["naranja"] }
     gray:  { en: ["gray"],  ru: ["серый"], es: ["gris"] }
   ```
   Keep the surface forms exactly aligned with the per-language `color.yaml` files (note the chyo/yo accents on Russian).

2. **Add `neighborhood_cloud` to `baselines/vectors.py`**:
   ```python
   def neighborhood_cloud(
       canon_terms: list[str],
       kv,
       k: int = 20,
       lang: str | None = None,
       domain: str | None = None,
   ) -> tuple[np.ndarray, list[str]]:
       """Build a pooled, deduplicated k-NN cloud from canon terms.

       For each canon term, retrieves the term itself plus its top-k most-similar
       neighbors via `kv.most_similar(term, topn=k)`. Pools all canon terms and
       their neighbors into a single point cloud, deduplicated by surface form.

       Returns
       -------
       matrix : np.ndarray, shape (n_unique, dim)
           Row matrix; one row per unique surface form.
       labels : list[str]
           Parallel list of length n_unique giving the surface form for each row.

       OOV behaviour
       -------------
       For MUSE-aligned `.vec` (no subword fallback): missing canon terms are
       skipped with a warning matching `_warn_oov` style. CC-300 `.bin` (subword)
       never has OOV.
       """
   ```
   Implementation: iterate canon_terms, append the term itself if found, then `kv.most_similar(term, topn=k)` and append each neighbor. Maintain a seen-set keyed on surface form to dedup. Use the existing logger pattern for OOV warnings (mirror `_warn_oov` from `distances.py`).

3. **Tests** in `tests/test_vectors_neighborhood.py` (or extend `tests/test_vectors.py`):
   - `test_returns_expected_shape`: with `k=3` and 1 canon term, matrix has ≤4 rows
   - `test_dedup_collapses_shared_neighbors`: if two canon terms share a top neighbor, only one row appears for that neighbor
   - `test_labels_parallel_to_matrix`: `len(labels) == matrix.shape[0]`, canon terms appear first in `labels`
   - `test_oov_canon_term_skipped`: with a fake KV-like object that raises KeyError, the OOV term is logged and skipped (not crashed); other canon terms still contribute
   - `test_empty_canon_returns_empty`: `neighborhood_cloud([], kv)` returns `(matrix.shape == (0, dim), [])`
   - Use a small fake `KeyedVectors`-like object (test helper or `unittest.mock`) — don't load the 4GB binaries in unit tests.

## Acceptance

- `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml` parses as valid YAML and lists 11 regions × 3 languages.
- `neighborhood_cloud` is importable from `baselines.vectors` and passes all unit tests.
- `pytest tests/` green.
- `python -m py_compile baselines/vectors.py` clean.

## Example

```python
>>> from baselines.vectors import load_fasttext, neighborhood_cloud
>>> kv = load_fasttext("ru", "aligned")
>>> matrix, labels = neighborhood_cloud(["синий", "голубой"], kv, k=5)
>>> matrix.shape
(N, 300)  # N ≤ 12 (2 canon + up to 10 neighbors, fewer after dedup)
>>> labels[:2]
['синий', 'голубой']
>>> labels[2:]
['тёмно-синий', 'светло-голубой', ...]  # actual neighbors will vary
```

title: Baseline B neighborhoods notebook (CC-300, per-language) at draganov/ours/
description:
## Summary

Copy `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` to `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb` and modify so that the Vietoris-Rips persistent-homology pipeline runs on per-(lang, color-region) k-NN clouds (built via `neighborhood_cloud` from 1iq.1) instead of just the canon terms themselves. Sibling notebook to 1iq.3 (MUSE version); the two run in parallel after 1iq.1 lands.

## Dependencies

- `ph-project-1iq.1` (provides `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml` and `baselines.vectors.neighborhood_cloud`)

## Files to create

- `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb` (new)
- `draganov/ours/results/baseline_B_neighborhoods_features.csv` (notebook artifact)

Implementer must `mkdir -p draganov/ours/notebooks draganov/ours/results` before writing.

## Files to read for context

- `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` (the source notebook to copy)
- `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml` (built in 1iq.1)
- `baselines/vectors.py` (uses `load_fasttext`, `neighborhood_cloud`)
- `baselines/distances.py` (uses `cosine_distance_matrix`)
- `baselines/topology.py` (uses `rips_barcode`, `barcode_features`)
- `CLAUDE.md` (color-only scope decision)

## Implementation steps

1. **Set up the directory and copy**:
   ```bash
   mkdir -p draganov/ours/notebooks draganov/ours/results
   cp notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb
   ```

2. **Update the title cell** (cell 0) to: `# Baseline B (neighborhoods) — Per-region k-NN clouds + PH (fastText CC-300)`. Add a one-paragraph description noting this is the static-embedding **baseline** for the Draganov sidecar to Kushnareva, that contextual mBERT clouds are the *main* experiment in a separate notebook, and that the original `baseline_B_topological.ipynb` is left unmodified.

3. **Adjust sys.path** if the notebook lives one level deeper. The existing notebook does `parent` from `notebooks/`; this one is in `draganov/ours/notebooks/`, so it needs three `parent` hops up to the repo root.

4. **Step 1 (load canon + vectors)** — keep the fastText CC-300 binary loading via `load_fasttext(lang, "cc")` exactly as in the source notebook, restricted to the color domain.

5. **New Step 1.5 — load region map and build per-region clouds**:
   ```python
   import yaml
   with open("canon-terms/color_regions.yaml") as f:
       region_map = yaml.safe_load(f)["regions"]

   from baselines.vectors import neighborhood_cloud

   clouds = {}  # (lang, region) -> (matrix, labels)
   for lang in ["en", "ru", "es"]:
       for region, terms_by_lang in region_map.items():
           region_terms = terms_by_lang[lang]
           matrix, labels = neighborhood_cloud(
               region_terms, vectors_by_lang[lang], k=20, lang=lang, domain="color"
           )
           clouds[(lang, region)] = (matrix, labels)
   ```

6. **Step 2 (distance matrices)** — replace per-(lang, domain) iteration with per-(lang, region):
   ```python
   matrices = {}
   for (lang, region), (matrix, labels) in clouds.items():
       matrices[(lang, region)] = cosine_distance_matrix(matrix)
   ```

7. **Steps 3–5** — same logic, just iterate over `(lang, region)` keys instead of `(lang, domain)`. Barcode dict keys, feature dict keys, and plot loops all reshape accordingly.

8. **Step 6 (assemble feature table)** — write CSV at `draganov/ours/results/baseline_B_neighborhoods_features.csv` with schema:
   ```
   lang, region, n_points, h0_s, h0_m, h0_v, h0_e, h0_n_d_m_t0.25, ..., h1_t_d
   ```
   Add `n_points` as a sanity-check column (number of unique points in the cloud after dedup).

9. **New Step 6.5 — blue-region canon-only-vs-cloud comparison** (per the agreed scope):
   ```python
   from baselines.distances import extract_term_vectors

   print("Blue region: canon-only vs k-NN cloud features")
   print("=" * 60)
   for lang in ["en", "ru", "es"]:
       blue_terms = region_map["blue"][lang]
       canon_matrix, _ = extract_term_vectors(blue_terms, vectors_by_lang[lang], lang=lang)
       canon_features = barcode_features(rips_barcode(cosine_distance_matrix(canon_matrix)))
       cloud_features = features_by_lang_region[(lang, "blue")]
       print(f"\n{lang}:")
       print(f"  canon-only n={canon_matrix.shape[0]}, h0_s={canon_features['h0_s']:.3f}, h0_e={canon_features['h0_e']:.3f}")
       print(f"  cloud      n={clouds[(lang, 'blue')][0].shape[0]}, h0_s={cloud_features['h0_s']:.3f}, h0_e={cloud_features['h0_e']:.3f}")
   ```
   This cell prints a small comparison; no plots needed.

10. **Step 7 (artifact check)** — point to the new CSV path.

11. **Step 8+ (Discussion)** — reframe wording from "color domain H0 across languages" to "blue-region H0 across languages, with other 10 regions as controls; expectation is that ru-blue's cloud is topologically more split than en/es-blue."

## Testing notes

- Notebook executes end-to-end without error:
  ```bash
  jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb --output /tmp/test_out.ipynb
  ```
- Output CSV exists and has 33 rows (3 langs × 11 regions) with the documented schema.
- Comparison cell prints non-trivially-different values between canon-only and cloud features (cloud H0 should typically have larger sums and more bars than canon-only's n=11 cloud).
- Spot-check that `n_points` for ru-blue is close to 2× the value for ru-red (since ru-blue has 2 canon terms feeding the cloud).

## Acceptance

- Notebook in `draganov/ours/notebooks/` executes cleanly.
- CSV output at `draganov/ours/results/baseline_B_neighborhoods_features.csv` with all 33 (lang, region) rows.
- Blue-region comparison cell runs and prints a 3-language table.
- Original `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` and its CSV outputs are untouched.

## Example transformation

**Before** (current Step 2 inner from source notebook):
```python
matrices[(lang, "color")] = cosine_distance_matrix(term_vectors[lang])
```

**After**:
```python
for region, terms_by_lang in region_map.items():
    region_terms = terms_by_lang[lang]
    matrix, labels = neighborhood_cloud(region_terms, vectors_by_lang[lang], k=20)
    clouds[(lang, region)] = (matrix, labels)
    matrices[(lang, region)] = cosine_distance_matrix(matrix)
```

title: Baseline C neighborhoods notebook (MUSE-aligned, cross-lingual) at draganov/ours/
description:
## Summary

Copy `notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb` to `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_C_aligned_neighborhoods.ipynb` and modify so that the Vietoris-Rips persistent-homology pipeline runs on per-(lang, color-region) k-NN clouds (built via `neighborhood_cloud` from 1iq.1) instead of just the canon terms themselves. Cross-lingual comparison in the shared MUSE-aligned space is the headline of this notebook (versus the per-language CC-300 comparison in 1iq.2). Sibling to 1iq.2; the two run in parallel after 1iq.1 lands.

## Dependencies

- `ph-project-1iq.1` (provides `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml` and `baselines.vectors.neighborhood_cloud`)

## Files to create

- `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_C_aligned_neighborhoods.ipynb` (new)
- `draganov/ours/results/baseline_C_neighborhoods_features.csv` (notebook artifact)

Implementer must `mkdir -p draganov/ours/notebooks draganov/ours/results` before writing (no-op if 1iq.2 ran first).

## Files to read for context

- `notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb` (the source notebook to copy)
- `canon-terms/color_regions.yaml` (built in 1iq.1)
- `baselines/vectors.py` (uses `load_fasttext(lang, "aligned")` for MUSE)
- `baselines/distances.py` (uses `cosine_distance_matrix`, OOV warning style)
- `baselines/topology.py` (uses `rips_barcode`, `barcode_features`)
- `data/fasttext/README.md` (notes that MUSE has NO subword fallback — OOV neighbors will be skipped)
- `draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb` (sibling, follow same structure)
- `CLAUDE.md` (color-only scope decision)

## Implementation steps

Same template as 1iq.2 with three substantive differences:

1. **Set up the directory and copy**:
   ```bash
   mkdir -p draganov/ours/notebooks draganov/ours/results
   cp notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_C_aligned_neighborhoods.ipynb
   ```

2. **Update the title cell** to: `# Baseline C (neighborhoods) — Per-region k-NN clouds + PH (MUSE-aligned)`. Note this is the cross-lingual aligned variant of the static-embedding Draganov-sidecar baseline; the per-language CC-300 variant lives at `baseline_B_topological_neighborhoods.ipynb`.

3. **Adjust sys.path** for the new three-level depth (`draganov/ours/notebooks/` → repo root via three `parent` hops).

4. **Step 1 (vectors)** — load MUSE-aligned vectors via `load_fasttext(lang, "aligned")` rather than `"cc"`. Keep this exactly as the source notebook does, restricted to color domain.

5. **New Step 1.5 — load region map and build per-region clouds**: identical to 1iq.2 step 5, except `neighborhood_cloud` will warn-and-skip MUSE OOV canon terms or OOV neighbors (no subword fallback). Capture and report any OOVs in a comment cell so the cross-lingual comparison is interpretable.

6. **Steps 2–6** — identical to 1iq.2 but writing CSV at `draganov/ours/results/baseline_C_neighborhoods_features.csv`.

7. **New Step 6.5 — blue-region canon-only-vs-cloud comparison**: same pattern as 1iq.2 but using MUSE-aligned vectors. Note that in MUSE space, **inter-language distances are directly meaningful** (shared coordinate system) — so add an extra two-line print comparing `d(en-blue-cloud-centroid, ru-blue-cloud-centroid)` etc. Lightweight, just centroid distances; the full cross-language Wasserstein is out of scope per the parent epic.

8. **Steps 7–8** — point to the new CSV path; reframe Discussion to emphasize that the shared MUSE space makes cross-lingual blue-region comparison direct.

## Testing notes

- Notebook executes end-to-end without error:
  ```bash
  jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute draganov/ours/notebooks/baseline_C_aligned_neighborhoods.ipynb --output /tmp/test_out.ipynb
  ```
- Output CSV exists with 33 rows (3 langs × 11 regions) and the documented schema.
- MUSE OOVs are reported (likely some Russian morphological variants among neighbors); notebook does not crash on OOVs.
- Comparison cell prints non-trivially-different values between canon-only and cloud features for blue across all three languages.

## Acceptance

- Notebook in `draganov/ours/notebooks/` executes cleanly.
- CSV output at `draganov/ours/results/baseline_C_neighborhoods_features.csv` with all 33 (lang, region) rows.
- Blue-region comparison cell runs and prints a 3-language table including centroid-distance lines.
- Original `notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb` and its CSV outputs are untouched.

## Why MUSE matters separately from CC-300

CC-300 vectors (used in 1iq.2) are language-specific — there's no shared coordinate system, so cross-language comparison is only via topology of each cloud independently. MUSE-aligned vectors share a coordinate system across en/ru/es, so the *positions* of clouds are commensurable. This makes 1iq.3 the more direct cross-lingual test, and 1iq.2 the per-language sanity check.

title: Add tests/test_substrate_sync.py to detect adapted-vs-substrate drift
description: When the replication substrate is re-patched, the hand-edited adapted notebooks (notebooks/phase3_*.ipynb) silently drift out of sync. The DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE markers identify which cells are intentionally divergent — by parsing them, a test can byte-compare every NON-diverged cell between adapted and substrate and fail loudly when drift occurs. Becomes more important as mwk.3 and mwk.4 land. Surfaced by the architecture review on PR for ph-project-mwk.2. Implementation sketch: scan each notebooks/phase3_*.ipynb for cells containing 'DIVERGES FROM SUBSTRATE cell N' to build a divergence-allowlist, then assert byte-equality of cell.source for every other cell vs. the corresponding substrate cell.

title: Install user-scoped capped kernel spec for VS Code / any Jupyter client
description:
Summary
-------
Add a Jupyter kernel spec that any client (VS Code primarily, also
JupyterLab/classic Notebook/nbconvert) can pick from its kernel
dropdown. Selecting it spawns Python inside a systemd-run --user
--scope cgroup with the same 48 GiB / 4 GiB caps as wz1.1's
launch_jupyter.sh.

The cap travels with the kernel choice, not the launch method, so
no special launch command or wrapped server is required. Anna runs
notebooks in VS Code; this gives her the wz1.1 safety net by simply
picking "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)" from the kernel dropdown.

Out of scope: deprecating launch_jupyter.sh (still useful for the
"JupyterLab in a browser" case). The two coexist.

Pre-flight (IMPORTANT)
----------------------
Branch this work from origin/main, NOT from feature/replication-4zo.
The wz1.1 launcher (launch_jupyter.sh) and its test pattern
(tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py) — which this task models — only
exist on main. Working from the older branch will cause the
"read for context" steps to fail.

Files to create
---------------
- replication/scripts/launch_kernel.sh
    The argv target of the kernel spec. Resolves the venv's python
    relative to ${BASH_SOURCE[0]} so it's portable across repo
    locations (no hardcoded /home/anna paths). Mirrors
    launch_jupyter.sh structure: cgroup-v2 guard, env-var overrides
    (PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX, PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX), exec systemd-run.

- replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py
    Writes ~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels/ph-project-capped/kernel.json
    with argv pointing at launch_kernel.sh (absolute path, resolved at
    install time) and display_name="Python 3 (ph-project, capped)".
    Idempotent: re-running overwrites with the same content.

- tests/test_launch_kernel_sh.py
    Hermetic tests analogous to tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py:
    bash -n syntax check, cgroup-v2 guard fires on shimmed v1, guard
    passes on real v2, default and custom MemoryMax/MemorySwapMax via
    PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX/PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX, "$@" forwarding to
    ipykernel_launcher, venv python is correctly resolved.

    Note on test mechanics (per plan review): the PATH-prepend shim
    trick used in test_launch_jupyter_sh.py only intercepts commands
    looked up via PATH (stat, systemd-run). It will NOT intercept the
    launcher's $VENV_PYTHON because that is an ABSOLUTE path computed
    at runtime. To test venv resolution / the "venv python missing"
    failure case, copy launch_kernel.sh into a tmp directory tree
    that has (or lacks) a fake .venv/bin/python alongside it, then
    run the copy. systemd-run is still PATH-shimmed so no real scope
    is created.

- tests/test_install_kernel_spec.py
    Override Path.home / pass tmp paths to install() so HOME doesn't
    get touched. Verify install creates a valid kernel.json with
    correct argv pointing at the launcher and the expected
    display_name; verify idempotency (second install overwrites with
    identical content).

Files to modify
---------------
- replication/README.md
    Two edits:
    a) Add a "Capped kernel for VS Code / Jupyter" subsection in the
       "Running notebooks" area. Steps:
         1. python replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py
         2. In VS Code: Select Kernel -> "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)"
         3. (Same dropdown in JupyterLab.)
       Mention the env-var overrides and the cgroup-v2 requirement.
    b) Update the Layout tree (currently lists download_webtext.sh,
       prepare_csv.py, patch_notebook.py, predict_threshold_only.py,
       launch_jupyter.sh) to add launch_kernel.sh and
       install_kernel_spec.py.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh
    The wz1.1 wrapper. launch_kernel.sh follows the same structure
    (guard, env vars, exec) but with a different exec target.

- tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py
    The shim pattern (stat shim, systemd-run shim) to copy for the
    new launcher tests. NOTE the limitation called out above — the
    PATH-shim trick doesn't cover the absolute venv python path.

- /home/anna/ph-project/.venv/share/jupyter/kernels/python3/kernel.json
    The default Python 3 kernel spec, for reference on the schema
    (argv, display_name, language, metadata.debugger).

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Write replication/scripts/launch_kernel.sh:

    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail

    SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
    PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)"
    VENV_PYTHON="$PROJECT_ROOT/.venv/bin/python"

    # cgroup v2 guard — same rationale as launch_jupyter.sh
    if [ "$(stat -fc %T /sys/fs/cgroup 2>/dev/null)" != "cgroup2fs" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: /sys/fs/cgroup is not cgroup2fs; this kernel launcher requires cgroup v2." >&2
      exit 1
    fi

    if [ ! -x "$VENV_PYTHON" ]; then
      echo "ERROR: venv python not found at $VENV_PYTHON" >&2
      exit 1
    fi

    MEM_MAX="${PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX:-48G}"
    SWAP_MAX="${PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX:-4G}"

    exec systemd-run --user --scope --quiet \
      -p "MemoryMax=${MEM_MAX}" \
      -p "MemorySwapMax=${SWAP_MAX}" \
      "$VENV_PYTHON" -m ipykernel_launcher "$@"

   chmod +x the file.

2. Write replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py:

    """Install ph-project-capped Jupyter kernel spec to user dir."""
    import json
    import os
    import sys
    from pathlib import Path

    REPO_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]
    LAUNCHER = REPO_ROOT / "replication" / "scripts" / "launch_kernel.sh"
    KERNEL_DIR = Path.home() / ".local" / "share" / "jupyter" / "kernels" / "ph-project-capped"

    def install(target_dir: Path = KERNEL_DIR, launcher: Path = LAUNCHER) -> Path:
        if not launcher.exists():
            sys.exit(f"Launcher not found: {launcher}")
        if not os.access(launcher, os.X_OK):
            sys.exit(f"Launcher not executable: {launcher}")
        target_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        spec = {
            "argv": [str(launcher), "-f", "{connection_file}"],
            "display_name": "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)",
            "language": "python",
        }
        target = target_dir / "kernel.json"
        target.write_text(json.dumps(spec, indent=2) + "\n")
        return target

    if __name__ == "__main__":
        path = install()
        print(f"Installed kernel spec at {path}")

   Factor install() out so tests can pass override paths without
   monkey-patching globals.

3. Write tests/test_launch_kernel_sh.py modeled on
   test_launch_jupyter_sh.py. Reuse the same shim pattern (temp dir
   prepended to PATH with stat / systemd-run shims). For the
   venv-resolution and venv-missing cases, copy launch_kernel.sh into
   a tmp tree with (or without) a fake .venv/bin/python and run the
   copy. Cover:
     - script exists and is executable
     - bash -n passes
     - cgroup v2 guard fires on shimmed v1, message contains "cgroup2fs"
     - cgroup v2 guard passes on real v2 (this host)
     - default MemoryMax=48G and MemorySwapMax=4G in systemd-run argv
     - PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX / PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX overrides apply
     - extra args are forwarded after ipykernel_launcher
     - venv python missing -> exit 1 with clear error
     - venv python present at expected location -> exec proceeds

4. Write tests/test_install_kernel_spec.py. Use tmp_path for the
   target dir; pass override args to install() so HOME doesn't get
   touched. Cover:
     - kernel.json is valid JSON
     - argv has shape [<launcher>, "-f", "{connection_file}"]
     - display_name matches "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)"
     - language == "python"
     - argv[0] points at an existing executable file
     - second install is idempotent (file content identical)
     - install fails fast if launcher does not exist
     - install fails fast if launcher exists but is not executable

5. Update replication/README.md:
   a) Add the new subsection under "Running notebooks":

      ### Capped kernel for VS Code / Jupyter

      Install once:

          python replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py

      Then in VS Code, click the kernel picker (top right of any
      notebook) and choose **Python 3 (ph-project, capped)**. The
      same option appears in JupyterLab's kernel dropdown.

      Each kernel start spawns inside a systemd-run --user --scope
      cgroup with MemoryMax=48G and MemorySwapMax=4G. Override via
      PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX / PH_KERNEL_SWAP_MAX env vars. Requires
      cgroup v2 (the launcher refuses to run on cgroup v1).

   b) Update the Layout tree (currently after the "Notebook edits
      needed" section) to list launch_kernel.sh and
      install_kernel_spec.py alongside the existing scripts.

6. Manual verification (note in PR test plan, not automated):
     - python replication/scripts/install_kernel_spec.py
     - jupyter kernelspec list -> confirm "ph-project-capped" appears
     - In VS Code, Select Kernel -> "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)"
       opens a kernel
     - In a cell: import os; os.system("systemctl --user status | grep MemoryMax")
       should show MemoryMax=48G on the scope unit

Testing notes
-------------
This is a launch path with the same risk profile as wz1.1 (a runaway
kernel can take down north). The shim-based tests catch regressions
in the script logic; live OOM verification is the user's job in the
PR test plan, same as wz1.1.

The tests for both scripts must NOT touch the real
~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels directory — use tmp_path.

VS Code kernel discovery: ~/.local/share/jupyter/kernels/ is the
standard user-scoped kernel dir, picked up by jupyter_client's
KernelSpecManager which the VS Code Jupyter extension delegates to.
This is verified to work on north.

Acceptance
----------
- launch_kernel.sh exists, is executable, bash -n clean.
- install_kernel_spec.py installs the spec to user kernels dir.
- jupyter kernelspec list shows "ph-project-capped" after install.
- VS Code kernel picker shows "Python 3 (ph-project, capped)".
- A kernel started via that spec runs inside a systemd-run scope
  with MemoryMax=48G visible via systemctl --user status.
- All tests pass.
- README documents install + selection in both VS Code and JupyterLab,
  and the Layout tree lists the two new files.

title: Add c_has_warn boolean column to baseline_summary.csv
description:
Summary
-------
results/baseline_summary.csv currently uses a string column c_warn that holds
the Unicode character U+26A0 ("⚠") as a sentinel for OOV warnings. Phase 4
(ph-project-bt5) will programmatically join this CSV with mBERT results;
filtering on a Unicode string is fragile (encoding drift, trailing whitespace,
NaN handling).

Implementation
--------------
1. In notebooks/baseline_comparison.ipynb (the assemble-summary cell), add
   c_has_warn: bool alongside c_warn (keep c_warn for human-readable display).
2. Compute c_has_warn = (str(marker).strip() == "⚠") for any pair
   involving the (lang, domain), ored across pairs.
3. Re-execute notebook; verify baseline_summary.csv has 32 cols (was 31).

Why this matters
----------------
Forward compatibility for Phase 4 (ph-project-bt5) which will consume
baseline_summary.csv. Should land before bt5 begins reading this file.

title: Pin spaCy model versions for byte-identical KWIC reproducibility
description:
Summary
-------
data/kwic/SCHEMA.md (lines 195-205) requires that re-running
scripts/extract_kwic.py with identical inputs produce a byte-identical
CSV. The sidecar JSON records the matcher as "spacy:en_core_web_md"
and "spacy:es_core_news_md" with no version tag. If a contributor
upgrades en_core_web_md between runs, the lemma sequence may change
and the CSV will no longer be byte-identical despite identical inputs.

Options
-------
1. Pin the spaCy model versions in a comment in requirements.txt and
   record the installed version in the sidecar JSON's "matchers" field
   at extraction time (e.g., "spacy:en_core_web_md==3.8.0"). This is
   the lightest fix and detects drift after the fact.
2. Add a download script (scripts/download_spacy_models.sh) that pins
   the URL of a specific .whl file from spaCy's GitHub releases. This
   is the same pattern used for fastText (scripts/download_fasttext.sh).
3. Provide a matcher-info accessor (e.g., SpacyMatcher.model_version())
   so 5f9.4 / 5f9.5 can write the version string into the sidecar
   without parsing pip metadata.

Recommendation
--------------
Pick (1) + (3): record installed version in the sidecar; add a
comment in requirements.txt naming the model version under which the
nine reference KWIC CSVs were produced.

Files to touch
---------------
- phase1_kwic/matchers.py (add a model_version property/method to
  SpacyMatcher and PymorphyMatcher)
- scripts/extract_kwic.py (record matcher.model_version() in the
  sidecar JSON's "matchers" field — task 5f9.4 will add this script)
- requirements.txt (comment naming the reference model versions)

Acceptance
----------
- Sidecar JSON includes spaCy / pymorphy3 model versions.
- Extraction with the same inputs and same model versions produces a
  byte-identical CSV (existing 5f9.4 acceptance criterion).

title: Within-language split null for Kushnareva pipeline
description:
Compute a within-(lang, term) split distance as a noise-floor baseline for the
cross-language Kushnareva PD distances. No re-extraction needed: the existing
data/phase3/diagram_distances/wasserstein.npz tensor (12, 12, 680, 680, 2)
already contains per-context pairwise distances, with metadata tagging each
context's (lang, term).

Recipe (mirrors notebook cell 7 aggregation):
  1. Load tensor, take mean over (layer, head) axes -> mean_h1 (680, 680).
  2. Group context indices by (lang, term).
  3. For each group with >= 20 contexts, K=200 random splits into halves A, B;
     record mean(mean_h1[A][:, B]) per trial.
  4. Compute cross-language same-term distance for each translation triple
     using existing per_term_test_statistic helper.
  5. Print a per-term table + write CSV to results/phase3_diagram_distances/.

Output: scripts/within_language_null.py + results/phase3_diagram_distances/within_language_null.csv

Discovered from: validation conversation 2026-05-06.

title: Consolidate phase3 result dirs in .gitignore as glob
description: Currently .gitignore lists 'results/phase3_thresholds/' as a per-notebook entry. mwk.3 will need 'results/phase3_ripser_templates/' and mwk.4 will need 'results/phase3_comparison/' (or similar). Consolidate as a single glob 'results/phase3_*/' to prevent per-PR forget. Surfaced by the architecture review on PR for ph-project-mwk.2.

title: Vectorize permutation_test_per_term and russian_blue_zoom (BLAS matmul, mirror chz)
description:
## Summary

`permutation_test_per_term` (around line 1416 of `replication/diagram_distances.py`) and `russian_blue_zoom` (around line 1610) still use Python-level inner loops with per-iteration DataFrame manipulation. After ph-project-chz vectorized the per-domain test, these are the two remaining slow paths in the same module. They are called from `phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (per-term and Russian-blues zoom analyses, blr.4).

## Why this is a follow-up, not part of chz

`permutation_test_per_term` shuffles term labels *within each language*, so the K-batch shuffle pattern from chz needs adapting (per-language sub-permutation rather than global permutation). Similarly, `russian_blue_zoom` is restricted to Russian samples and uses a sub-permutation pattern. Both are structurally a notch more complex than chz, but the same BLAS-chunked matmul on cached upper-triangle invariants will work.

## Implementation sketch

Mirror chz at `replication/diagram_distances.py:875` (now-vectorized `permutation_test_per_domain`):
1. Precompute per-cell invariants (`triu_i`, `triu_j`, `dists_upper`, `total_sum`, `n_pairs`).
2. Generate (K, n) permutation matrix upfront, but apply the per-language constraint: shuffle the term-label vector independently within each language block.
3. Build between-masks at the upper-triangle and compute null distribution via chunked float32 BLAS matmul.
4. Add equivalence tests in `tests/test_diagram_distances_per_term.py` mirroring the `TestVectorizedEquivalence` class added under chz.

## Acceptance

- Existing `tests/test_diagram_distances_per_term.py` continues to pass.
- New equivalence test inlines the old Python-loop algorithm and confirms p_value/effect_size match within Monte-Carlo noise.
- Wall-time benchmark on cell 9 (per-term) of `phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` drops by an order of magnitude.

## When

Same triage as chz: defer until after May 6 paper deadline. Most relevant for blr.6 robustness reruns.

## Discovered from

ph-project-chz architecture review (commit f613362). The chz pattern made it obvious that the same trick applies to the other two permutation tests in the module.

title: Replication: patch and run features_prediction.ipynb
description:
The prediction notebook (replication/notebooks/features_prediction.ipynb)
concatenates features from THREE families: threshold (Section 4.1),
barcode (Section 4.2), and template/distance-to-pattern (Section 4.3).
Loading any of barcode_train_file or templ_train_file will fail until
ph-project-nmh (ripserplusplus) is resolved, since those features are
computed in features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb.

INTERIM (2026-04-30): added replication/scripts/predict_threshold_only.py
as a smaller-scope substitute. It trains logistic regression on threshold
features alone and reports accuracy + matthews corrcoef. This unblocks
producing *a* classifier number now without waiting on ripserplusplus.

What this issue still tracks (do NOT close on the script-only path):

Files to edit when ripser/template features become available:
- replication/notebooks/features_prediction.ipynb (cells 5, 8, 12-14, 16)

Edits expected:
1. Setup cell at top with sys.path additions for reference/ and
   replication/ (same as threshold notebook).
2. train_subset/test_subset: "test_5k" -> "train" / "test".
3. input_dir: "./small_gpt_web/" -> "../data/processed/".
4. Add features_dir = "../outputs/" and replace
   `input_dir + "features/"` -> `features_dir + "features/"` in the
   feature-file path constructions (cell 5 and around old_f_/ripser_/templ_).
5. Verify no batch_encode_plus/pad_to_max_length usage (cursory grep
   shows none, but full pass needed).

Verification once features all exist: running should print accuracy and
matthews_corrcoef. With 500/class training data, accuracy will be well
below the paper's ~85-95% — anything above 50% indicates the pipeline
is wired up correctly.
close_reason: Merged in PR #6 (full notebook patches + dispatcher + 30 tests). Anna's interim path predict_threshold_only.py landed via PR #5. End-to-end notebook execution still gated on barcode notebook outputs — see ph-project-qrs.

title: Hoist plot_barcode helper from notebooks into baselines/topology.py
description: plot_barcode is duplicated verbatim across notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb (cell 11) and notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb (cell 16). The C copy is explicitly marked as mirroring B's. Both are pure matplotlib bar-drawing on a structured-array barcode dict. A third baseline notebook would make this a triplicate; even with two copies the silent-divergence risk is real. Hoist into baselines/topology.py (or a new baselines/plotting.py if more plotting helpers accumulate), add a unit test that draws against a synthetic barcode and asserts the right number of axes children, then update both notebooks to import it.

title: Fix get_token_length batch_encode_plus regression in mbert_attention_ripser cell 16
description:
mwk.3 missed migrating cell 16 of mbert_attention_ripser.ipynb (the get_token_length helper) off the deprecated `tokenizer.batch_encode_plus(...)` + `pad_to_max_length=True` API. The threshold substrate's analogous cell was already migrated by replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py, but the ripser substrate's was not, and we only patched the OTHER deprecated call site (cell 45 / get_list_of_ids) during mwk.3.

`transformers 5.5.3` (current install) removed `batch_encode_plus` entirely, so the call raises `AttributeError: BertTokenizer has no attribute batch_encode_plus` immediately on the first run. Confirmed by Anna while smoke-running the notebook on en/color.

Files modified
--------------
- notebooks/mbert_attention_ripser.ipynb cell 16 — replaced batch_encode_plus(...) with tokenizer(list(...), padding="max_length", ...). Same shape as the threshold notebook's cell 17.

Process gap
-----------
Pre-PR review missed this because tests/test_mbert_attention_ripser_features.py only checks output artifacts; it doesn't smoke-run the notebook. Adding an import/syntax-level smoke test for new notebooks is a candidate for follow-up but out of scope here — this issue tracks just the cell-16 fix.

Closes when: notebook runs end-to-end on en/color without AttributeError on the get_token_length call.
close_reason: Notebook ran end-to-end for en/ru/es color; all 18 ripser+template feature tests pass with PH_REQUIRE_FEATURES=1. Closing condition met.

title: Tighten env-isolation in launcher shim tests
description: Reviewer flagged: tests/test_launch_kernel_sh.py and tests/test_launch_jupyter_sh.py both use a _run_script helper that builds env from os.environ.copy() then .update(env_override). update() overwrites but never deletes, so popping a var from a dict copy in the test does not actually unset it for the subprocess. Default-value tests (test_default_mem_max_*) silently pass for the wrong reason if the developer has PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX (or JUPYTER_MEM_MAX) exported in their shell. Fix: extend _run_script to accept an unset_keys list and call env.pop(k, None) for each, then update test_default_* callers to pass unset_keys=["PH_KERNEL_MEM_MAX"] etc. Apply to both test files.

title: Kushnareva replication: run pipeline as binary classifier on WebText/GPT-2
description:
Replicate the original Kushnareva 2021 pipeline (binary human/machine text
classification) before adapting to cross-linguistic comparison. Pedagogical
goal: build intuition for the feature shapes, sanity-check the local stack
(CUDA, ripser, mBERT load), and produce a reference number that any future
port of grab_weights.py can be diffed against.

STATUS (2026-04-30): Smoke test successful. Threshold notebook runs
end-to-end on test split (500 human + 500 machine), producing:
- Attention tensor (1000, 12, 12, 128, 128) float16, range [0, 1],
  mean 1/128 (uniform softmax baseline) — sanity passes.
- Feature tensor (12, 12, 6, 1000, 6) float16, no all-zero slices —
  ripser-zero bug class from the prior tda-project attempt is not
  present here.

Five compat issues found and fixed during the smoke test (recorded in
replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py for repeatability):
1. tokenizer.batch_encode_plus -> tokenizer (transformers 5.x removed).
2. pad_to_max_length=True -> padding="max_length" (renamed kwarg).
3. tokenizer(np.ndarray) requires list[str] (stricter input check).
4. Hardcoded device='cuda:1' -> 'cuda:0' (one-GPU machine).
5. nx.from_numpy_matrix -> nx.from_numpy_array (networkx 3.x removed),
   monkey-patched in setup cell so reference/stats_count.py keeps
   working without edits.

Follow-up work filed:
- ph-project-f93: run threshold notebook on train + valid splits.
- ph-project-3nh: patch and run features_prediction.ipynb.
- ph-project-nmh: handle ripserplusplus dependency (or skip barcode
  notebook for the replication).

reference/ stays frozen throughout — all compat fixes live in
replication/grab_weights_compat.py and the patched setup cell.
close_reason: Smoke test complete: threshold notebook runs end-to-end on test split, sane outputs, no ripser-zero bug. Follow-ups filed as ph-project-f93/3nh/nmh.

title: Investigate why launch_jupyter.sh starts server without token
description:
Symptom
-------
Running replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh starts JupyterLab but
the printed URL does NOT include a ?token=... query string. When
VS Code attempts to connect via "Existing Jupyter Server...", it
prompts for a password.

Root cause (confirmed)
----------------------
~/.jupyter/jupyter_lab_config.py contains:

    c.ServerApp.password = 'argon2:$argon2id$v=19$m=10240,t=10,p=8$...'

A password was set (likely via `jupyter server password`) on
2026-02-07. When ServerApp.password is set, Jupyter prefers
password auth and doesn't print a tokenized URL.

Anna doesn't remember the password. Workaround for now: reset via
`jupyter server password`, OR delete the password line entirely
to let Jupyter auto-generate a fresh token per launch (matches
wz1.1's original design).

Acceptance
----------
- Decide between three options:
  (a) reset password to a known value, document where it's stored
      (password manager only — not in repo).
  (b) remove the password line so launch_jupyter.sh prints the
      standard tokenized URL each launch.
  (c) keep current behavior but document in launch_jupyter.sh /
      README that the host uses persistent password auth.
- ph-project-de9 README pivot should reflect whichever choice
  is made.

Out of scope
------------
- Network exposure / remote access hardening — server stays
  localhost-only.
close_reason: Resolved by removing c.ServerApp.password line from ~/.jupyter/jupyter_lab_config.py. Restarting launch_jupyter.sh now auto-generates a fresh token per launch (matches wz1.1's intended design). Verified by Anna 2026-05-02.

title: Phase 1 — KWIC extraction
description: Extract ~200 KWIC sentences per canon term per language from Leipzig Corpora. Three languages, three domains. Outputs feed Phase 3.

title: Decide and document KWIC output schema
description:
Before any KWIC extraction subtask can produce reusable outputs, decide and document the on-disk schema. Downstream tasks (ph-project-mwk.2 in particular) need to know exact paths, file format, and column names to write input-loader cells against.

Decisions to make:
1. File format: CSV per (lang, domain), JSONL per (lang, domain), one master CSV with lang+domain columns, or per-term files?
2. Required columns: at minimum `sentence` (the KWIC line) and `term` (the canon term that anchored the extraction). Optional: `position` (token index of the term within the sentence), `source` (Leipzig corpus file), `kwic_window` (left/right token count used).
3. Path convention: data/kwic/{lang}/{domain}.{ext}? data/kwic/{lang}_{domain}.csv? Per-term subdirs?
4. Per-term sample count: target ~200 per Phase 1 epic body. What's the floor for terms with fewer corpus hits?

Files to modify:
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md (extend with a KWIC output section, OR create data/kwic/SCHEMA.md sibling)

Files to read for context:
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md (existing schema doc for canon terms — model the new one after this)
- ph-project-5f9 (Phase 1 epic body — see "outputs feed Phase 3")
- replication/data/processed/ (for shape reference — replication uses single CSV with sentence/labels columns)

Testing notes: this is a documentation task. Implementation steps:
1. Sketch the schema in a markdown doc.
2. Open this issue (bd update ph-project-5f9.X --status in_progress) and post the proposed schema in a comment for Anna to review.
3. After approval, commit the schema doc and close.

Blocks ph-project-mwk.2 (which needs the schema before its input-loader cell can be written).

title: phase1_kwic.1: Leipzig 1M news corpus download script
description:
Summary
-------
Provide a reproducible download script that fetches Leipzig 1M news
corpora for en, ru, es and stages the sentences files at
data/leipzig/<lang>/. This is the raw input feed for the KWIC
extraction pipeline (5f9.3 / 5f9.4).

Files to modify
---------------
- scripts/download_leipzig.sh (new)
- .gitignore (append data/leipzig/)

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- scripts/download_fasttext.sh (existing pattern to mirror)
- replication/scripts/download_webtext.sh (existing pattern to mirror)
- CLAUDE.md (project structure conventions)

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Create scripts/download_leipzig.sh:
   - set -euo pipefail
   - Verify wget or curl is available; tar; sha256sum
   - Define corpus IDs (latest year available across all three; verify
     against https://wortschatz.uni-leipzig.de/en/download — 2020 is the
     fallback target):
       eng_news_2020_1M
       rus_news_2020_1M
       spa_news_2020_1M
   - URL pattern: https://downloads.wortschatz-leipzig.de/corpora/<ID>.tar.gz
   - For each language:
       a. Download tarball into data/leipzig/_downloads/ (keep so reruns
          can re-extract without re-downloading).
       b. Verify a recorded sha256 if present, else print the observed
          sha256 and emit a one-line "record this" warning the first
          time so we can pin it on the second run.
       c. Extract only the <ID>-sentences.txt member to
          data/leipzig/<lang>/<ID>-sentences.txt.
       d. Print final size + line count.
   - Idempotent: skip download if tarball exists; skip extract if the
     final sentences file exists with the expected size.
2. Append data/leipzig/ to .gitignore (mirror the existing fasttext
   entry style).
3. Add a "Leipzig corpora" section to the script's own header comment
   describing the pinned corpus IDs and the URL pattern.

Testing notes
-------------
No pytest needed — this is a download script. Acceptance is manual:
- bash scripts/download_leipzig.sh runs to completion on north.
- data/leipzig/{en,ru,es}/<ID>-sentences.txt all exist and are
  non-empty.
- Re-running the script is a no-op.

Acceptance
----------
- Script exists, is executable, and is idempotent.
- Three sentences files are present at the expected paths after a
  successful run.
- .gitignore excludes data/leipzig/ so the ~600 MB stays out of git.
- Pinned corpus IDs are documented in the script header.

title: phase1_kwic.matchers: per-language lemma matchers + canon loader
description:
Summary
-------
Implement the phase1_kwic Python package skeleton: canon-term YAML
loader plus per-language lemma matchers (pymorphy3 for ru, spaCy
en_core_web_md / es_core_news_md for en/es). The matchers turn a
running text into a list of (term, source_token_index) hits used by
the extraction pipeline (5f9.4).

Files to modify (new)
---------------------
- phase1_kwic/__init__.py
- phase1_kwic/canon.py
- phase1_kwic/matchers.py
- tests/phase1_kwic/__init__.py
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_canon.py
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_matchers.py
- requirements.txt (add pymorphy3, spacy; pin spaCy model versions in a comment)

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- data/kwic/SCHEMA.md (canonical schema; this task implements the
