  matchers it describes — see "Tokenization for window selection")
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md
- canon-terms/{en,es,ru}/{color,emotion,kinship}.yaml (sample inputs)
- baselines/__init__.py (SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES — reuse, do not duplicate)
- baselines/distances.py (HEAD_POSITION dict; multi-word head logic to
  mirror)
- baselines/vectors.py (existing package style: docstrings, env-var
  override pattern, KeyedVectors-style API)

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Add to requirements.txt:
       pymorphy3
       spacy>=3.7
   Plus a comment that en_core_web_md and es_core_news_md must be
   downloaded with `python -m spacy download <name>` (do NOT pin them
   in requirements.txt — spaCy models are installed via spacy CLI,
   not pip directly, on this stack).

2. phase1_kwic/__init__.py:
       SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES = baselines.SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES  # reuse
       DOMAINS = frozenset({"color", "emotion", "kinship"})

3. phase1_kwic/canon.py:
       @dataclass(frozen=True)
       class Term:
           surface: str          # the YAML "term" value
           gloss: str | None
           source: str
           notes: str | None
           lemmas: tuple[str, ...]   # lemmatized whitespace tokens of surface

       def load_canon(lang: str, domain: str) -> list[Term]:
           ...
   The matcher is responsible for filling .lemmas — load_canon
   accepts a matcher arg or returns Term objects with lemmas computed
   lazily on first access. Pick whichever is simpler; document in
   docstring. Validate lang/domain against SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES /
   DOMAINS and raise ValueError on mismatch.

4. phase1_kwic/matchers.py:

   IMPORTANT: import HEAD_POSITION directly — do NOT redefine it.
       from baselines.distances import HEAD_POSITION

   Matcher API (Protocol; only one method — see "Why" below):
       class Matcher(Protocol):
           def lemmatize(self, sentence: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
               """Return [(surface_token, lemma), ...]."""

       def get_matcher(lang: str) -> Matcher:
           if lang == "ru": return PymorphyMatcher()
           if lang in {"en", "es"}: return SpacyMatcher(lang)
           raise ValueError(...)

   Why only `lemmatize` and not a separate `find_term_hits`: 5f9.4's
   extraction loop builds a single lemma -> [term_idx] dict from all
   canon terms and scans sentences once, calling matcher.lemmatize()
   per sentence. Matching logic (single-word, multi-word head
   resolution) lives in 5f9.4, not in the matcher class.

   - PymorphyMatcher: tokenize on whitespace, lemmatize each token via
     MorphAnalyzer().parse(token)[0].normal_form. Cache the analyzer.
   - SpacyMatcher: lazy-load the model with the lemmatizer kept and
     parser/NER disabled for speed:
       nlp = spacy.load(model_name, disable=["parser", "ner"])
     Keep the tagger — the lemmatizer needs it for accurate
     English/Spanish lemmas. Tokens emitted as (text, lemma_).

5. Tests:
   - tests/phase1_kwic/test_canon.py:
     * Loads each of 9 (lang × domain) yaml files; row counts match
       hand-computed values (en color = 11, ru kinship = 34, etc.).
     * Term.lemmas matches whitespace token count for surface.
     * Invalid lang or domain raises ValueError.
   - tests/phase1_kwic/test_matchers.py: hand-curated sentence pairs:
     * en: lemmatize("I love red apples") yields a list whose lemmas
       contain "red" at index 2.
     * en: lemmatize("Mother, please come here") — "mother" lemma at
       index 0 (case-insensitive lemma).
     * ru: lemmatize("Я любил мать всю жизнь") — "мать" lemma at index 2.
     * ru: lemmatize("красная машина проехала") — "красный" lemma at
       index 0 (morphological inflection).
     * es: lemmatize("Tengo mucho miedo") — "miedo" lemma at index 2.
     * Multi-word head resolution is unit-tested in 5f9.4, not here.

Testing notes
-------------
Tests must run on north's stack without GPU. spaCy model loads are
slow (~5s); use module-scoped fixtures so each model loads once.
Total test runtime <30s acceptable. If pymorphy3 lemma differs from
expected on a few cases (it can, for proper nouns / homonyms),
prefer adjusting the test sentence over hard-coding the variant.

Acceptance
----------
- pytest tests/phase1_kwic/test_canon.py and test_matchers.py both
  pass on north.
- All 186 canon terms across 9 yamls load without errors and have
  lemmas computed.
- get_matcher("ru") uses pymorphy3; get_matcher("en") and ("es") use
  spaCy.
- HEAD_POSITION is imported from baselines.distances, not redefined.

title: phase1_kwic.extract: KWIC extraction pipeline + CLI
description:
Summary
-------
Implement the corpus iteration -> match -> dedup -> sample -> emit
pipeline that produces the per-(lang, domain) CSVs described in
data/kwic/SCHEMA.md. Provide a CLI wrapper that runs one (lang,
domain) at a time and writes the CSV plus the JSON sidecar.

Files to modify (new)
---------------------
- phase1_kwic/extract.py
- scripts/extract_kwic.py
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_extract.py

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- data/kwic/SCHEMA.md (canonical output schema — implement exactly)
- phase1_kwic/canon.py (load_canon — written under 5f9.3)
- phase1_kwic/matchers.py (get_matcher — written under 5f9.3)
- replication/scripts/prepare_csv.py (similar shape: read raw, write
  CSV; mirror the empty-string filter from lines 36-47 to avoid
  pandas NaN roundtrip)

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. phase1_kwic/extract.py:
       def extract_kwic(
           lang: str,
           domain: str,
           corpus_path: pathlib.Path,
           corpus_source_id: str,
           n_samples: int = 200,
           seed: int = 0,
           window: int = 10,
           min_post_target_tokens: int = 5,
       ) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, dict]:
           """Stream the Leipzig sentences file, match canon terms,
           dedup, sample, and return (df, report) per the schema."""

   Internals:
   a. Load canon: terms = load_canon(lang, domain).
   b. Load matcher: matcher = get_matcher(lang).
   c. Build a lemma -> [term_index] dict for O(N_sentence) scanning.
      Multi-word terms keyed on their head lemma; on hit, verify the
      full lemma sequence appears.
   d. Stream corpus_path (Leipzig TSV: idx\tsentence, UTF-8).
      Skip empty lines. For each sentence:
         - whitespace_tokens = sentence.split()
         - lemmatized = matcher.lemmatize(sentence)
         - For each candidate hit, compute target_idx in source, then
           compute KWIC window [max(0, i-window) : i+window+1] and the
           emitted target_idx in the windowed string.
         - Drop if windowed sentence has fewer than
           min_post_target_tokens tokens after target.
         - Append (term_surface, kwic_str, emitted_idx, corpus_source_id)
           to that term's hit list.
   e. Per term:
         - Dedup hits on the kwic_str field.
         - If len > n_samples, downsample using SHA-256 subseed per
           SCHEMA.md (do NOT use builtin hash()).
   f. Build DataFrame with columns in exact order: term, sentence,
      target_idx, corpus_source.
   g. Build report dict matching SCHEMA.md sidecar schema.

2. scripts/extract_kwic.py (CLI):
       --lang {en,ru,es}
       --domain {color,emotion,kinship}
       --corpus-path PATH (default: derive from a constant
         data/leipzig/<lang>/<corpus_id>-sentences.txt)
       --corpus-source-id (default: derived from filename)
       --n-samples 200
       --seed 0
       --output-dir data/kwic (default)

   Writes:
     <output-dir>/<lang>/<domain>.csv
     <output-dir>/<lang>/<domain>.report.json

   Use pandas.DataFrame.to_csv(..., index=False, encoding="utf-8").

3. Tests (tests/phase1_kwic/test_extract.py):
   - Synthetic 50-sentence corpus written to a tmp file. Three terms
     planted at known positions across the corpus with varying counts
     (e.g., "red" 20x, "blue" 5x, "purple" 1x).
   - Verify:
     * Output DataFrame columns match schema exactly, in order.
     * Sentences are deduplicated.
     * For terms with > n_samples_test (e.g., n_samples_test=10) hits,
       sample size is capped.
     * For terms with < n_samples_test hits, all are kept and
       under_target=True in the report.
     * Sampling is deterministic: extract twice with same seed -> same
       row order.
     * Sampling is sensitive to seed: different seed -> different sample.
     * target_idx is within bounds for every emitted row.
     * Windowed sentence never exceeds 2 * window + 1 tokens.
     * Min-tokens-after-target filter drops short sentences.

Testing notes
-------------
Test runs use a fake matcher (or the real spaCy/pymorphy matcher with
deliberately simple test sentences). Synthetic corpus avoids the
~150-300MB Leipzig file dependency for CI. Real-corpus integration is
the responsibility of 5f9.5 (run + validation notebook).

Acceptance
----------
- pytest tests/phase1_kwic/test_extract.py passes.
- python -m scripts.extract_kwic --lang en --domain color (with the
  real Leipzig corpus present) writes data/kwic/en/color.csv and
  data/kwic/en/color.report.json conforming to SCHEMA.md.
- Re-running with the same seed produces a byte-identical CSV.

title: phase1_kwic.run: execute extraction across all 9 (lang × domain) + validation notebook
description:
Summary
-------
Run scripts/extract_kwic.py across all nine (lang × domain) pairs to
produce the full Phase 1 KWIC dataset on disk, then build a validation
notebook that summarizes per-term hit counts, flags under-target
terms, and shows random sample sentences for spot-checking.

Files to modify (new)
---------------------
- notebooks/phase1_kwic_validation.ipynb

Side effects (gitignored)
-------------------------
- data/kwic/{en,ru,es}/{color,emotion,kinship}.csv (9 files)
- data/kwic/{en,ru,es}/{color,emotion,kinship}.report.json (9 files)

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- data/kwic/SCHEMA.md
- scripts/extract_kwic.py (5f9.4)
- phase1_kwic/extract.py (5f9.4)
- notebooks/baseline_A_descriptive.ipynb (existing notebook style to
  mirror: setup cell, per-(lang, domain) loop, matplotlib summary)

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Run extraction for all 9 combinations:
       for lang in en ru es; do
         for domain in color emotion kinship; do
           python -m scripts.extract_kwic --lang $lang --domain $domain
         done
       done
   Capture stdout/stderr; expect each to take ~30-60s on the 1M corpus.

2. Verify all 9 CSVs and 9 sidecars exist on disk. Commit nothing —
   data is gitignored.

3. Build notebooks/phase1_kwic_validation.ipynb:
   Cell 1 — setup: import pandas, matplotlib, json, pathlib.
   Cell 2 — config: data_dir = Path("../data/kwic"); LANGS = ["en",
            "ru", "es"]; DOMAINS = ["color", "emotion", "kinship"].
   Cell 3 — load all 9 reports into a dict keyed by (lang, domain).
   Cell 4 — global summary table: rows = (lang, domain), columns =
            n_terms, n_under_target, mean_n_emitted, total_rows.
            Print as a markdown table.
   Cell 5 — per-(lang, domain) bar charts: for each combo, plot
            n_emitted per term sorted descending. Mark the 200 target
            line. 9 subplots in a 3x3 grid.
   Cell 6 — under-target listing: for any term with under_target=True,
            print (lang, domain, term, n_corpus_hits) sorted by
            n_corpus_hits ascending. This is the "do we have enough
            data?" diagnostic.
   Cell 7 — sample inspection: for each (lang, domain), randomly draw
            3 terms and 3 sentences each, print the canon term + 3
            KWIC strings underneath. This is the human-readability
            spot-check.

4. If under-target terms cluster in any one (lang, domain), file a
   discovered-from issue (e.g., "ru/kinship has 8 under-target terms;
   investigate corpus genre or expand to 3M") with
   --deps discovered-from:ph-project-5f9.5 (or whichever ID this lands
   under).

Testing notes
-------------
This is execution + reporting, not unit-testable code. Acceptance is
visual + the schema-tests subtask (5f9.6) running cleanly against the
produced CSVs.

Acceptance
----------
- All 18 files exist (9 CSVs + 9 sidecars) at the expected paths.
- Notebook runs top-to-bottom without error.
- Summary table shows total rows in the 30-37k range (close to
  9 domains × ~20 terms × 200 = 36k).
- Under-target term list, if non-empty, has a discovered-from issue
  filed for any clustering pattern that needs follow-up.

title: phase1_kwic.schema_tests: validate KWIC outputs against SCHEMA.md
description:
Summary
-------
Add a test module that validates the on-disk KWIC outputs (CSVs +
JSON sidecars) against the rules in data/kwic/SCHEMA.md "Validation
rules (asserted in tests)" section. Two modes: synthetic-fixture
tests for CI (no real corpus needed), and a real-data integration
test that runs only when the data files exist on disk.

Files to modify (new)
---------------------
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_outputs.py
- tests/phase1_kwic/conftest.py (synthetic CSV+sidecar fixture)

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- data/kwic/SCHEMA.md ("Validation rules" section is the spec)
- phase1_kwic/extract.py (5f9.4)
- canon-terms/{en,es,ru}/{color,emotion,kinship}.yaml

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. conftest.py: a fixture builder that writes a synthetic CSV +
   sidecar to a tmp dir. Two parameterized fixtures:
   - schema_compliant: minimal valid pair.
   - schema_violating: parameterized with a single intentional bug
     (missing column, NaN in sentence, target_idx out of bounds,
     unknown term, mismatched language script, labels != term).
     Each violation should fail exactly one assertion.

2. test_outputs.py: parameterized over (lang, domain). For each,
   read data/kwic/<lang>/<domain>.csv and the sidecar; assert:
     a. CSV columns are exactly the FIVE columns from SCHEMA.md, in
        order: term, labels, sentence, target_idx, corpus_source.
     b. labels equals term row-for-row (substrate-compat alias).
     c. No NaN, empty, or whitespace-only sentence values.
     d. Every term value appears in the corresponding canon-terms
        YAML (load via phase1_kwic.canon.load_canon).
     e. target_idx is an int, 0 <= target_idx < len(sentence.split()).
     f. corpus_source matches the pinned ID expected for that
        language (constants in phase1_kwic).
     g. Lang-specific character-set checks:
        - ru: at least one Cyrillic char in every sentence.
        - es: at least one accented char in the file as a whole
          (not every sentence — many words are unaccented).
        - en: predominantly ASCII (>95% of chars in [a-zA-Z0-9
          punctuation/whitespace]); soft check.
     h. Per-term row counts in the CSV match n_emitted in the sidecar.

3. The real-data test class is gated on file presence:
       @pytest.mark.skipif(
           not (REPO_ROOT / "data/kwic/en/color.csv").exists(),
           reason="KWIC data not extracted — run scripts/extract_kwic.py first",
       )
   So CI without data still runs the synthetic-fixture tests; locally
   after a full extraction run, the real-data tests run too.

Out of scope
------------
- Reproducibility (byte-identical reruns) is covered by 5f9.4's own
  determinism tests; do NOT duplicate here.

Testing notes
-------------
The synthetic-fixture tests are the primary gate; real-data tests are
a secondary integration check. Both share the same assertion helpers
(extract them to a private module-level _assert_schema_compliant
function so the suite stays DRY).

Acceptance
----------
- pytest tests/phase1_kwic/test_outputs.py passes against the
  synthetic fixtures.
- Each schema_violating parameter yields exactly one assertion
  failure (not zero, not two) — proves the validator detects the
  intended bug class.
- After 5f9.5 lands, running the test suite locally validates all 9
  real CSVs.

title: phase1_kwic.extract: address 5f9.4 review findings (test gaps + duplication cleanup)
description:
Summary
-------
Address findings from the three pre-PR reviews of 5f9.4. The implementation
is correct, but the reviewers flagged a critical test-quality bug, several
test gaps, and two non-trivial duplication cases that should be cleaned up
before merge.

Files to modify
---------------
- phase1_kwic/extract.py
- phase1_kwic/canon.py (extract shared helper)
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_extract.py
- scripts/extract_kwic.py (no behavior change; only if needed for testability)
- scripts/download_leipzig.sh (one-line sync comment)

Required test additions / fixes
-------------------------------
1. CRITICAL: test_sha256_subseed_used currently computes `expected_subseed`
   but discards it (dead code) — the test would pass even if the
   implementation used `random.Random(seed)` with no SHA-256 involvement.
   Replicate the SHA-256 → random.Random → sample chain externally against
   the known synthetic corpus and assert the emitted KWIC sentences match
   that external replication exactly.

2. MAJOR: Add a per-term-subseed-independence test. Construct two
   over-target terms with the same global seed; assert their samples are
   different (proves the term surface is mixed into the subseed, not
   shared global RNG state).

3. MAJOR: Add a left-window-clip test. The current synthetic builder
   always plants the target at whitespace index 3, so `max(0, i-window)`
   is never exercised non-trivially. Add a sentence with the target at
   index 0 (with enough post-target tokens to pass the filter) and assert
   `target_idx == 0` in the emitted row.

4. MAJOR: Add at least one happy-path test for scripts/extract_kwic.py's
   `main()` function. Invoke with --lang/--domain/--corpus-path/--output-dir
   pointing at a synthetic corpus + tmp output dir; assert the CSV and
   JSON files appear at the schema-mandated paths with the expected
   column order. (Use `_matcher_override`/`_terms_override` if the CLI
   needs a small refactor to plumb them, OR write the test to use the
   real spaCy matcher with a tiny synthetic English corpus — implementer's
   judgment.)

5. MINOR: test_empty_corpus_file should assert column schema on the
   returned empty DataFrame.

6. MINOR: test_report_window_subfields should call extract_kwic with at
   least one non-default `window` value (e.g., 5) and assert the report's
   window.left/right reflect that value.

7. MINOR: test_report_top_level_fields should assert
   `isinstance(report["matchers"], dict)` and that it has at least one
   non-empty entry.

Required code cleanup
---------------------
8. NON-TRIVIAL DUPLICATION: `_lemmatize_ws_token` in
   phase1_kwic/extract.py:114-141 is a copy of the per-whitespace-token
   lemma logic in phase1_kwic/canon.py:183-202. Extract a shared
   module-private helper in phase1_kwic/canon.py (or a new
   phase1_kwic/_lemma.py) and import it from both. Single source of
   truth for "lemmatize one whitespace token, prefer first alphabetic
   lemma, fall back to ws_token.lower()".

9. NON-TRIVIAL: `_matcher_versions()` in phase1_kwic/extract.py:92-107
   hard-codes "en_core_web_md" / "es_core_news_md" as string literals;
   the authoritative map is `_SPACY_MODEL` in phase1_kwic/matchers.py.
   Import `_SPACY_MODEL` and reference it instead of inlining the names.
   Also narrow the broad `except Exception` to `(AttributeError,
   ImportError)` so a real broken install surfaces instead of silently
   producing a stale version string.

10. CORPUS_SOURCE_ID is defined in BOTH phase1_kwic/extract.py:68-72
    (Python dict, canonical) and scripts/download_leipzig.sh:66-68 (Bash
    array). At minimum, add a sync comment in download_leipzig.sh that
    the IDs must match phase1_kwic.extract.CORPUS_SOURCE_ID.

11. TRIVIAL: Drop the unnecessary f-strings in
    phase1_kwic/extract.py:102-106 / 108-109 (no interpolation). Drop
    the always-true `isinstance(sentence, str)` check at extract.py:260
    (the value comes from `str.split`). Drop the dead `if not ws_tokens:
    continue` at extract.py:266-267 (already covered by the upstream
    whitespace-only filter).

12. TRIVIAL CLEANUP / READABILITY: head_offset is recomputed inside the
    per-sentence scan loop for every hit (extract.py:278-281); store it
    alongside the term index in `head_lemma_to_term_idxs` to avoid the
    recomputation.

Testing notes
-------------
All the new/fixed tests should run without the real Leipzig corpus and
without loading spaCy models (use the existing StubMatcher pattern).
The CLI test in step 4 is the one exception — it may pull in a real
spaCy load. If so, mark it appropriately or use the matcher override
seam.

Acceptance
----------
- pytest tests/phase1_kwic/test_extract.py passes (all existing + new).
- The SHA-256 test in step 1 actually fails if the implementation is
  reverted to plain `random.Random(seed)` (verify by mutation: temporarily
  break the impl locally; the new test must catch it).
- No more duplication of per-whitespace-token lemma logic between
  extract.py and canon.py.
- _matcher_versions reads spaCy model names from matchers._SPACY_MODEL.

title: Phase 3: punctuation stickiness at KWIC target_idx may shift mBERT wordpiece alignment
description:
Summary
-------
KWIC tokenization is whitespace-based (per data/kwic/SCHEMA.md), so
attached punctuation rides along with the target word. Sanity-check on
the emitted CSVs (2026-05-04) showed surface forms at target_idx like:

    en/emotion love:    "love  (3 rows), love"  (1), love,"  (1), love-in (1)
    es/color   rojo:    roja"  (2), rojo'  (1), rojo"  (1)
    ru/color   красный: (Cyrillic punctuation similar pattern)

These are correct per the schema — Leipzig text is preserved verbatim
and target_idx points at the head whitespace token. The concern is
downstream, in Phase 3:

When the threshold notebook tokenizes the sentence with mBERT and we
want to index attention "at the target", the mBERT wordpiece span
covering whitespace-token target_idx may include trailing punctuation
wordpieces (e.g., `love` + `"`) rather than the bare word. Whether
this matters depends on how Phase 3 uses target_idx:

- If we only use the full-sentence attention graph and never index
  per-target, this is a non-issue (the full sentence's topology is
  unaffected).
- If we add any per-target analysis ("what does mBERT attend to when
  it reads the canon target?"), the indexed wordpiece span may carry
  attached punctuation, which could blur the per-target signal.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- data/kwic/SCHEMA.md (lines 76-130: window selection and the
  punctuation-attached note at line 130)
- phase1_kwic/extract.py (lines 268-275: kwic_tokens slicing and
  target_idx recomputation)
- replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb
  (Phase 3 substrate; check whether it indexes per-target)

Investigation prompts
---------------------
1. Audit the Phase 3 notebooks (mwk.* family) to determine if any cell
   indexes attention at a specific token position, or if all features
   are computed over the full sentence's attention matrix.
2. If per-target indexing happens: decide between (a) stripping
   trailing punctuation from the target token before recomputing
   target_idx, (b) computing a wordpiece span at extraction time and
   storing it explicitly, or (c) leaving as-is and documenting that
   per-target features include adjacent punctuation.
3. Quantify: across the nine CSVs, what fraction of rows have
   non-alphabetic characters attached to the target whitespace token?
   This bounds the worst-case impact.

Acceptance
----------
Either:
- Confirmation that Phase 3 does not index per-target (no action
  needed; close as not-applicable), OR
- A documented decision in SCHEMA.md or the Phase 3 notebook for how
  per-target indexing handles attached punctuation, with whatever
  schema/code change that decision implies.

Why non-blocking
----------------
Filed as priority 3. Phase 3 work is not yet started, and the
threshold-only path (Kushnareva-style sentence-level features) does
not need per-target indexing. This is a flag for the future, not a
gate on any in-flight work.

title: Canon term lists from literature
description: Build literature-grounded canon term lists per language per domain. Each canon term must trace to a specific publication. Document any deviations from sources.
close_reason: All three subtasks complete and closed (5m7.1 color, 5m7.2 emotion, 5m7.3 kinship). Nine yaml inventories on disk under canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/{color,emotion,kinship}.yaml plus three domain reports and SCHEMA.md. Every term traces to a specific publication; deviations from sources are documented per the project rule. Unblocks ph-project-ssa.3 (term vector extraction).

title: Color domain literature search
description: Berlin and Kay (1969) basic color terms; post-1969 critiques; Russian blue distinction (Davidoff 1999, Winawer 2007, Paramei). Compile authoritative term inventories for English, Russian, Spanish.
close_reason: Complete. canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/color.yaml all on disk: EN 11 BCTs (Berlin & Kay 1969 Stage VII), RU 12 BCTs (Berlin & Kay + Davidoff 1999 goluboj split), ES 11 BCTs with documented variants. canon-terms/color_report.md provides the interpretive framing.

title: Emotion domain literature search
description: Wierzbicka NSM primes; Russian тоска literature (Wierzbicka 1992, Apresjan); Spanish duende and affective vocabulary. Compile authoritative term inventories for English, Russian, Spanish.
close_reason: Complete. canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/emotion.yaml all on disk: 18 EN, 19 RU, 22 ES terms. Ekman 1992 spine + Wierzbicka 1999 NSM augmentations + culture-specific terms (RU тоска/грусть/печаль/жалость/совесть/тревога; ES duende/cariño/añoranza/ilusión). canon-terms/emotion_report.md frames the analytical purpose.

title: Kinship domain literature search
description: Murdock (1949) Social Structure; Greenberg cross-linguistic kinship typology; Kronenfeld. Compile authoritative term inventories for English, Russian, Spanish.
close_reason: Complete. canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/kinship.yaml all on disk. Anchored in Murdock 1949 typology + Friedrich 1964 for Russian affinal terms + Greenberg 1966 universals + Kronenfeld 1996 for step-relations. RU includes the lexicalized husband-side/wife-side affinal distinctions (свёкор/тесть, свекровь/тёща, деверь/шурин, золовка/свояченица) that EN collapses. canon-terms/kinship_report.md explains the typological signal.

title: Editorial pass on textbook overview based on REVIEW.md
description:
Apply non-trivial findings from docs/REVIEW.md to the textbook overview. The trivial fixes (B1 symmetrization, M2 \ref→\autoref, M5 double label, m2 missing citation, m6 unused bib, m8 figure overflow) were applied inline during PR p9f. The remaining editorial findings need Anna's judgment:

MAJOR
- M1: KWIC status contradiction between Ch 6 §6.7 and Ch 9 §9.3.3 — pick the truth and align both.
- M3: Ch 7 runs 7 pages vs 3-5 page target (overlap with Ch 8 is the cause; see M4).
- M4: Ch 7 §§7.10-7.11 duplicate Ch 8 §§8.5-8.6 (bd workflow + repository hygiene). Keep full treatment in Ch 8; reduce Ch 7 to a forward reference.

MINOR
- m1: Ch 4 line 93 conflates threshold-on-weights and Vietoris-Rips filtration directions; add clarifying sentence on the duality.
- m3: Cyrillic markup inconsistency — Ch 1 uses \cyr{...} wrappers, Ch 2 uses bare Cyrillic. \cyr is now a no-op; pick one convention. STYLE.md says bare Cyrillic.
- m4: Ch 5 §5.2 says simplicial complex is "closed under taking faces" without defining "face" — add a parenthetical.
- m5: Ch 6 §6.5 "11–34 per language-domain pair" — clarify the per-domain ranges.
- m7: Ch 5 notation table has W (attention weights) vs W_p (Wasserstein). Add a disambiguation note or use \mathcal{W}_p.
- m9: chapter \autoref renders lowercase ("chapter 4"). Global decision: keep lowercase or add \renewcommand{\chapterautorefname}{Chapter} to the preamble.

Architecture finding not yet addressed:
- Ch 7 has hardcoded beads epic IDs (5f9, ssa, 0cm, bt5, vdh, mwk, p9f) and a directory tree that duplicates CLAUDE.md. Either reframe Ch 7 around concepts rather than enumeration, or add a "regenerate against git ls-tree HEAD before each build" comment.

Files
- docs/REVIEW.md — the canonical punch-list. Apply findings, then update REVIEW.md or delete.
- docs/01-motivation.tex through docs/09-results.tex — chapter files.
- docs/STYLE.md — for any global convention decisions.

Acceptance

- MINOR findings each either resolved or marked "won't fix (reason)".
- `cd docs && make pdf` still produces a clean 61-page (or close) PDF.

title: Add test distinguishing global vs per-layer BH application in permutation_test_per_head
description:
## Summary

`permutation_test_per_head` applies BH/FDR correction globally across all 144 (layer, head) cells. The test suite in `tests/test_diagram_distances_perm.py` does not include a test that would distinguish a correct global BH application from a buggy per-layer or per-head BH application — both would pass current tests.

## Resolution

Add a test that crafts a planted-signal scenario where global and per-layer BH would produce *different* rejection counts (e.g., one layer with 12 strongly-significant heads, eleven layers with all p≈1.0). Assert that the global rejection count matches expectations and differs from what per-layer correction would give.

## Acceptance

- New test in `tests/test_diagram_distances_perm.py` that fails if BH is applied per-layer instead of globally.
- Test runs under K ≥ 2880 (minimum for BH at 144 tests with alpha=0.05 to reject) — gate behind a slow marker if needed.

## Discovered from

blr.3 test-quality review (issue #4).

title: Substrate filename collision in cell-26 leftover dump (phase3 thresholds)
description:
## Summary

The leftover-dump branch in `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` cell 26 (and
its ancestor `replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb`)
uses the same filename formula as the periodic-dump branch:

```python
filename = r_file + "_part" + str(ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)) + "of" + str(number_of_files) + '.npy'
```

In the periodic branch, `i+1` is divisible by `DUMP_SIZE`, so `ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)` produces the right part number. In the leftover branch, however, `i` is at the last batch index — and when `number_of_batches == DUMP_SIZE * k + 1` (i.e., one trailing batch beyond a clean dump boundary), `ceil(i/DUMP_SIZE)` collides with a part number that the periodic branch already wrote.

### Concrete failure case

`DUMP_SIZE=100`, `number_of_batches=101`:
- Periodic dump fires at `i=99` → `ceil(99/100)=1` → writes `_part1of2.npy`.
- Loop continues to `i=100` (last batch). `(i+1) % DUMP_SIZE != 0`, so no
  periodic dump.
- Leftover branch runs with `i=100` → `ceil(100/100)=1` → **overwrites
  `_part1of2.npy`**, losing the periodic dump's data.

### Why it doesn't bite the in-scope inputs

For `en/color` (`number_of_batches=220`, `DUMP_SIZE=100`):
- Dumps fire at `i=99, 199` → parts 1, 2.
- Leftover at `i=219` → `ceil(219/100)=3` → part 3. No collision.

`ru/color` and `es/color` will land at similar magnitudes (n ~ 2k–3k) so the
risk is low for the COSI 115a in-scope runs. But any future input with batch
counts of the form `DUMP_SIZE*k + 1` will silently lose data.

### Discovered

While planning ph-project-16v (final-layer mBERT embedding sidecar emit) —
the same formula was about to be reused for embedding artifacts, doubling
the surface. 16v.2 explicitly preserves the existing buggy formula so part
numbering stays aligned between attention and embedding outputs; this issue
tracks the underlying fix.

## Files affected

- `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` cell 26 (adapted)
- `replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb` (substrate
  copy in the replication tree — this is editable per replication/README.md,
  not the frozen `reference/` tree)

The frozen `reference/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb`
also has this bug, but per CLAUDE.md hard rules it is NEVER edited.

## Suggested fix

Either:
1. Track the dump count explicitly (`current_part_number += 1` at every dump,
   used directly as the filename suffix), OR
2. Compute leftover part number as `ceil((i+1)/DUMP_SIZE)` (note the +1 — same
   as the periodic-branch's effective offset).

Option 1 is more robust and also helps the manifest in 16v.2 (which already
tracks `current_part_number` for the same reason).

Both `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` and the corresponding adapted
ripser+templates notebook (when 16v.2 / mwk.3 land) should adopt the same fix
to stay in sync.

## Acceptance

- The leftover branch in cell 26 produces a part number that does not
  collide with any periodic-dump part for any value of `number_of_batches`.
- A regression test (or a notebook-side assertion) catches the corner case
  where `number_of_batches % DUMP_SIZE == 1`.
- Same fix applied to `replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb`.
- DIVERGES marker on cell 26 updated to note the deviation from the substrate
  formula.

## Priority rationale

P3 (low) — does not affect any in-scope COSI 115a 2026 input. File for the
record so a future contributor with a 1-mod-DUMP_SIZE input doesn't lose
data silently.

title: Document фиолетовый as kept-with-caveat under-target color term
Replication: rework attention persistence to avoid filling disk
description:
ph-project-f93 (running threshold notebook on train + valid splits)
filled the host disk to 100% before completing — to the point where
bash itself stopped initializing. Each subset persists ~4.7GB of
attention tensors (1000 samples × 12 layers × 12 heads × 128² ×
float16) to replication/outputs/attentions/, with three subsets
intended (test already done, train and valid pending).

Two viable rework directions:

A. Stream — modify the threshold pipeline so attention tensors are
   computed on the fly per sample (or per small batch), features
   are extracted, and attention is discarded immediately. Disk only
   needs to hold the (much smaller) feature arrays.

B. Persist with cleanup — keep the persistence pattern from the
   Kushnareva original but add explicit cleanup steps that delete
   per-subset attention files once features are extracted, and
   document the disk budget loudly in the notebook.

Option A is the bigger refactor but cleaner long-term; Option B is
the smaller change. Pick based on how invasive A would be vs. how
many more notebooks (barcode, templates) will hit the same constraint.

Discovered while ph-project-f93 was running. f93 itself is now blocked
on this — re-running the threshold notebook for train + valid will hit
the same wall unless the persistence pattern changes.

Note: 9.4GB of attention data was successfully written for the train
subset before disk filled. Anna manually deleted these to recover.

ARTIFACT TO SALVAGE:
The killed f93 implementer wrote a useful but uncommitted test file:
  /home/anna/ph-project-replication-f93/tests/test_replication_features.py
It parametrizes over train/valid/test and verifies feature .npy
shape (12,12,6,1000,6). When f93 resumes (after this issue lands),
move that file into tests/ as a quality gate. Note it currently
asserts existence so will fail until all three splits have run —
either gate the existence checks behind a pytest.mark.skipif on
file presence, or only enable after the run completes.

title: Wasserstein/bottleneck eval pipeline for cross-linguistic color topology
description:
## Goal

Implement a metric-space evaluation of mBERT attention-graph persistent homology
across English, Russian, and Spanish color vocabulary. Complements the existing
`phase3_comparison.ipynb` (which compares vectorized features) by computing
direct distances on persistence diagrams themselves.

## Scope

- Domain: COLOR only (per CLAUDE.md "Current scope (May 2026)").
- Languages: en, ru, es.
- Source data: `data/phase3/barcodes/{lang}_color_all_heads_12_layers_MAX_LEN_32_bert-base-multilingual-cased_part{1,2,3}of3.json`, produced by `notebooks/mbert_attention_ripser.ipynb`. As of filing, en/part1 exists; full run in progress.
- Library: giotto-tda (already a dep). `gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance` — note that one fit returns BOTH H_0 and H_1 simultaneously; cache is one .npz per metric, with internal dim axis.
- Distances: Wasserstein-2 (primary) + bottleneck (sanity check).
- Dimensions: H_1 (cycles, primary) + H_0 (robustness check).
- Statistics: permutation test (Mantel-style). K=10000. Two granularities — per-domain and per-term.

## Methodological framing

The hypothesis under test is that distributional patterns in language encode
culturally-specific attentional structures visible as measurably different
*topology* in mBERT attention graphs. The existing phase3_comparison answers
"do the feature vectors differ?" This evaluation answers "do the diagram shapes
differ in a metric sense?" — a distinct, complementary claim.

## Russian-blues design

Russian has 12 BCTs vs en/es 11 (sinij/голубой split — Paramei 2005, Winawer
2007). The per-term test runs the triple permutation TWICE (with sinij, with
голубой) AND adds a within-Russian zoom: predict d(sinij, голубой) > median
d(other Russian color pairs). Strongest theoretically-motivated test in the
plan; if it fails, that itself is informative.

**Translation-table join warning:** `canon-terms/ru/color.yaml` glosses for
the two blues are `"dark blue"` (синий) and `"light blue"` (голубой) — NOT
`"blue"`. Naive merge on gloss would silently drop both Russian blues. The
loader uses `_normalize_gloss` to strip "dark "/"light " prefixes. See
subtask blr.4 for the canonical join logic.

## Compute envelope (calibrated 2026-05-05)

n_per_term=30 → ~38h wall-time at n_jobs=-1 (24 cores). Default
`n_per_term=20` (~17h, fits one overnight). Document n=30 option in
`scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py --help` for users with
two-night budgets.

## Deliverables

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` — loader, conversion, distance helpers.
- `scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py` — overnight CLI runner.
- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` — analysis notebook (parallel to phase3_comparison.ipynb).
- `data/phase3/diagram_distances/{wasserstein,bottleneck}.npz` — cached distance tensors, shape `(12, 12, N, N, 2)`.
- `results/phase3_diagram_distances/*.csv` — summary outputs.
- `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_*.{pdf,png}` — figures (mirrors `phase3_comparison.ipynb` convention; single shared figures dir, no per-notebook subdirectory).
- `tests/test_phase3_diagram_distances_outputs.py` — integration test, gated by `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES`.

## Sequencing

Subtasks 1-2 are unblocked now (1 against partial en/part1 data on disk).
Subtasks 3-7 wait on (a) ripser run finishing all 3 langs, and (b) subtask 2
finishing its overnight distance computation. Expect natural session hand-off.

## References

- Kushnareva et al. 2021 EMNLP — substrate methodology (vectorized features).
- Paramei 2005, Winawer et al. 2007 — Russian sinij/голубой split.
- Berlin & Kay 1969 — basic color terms anchor for canon.
- bd ph-project-bcy — фиолетовый under-target casualty (n=104).

title: Diagram loader + giotto-tda format conversion + deterministic subsampling
description:
## Summary

Build the data ingestion layer for the Wasserstein/bottleneck eval. Loads
per-language Kushnareva-format barcode JSONs, attaches metadata (lang, term,
sentence_idx), subsamples deterministically, and converts to the giotto-tda
diagram format expected by `gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance`.

Testable now against `data/phase3/barcodes/en_color_*part1of3.json` — does
not block on the full ripser run.

## On-disk JSON structure (verified 2026-05-05)

The barcode JSONs use **string keys throughout** (json round-trip artifact):

```
{
  "<layer_str>": {        # "0".."11"
    "<head_str>": [        # "0".."11"
      {                    # one dict per sample (1000 per part)
        "0": [[birth, death], ...],   # H_0 features
        "1": [[birth, death], ...]    # H_1 features
      },
      ...
    ]
  }
}
```

Each `partNofM` JSON contains 1000 samples. Three parts per (lang, domain).
The closest reference for re-structuring is `reformat_barcodes` in
`mbert_attention_ripser.ipynb` (~cell after `save_barcodes`).

## Files to modify

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (new file).
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py` (new file).
- `requirements.txt` — verify `giotto-tda` is present (it is); no change expected.

## Files to read for context

- `replication/ripser_helpers.py` — module-style helper pattern to follow.
- `notebooks/mbert_attention_ripser.ipynb` cells around `save_barcodes` (~line 757) — exact JSON structure being saved + the `reformat_barcodes` shape transformation.
- `canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/color.yaml` — canon term lists; loader needs to recognize term identity per language.
- `data/phase3/kwic/` — sample → term association (each sample is one KWIC sentence for a specific term). The ripser notebook consumes KWIC files in a fixed order; loader must replay that order.
- `notebooks/phase3_comparison.ipynb` cells 5-7 — sample-loading conventions (per-(lang, domain) tensors, casting, padding handling).
- `tests/test_phase3_comparison_outputs.py` — env-var-gated test pattern. Note: existing gate is `PH_REQUIRE_COMPARISON`. For this new module, use a parallel name: `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES`.

## Implementation steps

1. Create `replication/diagram_distances.py` with these functions:
   - `load_barcode_json(path) -> dict[tuple[int,int], list[dict[int, np.ndarray]]]`: parses the string-keyed JSON, casts `layer_str`/`head_str`/`dim_str` to ints, converts birth/death lists to `np.float64` arrays of shape `(n_features, 2)`. Output: outer dict keyed by `(layer, head)` tuples, inner list indexed by sample, each sample dict keyed by hom-dim int (`0` or `1`).
   - `load_lang_barcodes(barcode_dir, lang, domain, model_tag='bert-base-multilingual-cased', max_len=32, n_layers=12) -> tuple[dict, pd.DataFrame]`: globs and concatenates all `partNofM` files for `(lang, domain)` in numeric order (part1, part2, part3); validates that the global sample count equals the sum of per-part lengths. Builds metadata DataFrame indexed by global sample_idx with columns `[lang, term, sentence_idx_within_term, source_file, source_part]`. The (term, sentence_idx) mapping comes from KWIC files in `data/phase3/kwic/`, replayed in the same order the ripser notebook consumes them. Document this lookup contract in the docstring.
   - `subsample_per_term(metadata_df, n_per_term=30, seed=42) -> np.ndarray[int]`: returns sample indices to keep. Uses `np.random.default_rng(seed)` for determinism. If a term has fewer than `n_per_term` samples, take all of them and emit a `warnings.warn` (not just print). ru/фиолетовый is the expected case (n=104 < 150 acceptance threshold but kept; see CLAUDE.md "Current scope (May 2026)" and bd ph-project-bcy).
   - `to_giotto_format(per_layer_head_diagrams, sample_indices, layer, head, dims=(0, 1)) -> np.ndarray[float64]`: returns shape `(N, F, 3)` where last axis is `(birth, death, hom_dim)` and F is padded to the max feature count across selected samples × dims. Pad with `(0, 0, hom_dim)` rows — giotto-tda's no-feature convention. The hom_dim padding values must match the dim of real features in that block, not zero, or PairwiseDistance will misclassify them.

2. Add docstrings explaining the substrate JSON layout and giotto-tda expected format.

3. Write `tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py`:
   - Smoke test against `data/phase3/barcodes/en_color_*part1of3.json` if present (gate behind a new `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES` env var, mirroring the `PH_REQUIRE_COMPARISON` pattern in `tests/test_phase3_comparison_outputs.py`).
   - Test `subsample_per_term` is deterministic across calls with the same seed.
   - Test `to_giotto_format` output shape, dtype, and padding-row convention. Specifically test that the padded rows have the correct hom_dim value, not zero.
   - Test that an under-target term (synthetic case with n < n_per_term) produces all available samples + a warning is raised (use `pytest.warns`).

## Why this granularity

This is the foundation for subtasks 2-7. Getting the metadata DataFrame
right (lang, term, sentence_idx) is what the permutation tests later need
to permute — sloppy joins here propagate everywhere. Worth being precise.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py -v` passes.
- `python -m py_compile replication/diagram_distances.py` succeeds.
- Loader handles the partial `en_color_part1of3.json` on disk today without crashing.
- `subsample_per_term` warning triggers on synthetic under-target case.

title: Pairwise distance computation per (layer, head), cached
description:
## Summary

Compute Wasserstein-2 and bottleneck pairwise distance matrices for every
(layer, head) ∈ 12×12, across all 3-language samples. Each `PairwiseDistance.fit`
call returns BOTH H_0 and H_1 in a single pass (so the cache layout has an
internal dim axis, not separate files per dim). Cache to disk so the
downstream permutation tests are cheap re-reads.

This is the overnight job. Designed as a CLI for safe rerun and progress
tracking; should not run inside a notebook.

## Calibration (verified 2026-05-05 by plan reviewer)

On `data/phase3/barcodes/en_color_part1of3.json` layer 0 head 0 with
`gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance(metric='wasserstein', metric_params={'p': 2}, n_jobs=-1)`
on 24 cores: N=400 → 11.4s, N=600 → 23.7s. Per-(layer, head) extrapolation
to N=3000: ~10 min for W_2, ~6 min for bottleneck (single fit per metric
returns both dims). Total for 144 (layer, head) × 2 metrics ≈ **~38h
wall-time at n_per_term=30, n_jobs=-1**.

That blows the "one overnight" assumption. **Default to `n_per_term=20`**,
which scales pair count quadratically: ~17h total at n_jobs=-1 — fits one
night. Document the n=30 option in the script's `--help` for users with
two-night budgets.

## Files to modify

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (extend with computation funcs).
- `scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py` (new CLI runner).
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py` (new file).

## Files to read for context

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (subtask 1's output) — uses `load_lang_barcodes`, `subsample_per_term`, `to_giotto_format`.
- `replication/scripts/` directory if present — follow existing CLI conventions.
- giotto-tda docs / source for `gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance`. Key API note: `homology_dimensions` parameter (default `(0, 1)`) controls which dims are computed; a single fit returns shape `(N, N, n_dims)` with the dim axis last. Setting `metric='wasserstein'` + `metric_params={'p': 2}` gives W_2; `metric='bottleneck'` gives bottleneck.
- `tests/test_mbert_attention_ripser_features.py` — env-var-gated integration test pattern.

## Implementation steps

1. In `replication/diagram_distances.py`, add:
   - `compute_per_head_distances(per_layer_head_diagrams, sample_indices, layer, head, metric, dims=(0, 1), n_jobs=-1) -> np.ndarray[float32]`: extracts diagrams for one (layer, head), passes them through `PairwiseDistance(metric=metric, metric_params=...)` with `homology_dimensions=dims`, returns shape `(N, N, len(dims)) float32`. Cache size note: each tensor is `(12, 12, N, N, 2)` × 4B; at N=600 that's ~166MB per metric, ~330MB total cache — comfortable.
   - `compute_full_distance_tensor(per_layer_head_diagrams, sample_indices, metric, dims=(0, 1), layers=range(12), heads=range(12), progress=True, n_jobs=-1) -> np.ndarray[float32]`: shape `(12, 12, N, N, len(dims))`. Uses tqdm for progress. Logs per-(layer, head) wall time so launching the full sweep can fail fast if calibration drifts.
   - `cache_path(cache_dir, metric) -> Path`: returns `cache_dir / f'{metric}.npz'`. (No `_h{dim}` suffix — both dims live in one file.)
   - `save_distance_tensor(tensor, sample_metadata, dims, path)`: writes `.npz` with keys `tensor` (shape `(12, 12, N, N, len(dims))`), `metadata` (DataFrame as records), `metric` (str), `homology_dimensions` (tuple). Including metadata is what lets downstream subtasks load a tensor without re-deriving the (lang, term) mapping.
   - `load_distance_tensor(path) -> tuple[np.ndarray, pd.DataFrame, str, tuple]`: returns `(tensor, metadata_df, metric, homology_dimensions)`.

2. Create `scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py`:
   - argparse: `--barcode-dir`, `--cache-dir`, `--n-per-term` (default **20**, calibrated for one-overnight at n_jobs=-1), `--seed` (default 42), `--metrics` (default `wasserstein,bottleneck`), `--dims` (default `0,1`), `--n-jobs` (default **-1** — use all cores; document the memory tradeoff).
   - Loads en/ru/es color, subsamples, computes 2 tensors (one per metric, each carrying both dims internally), saves to cache.
   - Skips already-cached metrics unless `--force`.
   - Logs total wall time and per-metric breakdown.
   - First action of the script: assert all expected `data/phase3/barcodes/{en,ru,es}_color_*part3of3.json` exist; abort with a clear error if not (full ripser run not yet complete).

3. Write `tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py`:
   - Unit test: distance matrices are symmetric, zero on diagonal (per dim).
   - Unit test: deterministic given same input + seed.
   - Unit test: output dtype is float32, shape `(12, 12, N, N, 2)` for the (12, 12) layout case.
   - Cache round-trip: save then load returns identical tensor, metadata, metric, dims.
   - Integration test (gated by `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES`): run on a tiny synthetic input set (e.g. 20 samples, 1 layer, 1 head, 1 metric); finishes in <30s.

## Why this granularity

The full computation will run for hours; we want it to be a clean,
restartable CLI we can launch overnight, not a notebook cell. The
"one fit returns both dims" structure halves cache file count and
matches `PairwiseDistance`'s actual API. The cache contract (sample
metadata travels with the tensor) is what lets subtasks 3-6 be
self-contained.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py -v` passes.
- `python scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py --help` runs without error.
- A small smoke run (`--n-per-term 5`, single layer/head, single metric) completes successfully against partial data.
- Wall-time estimate is logged after the smoke run so we know whether the overnight assumption holds for the chosen `--n-per-term` value before launching the full sweep.
- Script aborts with a clear message if the ripser run is incomplete.

title: Per-domain permutation test (between-vs-within language)
description:
## Summary

Headline cross-linguistic test. Builds the new analysis notebook
`notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` and adds the per-domain
permutation test: are between-language attention-topology distances
larger than within-language distances? Runs per-(layer, head) plus
an aggregated head-averaged test.

Uses cached W_2 + H_1 distance tensors from subtask 2 as primary;
H_0 / bottleneck variants are added in subtask 6.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (new file).
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (extend with permutation helpers).
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_perm.py` (new file).

## Files to read for context

- `notebooks/phase3_comparison.ipynb` cells 9-12 — existing permutation-test pattern in this codebase, including FDR correction. Mirror its conventions: imports, `save_fig` helper (cell 9), figure naming (`phase3_*`), CSV layout.
- `notebooks/phase3_comparison.ipynb` cell 9 (`save_fig`) — figures go to `results/figures/{stem}.{pdf,png}` (single dir, no per-notebook subdirectory). Mirror exactly.
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (subtask 1+2 output).

## Implementation steps

1. Build `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` with this structure (parallel to phase3_comparison.ipynb):
   - Cell 0 (md): title + scope statement (color only, hypothesis).
   - Cell 1-2 (md+code): sys.path setup, imports.
   - Cell 3 (md): "Load cached distance tensors".
   - Cell 4 (code): `tensor_w2, meta, metric, hom_dims = load_distance_tensor(CACHE_DIR / 'wasserstein.npz')`. Then `tensor_w2_h1 = tensor_w2[..., hom_dims.index(1)]` to slice the H_1 sub-tensor with shape `(12, 12, N, N)`.
   - Cell 5 (md): "Per-domain permutation test".
   - Cell 6-7 (code): permutation-test helpers + invocation.
   - Cell 8 (md): "Per-(layer, head) effect-size heatmap".
   - Cell 9 (code): figure. Saves to `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_per_domain_effect_heatmap.{pdf,png}` (consistent with `phase3_thresholds_comparison_color.{pdf,png}` pattern in `phase3_comparison.ipynb`).
   - Cell 10 (md): "Save summary CSV".
   - Cell 11 (code): write `results/phase3_diagram_distances/per_domain_perm.csv`.
   - (Cells 12+ reserved for subtasks 4-7 — leave as placeholder markdown stubs.)
   - Reuse the `save_fig` helper from `phase3_comparison.ipynb` cell 9 verbatim — copy that cell into this notebook's setup. Same FIGURES_DIR target.

2. In `replication/diagram_distances.py`, add:
   - `per_domain_test_statistic(distance_matrix, metadata_df) -> float`: computes mean(between-language pair distances) − mean(within-language pair distances). Distance_matrix is shape `(N, N)`; metadata_df has a `lang` column. Use `metadata_df['lang'].values[:, None] != metadata_df['lang'].values[None, :]` for the cross-language mask.
   - `permutation_test_per_domain(distance_matrix, metadata_df, K=10000, seed=42) -> dict`: shuffles language labels K times via `np.random.default_rng(seed).permutation`, returns `{observed: float, null: np.ndarray[K], p_value: float, effect_size: float}`. Two-tailed p-value: `(np.sum(np.abs(null - null.mean()) >= np.abs(observed - null.mean())) + 1) / (K + 1)`. Effect size: `(observed - null.mean()) / null.std()` (z-score under null).
   - `permutation_test_per_head(distance_tensor, metadata_df, K=10000, seed=42) -> pd.DataFrame`: applies the test independently per (layer, head). Returns DataFrame with columns `[layer, head, observed, p_value, effect_size]`. Uses BH FDR correction across the 144 tests at q=0.05; adds `passes_bh` boolean column. Mirror `_bh_correction` in `phase3_comparison.ipynb` cell 11 (verbatim if licensing allows; otherwise re-implement).

3. Notebook cells call these functions and produce:
   - `results/phase3_diagram_distances/per_domain_perm.csv` (one row per (layer, head) plus an aggregate "head-averaged" row).
   - `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_per_domain_effect_heatmap.{pdf,png}` — 12×12 heatmap of effect size, with significant cells outlined.

4. Tests in `tests/test_diagram_distances_perm.py`:
   - Unit test: under H_0 (random distance matrix with random labels), `p_value` is approximately uniform across many seeds — KS test against uniform.
   - Unit test: under a strong planted signal (within-language distances zero, between-language distances large), `p_value < 0.001` and `effect_size > 0`.
   - Unit test: BH correction monotonicity.

## Why this granularity

Per-domain is the cleanest claim and the headline result. Decoupling it
from the per-term test (subtask 4) means the headline can ship even if the
canon-translation-table integration runs into problems. The notebook
structure is laid out fully so subtasks 4-7 can drop their cells in
without re-architecting.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_perm.py -v` passes.
- Notebook executes top-to-bottom (existing cells only) on the cached W_2 tensor.
- Summary CSV and heatmap exist after notebook run, in the documented paths.

title: Per-term permutation test with Russian-blues design
description:
## Summary

Per-term zoom on the cross-linguistic comparison, using the canon
translation triples from `canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/color.yaml`. Includes
the theoretically-motivated Russian-blues sub-test that targets the
sinij/голубой split (Paramei 2005, Winawer et al. 2007) — the cleanest
falsifiable prediction of the underlying hypothesis.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (extend, after subtask 3's cells).
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (extend with translation-triple + per-term helpers).
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_per_term.py` (new file).

## Files to read for context

- `canon-terms/en/color.yaml` — English entries have `term` only (no `gloss`).
- `canon-terms/ru/color.yaml` — Russian entries have `term` (Russian word) and `gloss` (English translation). **Critical:** ru/color.yaml glosses for the two blues are `"dark blue"` (синий) and `"light blue"` (голубой) — not `"blue"`. A naive merge on the gloss field will silently DROP both Russian blues, leaving 10 triples instead of 12.
- `canon-terms/es/color.yaml` — Spanish entries have `term` (Spanish word) and `gloss` (English translation). Glosses match English terms 1:1 for the 11 BCTs.
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (subtasks 1-3 output).
- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (subtask 3 output) — append cells.
- CLAUDE.md "Current scope (May 2026)" — фиолетовый (n=104) context, blue split.

## Implementation steps

1. In `replication/diagram_distances.py`, add:
   - `_normalize_gloss(g: str) -> str`: helper. Returns `g` with `"dark "` and `"light "` prefixes stripped (so `"dark blue"` → `"blue"` and `"light blue"` → `"blue"`). Lowercases for safety. Document explicitly that this is the join key for Russian blues.
   - `load_translation_triples(canon_dir, domain='color') -> pd.DataFrame`: reads all 3 language YAMLs. For each English term, looks up the matching Russian and Spanish entries by `_normalize_gloss(entry['gloss']) == en_term`. If multiple Russian entries match (the blue case: both синий and голубой normalize to `"blue"`), produces ONE ROW PER MATCH — yielding 12 total rows for color (10 1:1:1 + 2 for blue). Columns: `[en_term, ru_term, es_term, ru_gloss_raw]` (the raw gloss preserves "dark blue"/"light blue" for downstream interpretation). Document the duplication explicitly in the docstring with an example.
   - `per_term_test_statistic(distance_matrix, metadata_df, triples_df, ru_blue_choice='синий') -> float`: builds a sample-pair "same-triple" mask. For each row in triples_df, mark all CROSS-LANGUAGE pairs (en, ru), (en, es), (ru, es) where the sample's (lang, term) matches the row's languages and terms. The statistic is the mean distance over those pairs. Within-language pairs are NOT included in the statistic (they're not what "translation cluster" means). `ru_blue_choice` ∈ {'синий', 'голубой'} selects which Russian blue is used for the row whose `en_term == 'blue'`.
   - `permutation_test_per_term(distance_matrix, metadata_df, triples_df, K=10000, seed=42, ru_blue_choice='синий') -> dict`: shuffles term labels WITHIN each language (so cross-language pairs' triple-membership changes; within-language stays unchanged but isn't in the stat anyway). Returns `{observed, null, p_value, effect_size}`. Two-tailed p-value, z-score effect.
   - `russian_blue_zoom(distance_matrix, metadata_df, K=10000, seed=42) -> dict`: Russian-only test on the Russian-language subset of metadata. Statistic: mean d(sample with term=синий, sample with term=голубой) MINUS median over `(term_a, term_b)` pairs where `{term_a, term_b}` is any non-blue Russian color pair, of `mean d(samples with term=term_a, samples with term=term_b)`. Null: shuffle Russian term labels within Russian samples. Returns `{observed, null, p_value, effect_size}`. Document that the prediction is `observed > 0` if the language-mediated split is encoded in mBERT attention topology.

2. Notebook cells (appending to the file from subtask 3):
   - Cell N (md): "Per-term permutation test".
   - Cell N+1 (code): build triples DataFrame, run per-term test twice (`ru_blue_choice='синий'` then `'голубой'`), aggregate over heads (head-averaged distance matrix). Save to `results/phase3_diagram_distances/per_term_perm.csv`.
   - Cell N+2 (md): "Russian-blues zoom" — explain the prediction (Paramei 2005, Winawer 2007) and what we expect.
   - Cell N+3 (code): run `russian_blue_zoom`, plot null distribution with observed line, save figure `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_russian_blue_zoom.{pdf,png}` (using the `save_fig` helper).
   - Cell N+4 (md): interpretation of the Russian-blues result regardless of outcome (positive: language-mediated split is detectable in attention topology; null: it isn't, which is also a real finding given Paramei/Winawer's behavioral evidence).

3. Tests:
   - `_normalize_gloss` strips both "dark " and "light " prefixes; idempotent.
   - `load_translation_triples` produces exactly 12 rows for color (10 single + 2 for blue). Test that BOTH (en=blue, ru=синий, es=azul) AND (en=blue, ru=голубой, es=azul) are present.
   - `per_term_test_statistic` excludes within-language pairs (synthetic test: zero within-language distance, large cross-language → stat reflects only cross).
   - Triple test under H_0 yields ~uniform p-values (KS test).
   - Triple test with planted within-triple proximity yields p < 0.01.
   - `russian_blue_zoom` returns valid p-value structure on synthetic data.

## Why the Russian-blues zoom matters separately

The triple test asks "do translations cluster?" The within-Russian zoom
asks the more specific question: "does mBERT's attention topology
*itself* encode the sinij/голубой distinction the language requires?"
That's a much sharper test of the hypothesis. If both Russian blues
land equally far from English blue, the triple test is mute on whether
the language-internal split is detectable; the zoom is what discriminates.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_per_term.py -v` passes.
- Notebook produces `per_term_perm.csv` and `phase3_diagram_distances_russian_blue_zoom.{pdf,png}`.
- Both Russian-blue triple variants are reported (синий + голубой), not just one.
- Test `load_translation_triples` returns 12 rows for color, including two blue entries.

title: Per-head signal aggregation + 12x12 effect heatmap
description:
## Summary

Identify which mBERT (layer, head) cells carry the strongest cross-linguistic
topology signal. Output is a 12×12 effect-size heatmap, a ranked top-K head
table, and a discussion paragraph cross-referencing Clark et al. 2019 head
specialization (qualitative).

This is the "free finding" the per-(layer, head) compute structure was
designed to expose.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (extend, after subtask 4's cells).
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (extend with aggregation helpers).
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_aggregation.py` (new file — unit tests for the helpers).
- `results/phase3_diagram_distances/top_heads.csv` (output).
- `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_per_head_effect_heatmap.{pdf,png}` (output).

## Files to read for context

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (subtasks 1-4 output).
- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (subtask 3-4 output) — append cells.
- `notebooks/phase3_comparison.ipynb` cell 16 — `_make_comparison_fig` 2-panel figure pattern; mirror the layout style.
- Clark et al. 2019 ("What Does BERT Look At?") — qualitative reference for head specialization. The citation is sufficient unless something specific informs the discussion.

## Implementation steps

1. In `replication/diagram_distances.py`, add:
   - `rank_heads_by_effect(per_head_results_df, top_k=20) -> pd.DataFrame`: from the (layer, head, effect_size, p_value, passes_bh) DataFrame produced by subtask 3, returns the top-K rows by absolute effect size, sorted descending. Adds a `rank` column (1-indexed) and preserves `passes_bh`.
   - `effect_heatmap_data(per_head_results_df) -> np.ndarray[float, (12, 12)]`: pivots the per-head results into a layer × head matrix of effect sizes for plotting. Layers on rows, heads on columns. Handles missing (layer, head) cells by inserting NaN (defensive — should never happen with the pipeline above).

2. Notebook cells (appending to subtask 4's cells):
   - Cell N (md): "Per-head signal aggregation".
   - Cell N+1 (code): rank heads, save `results/phase3_diagram_distances/top_heads.csv` with columns `[rank, layer, head, effect_size, p_value, passes_bh]`. Print top-10 inline for the notebook reader.
   - Cell N+2 (md): "Effect-size heatmap".
   - Cell N+3 (code): plot 12×12 heatmap (layers on Y, heads on X). Use a diverging colormap centered at 0 (e.g. `RdBu_r`). Outline cells where `passes_bh` is True. Save via `save_fig(fig, 'phase3_diagram_distances_per_head_effect_heatmap')` (helper writes to `results/figures/`).
   - Cell N+4 (md): brief interpretive paragraph. Note which layers (early/middle/late) seem to carry the most cross-linguistic signal. Cross-reference Clark et al. 2019 only at the level of "BERT's syntactic specialization is concentrated in middle layers; we observe X in our data" — do not over-claim.

3. Tests in `tests/test_diagram_distances_aggregation.py`:
   - `rank_heads_by_effect`: top_k=5 returns 5 rows; rows are sorted by `|effect_size|` descending; `rank` column is `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`; `passes_bh` is preserved.
   - `effect_heatmap_data`: shape `(12, 12)`; layer/head indexing matches input DataFrame; missing cells (synthetic case) become NaN.

## Why this granularity

This is a pure-aggregation task that consumes subtask 3's output and adds
a small but high-impact piece of the analysis (the "which heads matter"
finding). Splitting it from subtask 3 keeps that subtask focused on the
permutation-test correctness; this one focuses on visualization and
interpretation.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_aggregation.py -v` passes.
- `results/phase3_diagram_distances/top_heads.csv` exists with at least the top 20 heads ranked.
- `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_per_head_effect_heatmap.{pdf,png}` exists.
- Heatmap uses a diverging colormap centered at 0; significant cells visually distinguishable.

title: Bottleneck + H_0 robustness reruns
description:
## Summary

Pure analysis task: re-run the per-domain test (subtask 3), per-term test
(subtask 4), and per-head heatmap (subtask 5) using the bottleneck distance
and the H_0 (connected components) dimension. Reuses cached distance tensors
from subtask 2 — no new computation needed.

The point is robustness, not headline. Wasserstein-2 + H_1 is the primary
pipeline; this subtask verifies the directional findings survive metric
and dimension changes.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (extend, after subtask 5's cells).
- `results/phase3_diagram_distances/robustness_summary.csv` (output).
- `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_robustness_grid.{pdf,png}` (output).

## Files to read for context

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (full state after subtasks 1-5).
- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (full state after subtasks 3-5).
- `data/phase3/diagram_distances/{wasserstein,bottleneck}.npz` (cached tensors; both dims live inside each file as the trailing axis indexed by `homology_dimensions`).

## Implementation steps

1. Notebook cells (appending after subtask 5's cells):
   - Cell N (md): "Robustness: bottleneck and H_0".
   - Cell N+1 (code): for each (metric, dim) ∈ {('wasserstein', 0), ('bottleneck', 0), ('bottleneck', 1)}:
     - Load cached distance tensor: `tensor, meta, _, hom_dims = load_distance_tensor(CACHE_DIR / f'{metric}.npz')`. Slice the dim axis: `subtensor = tensor[..., hom_dims.index(dim)]` → shape `(12, 12, N, N)`.
     - Run `permutation_test_per_domain` (head-averaged) → (p_value, effect_size).
     - Run `permutation_test_per_term` (синий + голубой variants) → 2 p-values, effect sizes.
     - Run `russian_blue_zoom` → (p_value, effect_size).
     - Append rows to `robustness_summary.csv`: `[metric, dim, test, observed, p_value, effect_size]`.
   - Cell N+2 (code): figure showing 2×2 grid (metrics × dims) of per-domain effect sizes for direct comparison. Save via `save_fig(fig, 'phase3_diagram_distances_robustness_grid')`.
   - Cell N+3 (md): interpretation. Specifically call out cases where Wasserstein and bottleneck *disagree* — that disagreement itself is informative (long-tail-of-small-features signal vs. dominant-feature signal).

2. No new code in `replication/diagram_distances.py` — everything reuses subtask 3-5 helpers with different inputs.

3. No new tests — covered by subtask 7's integration test.

## Why this granularity

Analysis-only, no new code paths. Splitting from subtasks 3-5 keeps each
of those focused on its primary metric/dim combo (W_2, H_1) and gives
this task a clear, reportable role: "do the findings survive metric
and dimension changes?"

## Acceptance criteria

- `results/phase3_diagram_distances/robustness_summary.csv` exists with rows for (W_2 H_0), (bottleneck H_0), (bottleneck H_1) × (per-domain, per-term-синий, per-term-голубой, ru-blue-zoom).
- `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_robustness_grid.{pdf,png}` exists.
- Notebook discussion explicitly addresses any metric-disagreement cases.

title: Discussion cell + integration test for diagram-distances notebook
description:
## Summary

Final wrap-up: write the discussion cell tying findings to the underlying
hypothesis, and add an integration test that mirrors the existing
`test_phase3_comparison_outputs.py` pattern to verify all expected
notebook outputs exist after a full run.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (final discussion cell).
- `tests/test_phase3_diagram_distances_outputs.py` (new file).

## Files to read for context

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (full state after subtasks 3-6).
- `tests/test_phase3_comparison_outputs.py` — template for the integration test, including the `PH_REQUIRE_COMPARISON` env-var gate. For this notebook, use the parallel name `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES`.
- CLAUDE.md — re-read the hypothesis statement to ground the discussion cell.

## Implementation steps

1. Notebook discussion cell (final markdown cell):
   - Restate the hypothesis: "distributional patterns in language encode culturally-specific attentional structures that show up as measurably different topology in attention graphs."
   - Summarize the per-domain finding (effect size, significance, head distribution).
   - Summarize the per-term finding (which translation triples cluster, which don't).
   - Summarize the Russian-blues result (the central theoretical prediction).
   - Note the robustness conclusions (does W_2 H_1 generalize across metric/dim?).
   - Acknowledge limitations: subsampling (n_per_term, default 20), фиолетовый under-target (n=104), color-only scope, single model (mBERT).
   - Suggest follow-up: emotion + kinship domains (currently out of scope per CLAUDE.md), other models (XLM-R), other Berlin-Kay-relevant languages.

2. Write `tests/test_phase3_diagram_distances_outputs.py`:
   - Gate behind `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES` env var (mirror the `PH_REQUIRE_COMPARISON` pattern in `tests/test_phase3_comparison_outputs.py`).
   - Verify these files exist after a full notebook run:
     - `data/phase3/diagram_distances/wasserstein.npz`
     - `data/phase3/diagram_distances/bottleneck.npz`
     - `results/phase3_diagram_distances/per_domain_perm.csv`
     - `results/phase3_diagram_distances/per_term_perm.csv`
     - `results/phase3_diagram_distances/top_heads.csv`
     - `results/phase3_diagram_distances/robustness_summary.csv`
     - `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_per_domain_effect_heatmap.{pdf,png}`
     - `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_per_head_effect_heatmap.{pdf,png}`
     - `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_russian_blue_zoom.{pdf,png}`
     - `results/figures/phase3_diagram_distances_robustness_grid.{pdf,png}`
   - Verify CSVs are non-empty and have expected columns.
   - Verify NPZ tensors load and have shape `(12, 12, N, N, 2)` with `N == metadata.shape[0]` and `homology_dimensions == (0, 1)`.

## Why this granularity

The discussion cell is what turns a stack of figures into an answer to
the research question; the integration test guards against output
regressions if any of the upstream subtasks change. Both are small
but load-bearing — separating them from the analytical subtasks 3-6
keeps those focused on their statistical claims.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_phase3_diagram_distances_outputs.py -v` passes (gated by `PH_REQUIRE_DIAGRAM_DISTANCES=1`).
- Final notebook discussion cell exists and addresses all bulleted summary points above.
- All expected output files exist after a full notebook run on real data.

title: Investigate corpus sparsity for ru/* and emotion/kinship domains (5f9.5 surfaced 100 under-target terms)
description:
RESOLUTION FOR COLOR (2026-05-04)
---------------------------------
The COSI 115a final scope was pivoted to the COLOR domain only on
2026-05-04. Color sparsity is being resolved via ph-project-i6q
(re-extract color KWIC from Leipzig 3M news corpora — option B from
the Investigation prompts below). With ~3× more sentences, all but
~1 color term should reach n ≥ 150.

Emotion + kinship sparsity remains UNRESOLVED in the May 2026 study
scope; this issue stays open as a placeholder for follow-up work
after the deadline. If/when the project expands beyond color, the
Wikipedia stratification pass (option A) is the recommended path —
that conversation lived in this issue's planning thread on 2026-05-04
but was descoped due to the May 6 timeline.

Original investigation below
============================

Discovered while running 5f9.5 KWIC extraction across all 9 (lang × domain)
pairs against the Leipzig 1M news corpora. Of the ~186 canon terms total,
**100 are under-target** (n_emitted < 200).

Distribution of under-target terms
----------------------------------
| pair        | n_terms | n_under_target | % under |
|-------------|---------|----------------|---------|
| en/color    | 11      | 2              | 18%     |
| en/emotion  | 18      | 9              | 50%     |
| en/kinship  | 27      | 9              | 33%     |
| ru/color    | 12      | 8              | 67%     |
| ru/emotion  | 19      | 15             | 79%     |
| ru/kinship  | 34      | 25             | 74%     |
| es/color    | 11      | 3              | 27%     |
| es/emotion  | 22      | 10             | 45%     |
| es/kinship  | 32      | 19             | 59%     |

Two clear clusters:

1. **Russian dominates** — every ru/* pair has > 60% under-target. This
   is partly a corpus-genre effect (news skews toward a small set of
   high-frequency lemmas) and partly Russian's morphological richness:
   34 kinship terms includes very fine-grained affinal vocabulary
   (свёкор / шурин / золовка / свояк / свояченица / деверь / сноха etc.)
   that is rare even in large corpora.

2. **Emotion + kinship across all languages** — color is well-attested
   in news (mean ≈ 90% on-target), emotion and kinship are not.
   Affinal kinship terms (in-laws) and stepfamily terms come up on
   the bottom of the list across en, ru, and es. Emotion vocabulary
   is broadly underrepresented in news genre versus, e.g., literary
   or social-media corpora.

Specific zero-hit terms (excluding the homoglyph bug fixed in
ph-project-e1q via term removal, not the originally-proposed homoglyph
fix):
  - ru/kinship: шурин, золовка (after dedup)
  - es/kinship: consuegra (note: consuegro = 3 hits, plural form is rarer)

Investigation prompts
---------------------
A. Is news the wrong genre for emotion + kinship? Leipzig publishes
   mixed-corpus dumps (news + Wikipedia + web). Wikipedia tends to
   cover kinship more (genealogy articles, royal family pages).
B. Would a 3M corpus help? Leipzig has 3M dumps for the major
   languages. 3× more text might pull the borderline terms (those
   with 50-180 corpus hits today) up to or near 200 — but it won't
   help terms with 0-10 hits, which need a different corpus.
   ↳ APPLIED for color via ph-project-i6q.
C. Should we accept the sparsity for some terms? Persistent homology
   is sensitive to per-term sample size; if a term has only 10 KWIC
   sentences, its mBERT attention graph is much noisier than for a
   term with 200. We may need to either (a) drop sub-threshold terms,
   or (b) bootstrap their per-term graphs more carefully.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
- notebooks/phase1_kwic_validation.ipynb (the under-target listing
  and bar charts; rerun on the executed .ipynb to see current data)
- canon-terms/ru/{kinship,emotion}.yaml (the affected canon lists)
- data/kwic/SCHEMA.md (sampling and threshold semantics)

title: Phase 4 — comparison and prediction tests
description:
Statistical comparison of feature profiles cross-linguistically — the pre-registered prediction tests for the topology-on-attention story.

Scope (revised 2026-05-03 to resolve overlap with ph-project-mwk.4):
- Bottleneck and Wasserstein distances between barcodes per (lang x domain) pair.
- The four pre-registered predictions (see Phase 5 writeup epic ph-project-vdh for the prediction list).
- Comparison summary tables and figures specific to the prediction tests.

Out of scope (handled by mwk.4):
- Per-(lang x domain) feature profile aggregation from mwk.3's tensors.
- Mann-Whitney U / permutation tests across language pairs on scalar features.
- Baseline-style comparison figures.

mwk.4 produces the per-cell profiles + scalar stats. bt5 consumes them, adds barcode-distance metrics, and runs the prediction tests against the combined evidence.

Depends on ph-project-mwk (which owns the upstream feature pipeline including mwk.4).

title: Set torch.manual_seed for phase3 reproducibility
description: phase3_thresholds.ipynb sets np.random.seed(42) but does not set torch.manual_seed. Running the notebook twice on the same KWIC CSV could produce slightly different attention weights (and therefore slightly different topology features) due to nondeterministic CUDA kernels. For a comparative study where reproducibility matters, add torch.manual_seed(42) and torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42) in the parameters cell or just before model load. Surfaced by the test-quality review on PR for ph-project-mwk.2.

title: wz1.2 OLD_BARCODE_LOOP pattern is brittle — splits=2 hardcoded
description:
Discovered while setting up wz1.3 verification.

OLD_BARCODE_LOOP in patch_notebook.py (line 241) hardcodes the substring
"number_of_splits = 2\n" as part of the OLD pattern. If anyone bumps
that constant to a different value before running the wz1.2 patch,
the OLD substring no longer matches the live notebook and the patch
silently no-ops on cell 29 (only cell 28 lands).

This actually happened during wz1.3 setup: the parallel
feature/replication-4zo branch had ph-project-xyo commits that bumped
splits 2->20 in the local notebook. When we tried to apply the wz1.2
patch to that local notebook, only the cell-28 patch landed.

Workaround applied for wz1.3: directly swapped cell 29 source to
NEW_BARCODE_LOOP via nbformat. The patch script itself was not updated.

Fix options:
  a) Switch OLD_BARCODE_LOOP from str.replace to re.sub with a more
     permissive regex (allow any int for splits).
  b) Split cell-29 into two patches: normalise splits to a known value
     first, then OLD->NEW.

Either way, add a test that the patch survives a notebook where
splits has been bumped to 20.

title: phase1_kwic.extract: pass loaded matcher into load_canon to avoid double-loading
description:
Summary
-------
The 5f9.4 spec (Implementation steps a/b) instructs the implementer to
load the canon and matcher independently:

    a. terms = load_canon(lang, domain)
    b. matcher = get_matcher(lang)

Following that order verbatim makes load_canon call get_matcher(lang)
internally, then 5f9.4 calls get_matcher(lang) again — yielding two
