  references; update path examples but keep the file name (it's about phase 3
  work generally).

title: Adapt Draganov pipeline to contextual mBERT for per-(lang, term) color topology
description:
## Goal

Build a Draganov-style per-(language, term) point-cloud topology pipeline using the contextualized mBERT embeddings already extracted in `data/phase3/embeddings/`. Provides a second, methodologically independent line of evidence for the cross-linguistic color hypothesis, complementing the Kushnareva attention-graph permutation tests in `replication/diagram_distances.py`.

## Methodological framing

- **Kushnareva (existing, ph-project-blr)**: per-sentence attention-graph PH → distribution of small diagrams → permutation test on within- vs between-language pair distances. Asks: *what is the topology of attention inside each sentence containing a color term?*
- **Draganov-static-fastText (existing, ph-project-1iq)**: per-(lang, region) fastText k-NN cloud → one PD per region → bottleneck/SW/PI/bars_statistics distances. Static-embedding sub-baseline.
- **This epic (Draganov-contextual-mBERT)**: per-(lang, term) cloud of contextualized mBERT embeddings (one point per sentence; mean-pooled over the target color-term WordPiece span) → one PD per (lang, term) → 4-way PD distance grid. Asks: *what is the topology of the cloud of contextualized representations of each color term in each language?*

The three pipelines are complementary, not competing. Together they argue that any cross-linguistic topology effect is robust across (a) attention vs. embedding substrate, and (b) static vs. contextual representation.

## Adaptation faithfulness

This epic is an **adaptation** of the Draganov scripts, not a fresh design. **Be as Draganov-script-faithful as possible.** When the substrate allows it, preserve their script structure, function names, CLI semantics, computational primitives (ripser invocation, distance metric options, bar-stats definitions), and output formats. Diverge only where the substrate genuinely forces it; document each divergence inline.

The two substrate-forced divergences are:

1. **Input I/O**: Draganov reads space-separated `.vec` text files (one row per word type, static fastText). We read a parquet manifest + chunked float16 `.npy` arrays (sentence-level contextual mBERT). No way around this — the data shapes are different.
2. **WP-span pooling**: Draganov has no pooling step (one vector per word type, full stop). We must mean-pool over the target term's WordPiece span per sentence to get a single 768-d point. This step is unique to contextual embeddings; it has no Draganov counterpart.

Everything downstream of the point cloud (distance matrix → ripser → PDs → PD distances) is genuinely portable from their pipeline and should look like a port, not a rewrite.

**Per-subtask faithfulness target:**

- **inu.1 (point clouds)**: substrate-forced divergence. Their `A/raw_to_point_cloud.py` reads SSV; ours reads parquet + npy. The *output* shape (a numpy `(n, d)` matrix per language-or-cell) is the canonical interface — match that exactly so downstream stages are script-portable.
- **inu.2 (persistence diagrams)**: **high faithfulness target.** Their `A/compute_point-cloud_distance_matrix.py` + `2-point_clouds_to_persistence_bars.sh` flow is pointcloud → distance matrix → ripser → bars. Mirror that pipeline. Use the same ripser. Preserve the cosine/euclidean metric option in the API even though we lock cosine.
- **inu.3 (PD distances)**: **high faithfulness target.** Their `B-from_persistence_diagrams_to_language_distances/run_compute_pd_distances.py` is the canonical reference; preserve function names, distance-notion choices, and on-disk output format (`.npz` per distance × dim, or whatever they use) where possible.
- **inu.4 (notebook analysis)**: low faithfulness. Their C-step (tree construction across 81 Indo-European languages) doesn't apply at 3 languages; our analysis (34×34 grid + Russian-blues zoom) is bespoke.

When in doubt, mirror their script structure and function names. The closer our intermediate artifacts look to theirs, the more our results can be cross-checked against the static-fastText sub-baseline (`ph-project-1iq`) and any future Draganov-corpus replication.

## Russian-blues design

Russian obligatorily splits blue into синий (dark, n=200) and голубой (light, n=200) — the central cross-linguistic deviation in this study (Paramei 2005, Winawer 2007). Treating each as its own (lang, term) cell lets us ask: is d(en/blue, ru/синий) ≠ d(en/blue, ru/голубой)? Is the синий–голубой within-language distance comparable to between-language distances? Symmetric questions for es/azul. The 34×34 distance grid surfaces these directly.

Three of 34 cells are below the n≥200 target: ru/фиолетовый (n=104; documented under-target per CLAUDE.md scope and bd ph-project-bcy), ru/коричневый (n=163), and es/marrón (n=161). All four cells in the Russian-blues comparison (en/blue, es/azul, ru/синий, ru/голубой) are at full n=200, so the centerpiece test is unaffected; surrounding-grid distances will be slightly noisier for the three undertarget cells. 34 cells total: 11 (en) + 12 (ru) + 11 (es).

## Architecture

```
draganov_replication/                         # parallel to replication/ (Kushnareva-adapted)
├── README.md
├── pointclouds.py     # build_pointclouds(...)
├── diagrams.py        # compute_diagrams(...)
├── pd_distances.py    # compute_pd_distance_grid(...)
└── notebooks/
    └── draganov_color_per_term.ipynb        # end-to-end runner + analysis

data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/{lang}_{term}.npy            # float32, (n_samples, 768)
data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/manifest.csv                 # (lang, term, n_samples, file)
data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/{lang}_{term}.npz               # H_0 + H_1 arrays
data/phase3/draganov_pd_distances/{distance}_d{dim}.npz       # 34×34, 8 files total
```

Library + notebook (no CLIs — total compute is <10 minutes; argparse boilerplate adds nothing). Pattern matches `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` + `baselines/topology.py`.

The `to_giotto_format` helper used for the bottleneck distance path is shared between this pipeline and `replication/diagram_distances.py`; it is extracted as `replication/giotto_format.py` in subtask `ph-project-inu.5` (a dependency of `ph-project-inu.3`) to avoid duplication of its non-obvious padding-row contract.

## Decisions locked at planning time (2026-05-05)

- **Pooling**: mean over `target_wp_start:target_wp_end` (manifest columns).
- **Point-cloud distance metric**: cosine (single metric; fits embedding-space convention and existing `baselines.distances.cosine_distance_matrix`).
- **max_dim**: 1 (H_0 + H_1; matches `replication/diagram_distances.py` default).
- **PD distance notions**: all four — bottleneck, sliced_wasserstein, persistence_image, bars_statistics.
- **Layer**: final (the only layer cached as of 2026-05-05).

## Compute envelope

- 34 (lang, term) cells × cosine ripser at N≈100–200, max_dim=1: ~1 minute total.
- 34×34 = 1156 cell-pairs × 4 distances × 2 dims: bottleneck dominates, but at this scale single-digit minutes.
- Total end-to-end: <10 minutes. No overnight budget needed.

## Out of scope

- Emotion and kinship domains (paused per May 4 decision; CLAUDE.md "Current scope (May 2026)").
- All-12-layer embeddings (only final layer is currently cached).
- Tree construction / language-family evaluation (Draganov C-step). Doesn't apply at 3 languages.
- fastText comparison (covered by ph-project-1iq).
- CLI shims (drop unless an overnight run actually emerges).
- Permutation tests on PD distances (single PD per cell; would need a different framing).

## Deliverables

- Three library modules + one analysis notebook under `draganov_replication/`.
- A shared `replication/giotto_format.py` helper extracted from the existing `replication/diagram_distances.py`.
- Cached point clouds, diagrams, and PD-distance matrices under `data/phase3/draganov_*/`.
- Analysis CSVs in `results/draganov_replication/`.
- Figures in `results/figures/draganov_replication_*.{pdf,png}` (single shared figures dir convention; matches `phase3_diagram_distances`).
- Tests in `tests/test_draganov_replication_*.py` and `tests/test_giotto_format.py`.

## Sequencing

- `inu.5` (extract `to_giotto_format`) is independent of `inu.1` and can land in parallel.
- `inu.1` → `inu.2` → `inu.3` → `inu.4` is the linear chain on the draganov_replication side.
- `inu.3` also depends on `inu.5` (uses the extracted helper).

Each subtask is small (~1–3 production files modified, ~5–8 read for context).

## Hard rules

- **Be as Draganov-script-faithful as possible.** When in doubt, mirror their script structure, function names, and output formats. Diverge only where the substrate (contextual mBERT vs static fastText) genuinely forces it; document each divergence inline. See "Adaptation faithfulness" above for per-subtask targets.
- Do NOT edit `draganov/` (frozen upstream clone). It is fine — and encouraged — to *read* code there for reference; reimplement small functions in our tree rather than importing.
- Do NOT edit `reference/` (frozen Kushnareva original; CLAUDE.md hard rule).

## References

- Draganov & Skiena (2024) — "The Shape of Word Embeddings: Quantifying Non-Isometry With Topological Data Analysis", Findings of EMNLP 2024.
- Kushnareva et al. (2021) EMNLP — substrate methodology.
- Berlin & Kay (1969) — color BCT anchor.
- Paramei (2005), Winawer et al. (2007) — Russian синий/голубой split.
- bd ph-project-bcy — фиолетовый under-target (n=104, kept).
- bd ph-project-blr — Kushnareva attention-graph PD pipeline.
- bd ph-project-1iq — Draganov-style static-fastText sub-baseline.

title: Build per-(lang, term) point clouds + draganov_replication README
description:
## Summary

Convert existing mBERT contextual embeddings into 34 per-(lang, term) point clouds, one `.npy` per (language, color term). Each row is the mean-pooled embedding of the target color-term WordPieces in one sentence. Also write the `draganov_replication/README.md` and a manifest CSV.

## Files to create

- `draganov_replication/__init__.py` (empty)
- `draganov_replication/pointclouds.py`
- `draganov_replication/README.md`
- `tests/test_draganov_replication_pointclouds.py`

## Files to read for context

- `data/phase3/embeddings/{en,ru,es}_color_manifest.parquet` — inspect columns, especially `target_wp_start`, `target_wp_end`, `embedding_part`, `embedding_offset`, `term`.
- `data/phase3/embeddings/{en,ru,es}_color_final_layer_MAX_LEN_32_bert-base-multilingual-cased_part{1,2,3}of3.npy` — shape `(N_part, 32, 768)`, dtype `float16`. Always `mmap_mode='r'`.
- `canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/color.yaml` — authoritative term lists (11 + 12 + 11 = 34 cells).
- `canon-terms/SCHEMA.md` — canon-terms format.
- `CLAUDE.md` — "Current scope (May 2026)" (фиолетовый kept at n=104). Two other cells are also undertarget but kept: ru/коричневый (n=163), es/marrón (n=161).
- `tests/` — search for existing `PH_REQUIRE_*` env-gated tests (e.g. `PH_REQUIRE_EMBEDDINGS`) to follow the per-stage skip-when-data-missing convention.

## Implementation steps

1. **Public API** in `draganov_replication/pointclouds.py`:

   ```python
   def build_pointclouds(
       emb_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("data/phase3/embeddings"),
       canon_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("canon-terms"),
       out_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds"),
       langs: tuple[str, ...] = ("en", "ru", "es"),
       domain: str = "color",
       overwrite: bool = False,
   ) -> pd.DataFrame:
       """Build per-(lang, term) contextual point clouds from existing mBERT embeddings.

       Returns the manifest DataFrame (also written to out_dir/'manifest.csv').
       Skips work for cells whose .npy file already exists; pass overwrite=True
       to force regeneration.
       """
   ```

2. **Per language**:
   a. Load `{emb_dir}/{lang}_{domain}_manifest.parquet`.
   b. Load `canon-terms/{lang}/{domain}.yaml`. Extract the `terms[].term` list. Assert each canon term appears in `manifest['term'].unique()`. Warn if any manifest term isn't in the canon (include it but flag).
   c. For each canon term:
      - Filter manifest to rows where `term == canon_term`.
      - Group rows by `embedding_part`. For each part, mmap-open `{lang}_{domain}_final_layer_*_part{P}of3.npy`, gather rows by `embedding_offset`, slice `target_wp_start:target_wp_end` along the WordPiece axis, mean-pool to a 768-d vector. Cast float32.
      - Concatenate all rows in manifest order. Result: `(n_term_samples, 768)` float32 array.
      - Save to `out_dir / f"{lang}_{term}.npy"`.

3. **Manifest CSV** (`out_dir/manifest.csv`): columns = `[lang, term, n_samples, file]`, sorted by `(lang, term)`. Cyrillic chars in filenames are fine (filesystem supports them; matches канон conventions in `canon-terms/`).

4. **README** (`draganov_replication/README.md`): one paragraph each on:
   - What this directory is (Draganov-style contextual mBERT topology).
   - Relation to `replication/` (Kushnareva-adapted) and `draganov/` (frozen upstream).
   - The three modules + notebook layout.
   - Pointer to epic (`bd ph-project-inu`) and the two sibling epics (`bd ph-project-blr`, `bd ph-project-1iq`).

5. **Tests** in `tests/test_draganov_replication_pointclouds.py`. Real-data tests are gated by env var `PH_REQUIRE_DRAGANOV_POINTCLOUDS=1` (matches the existing per-stage convention `PH_REQUIRE_EMBEDDINGS`, `PH_REQUIRE_FEATURES`, etc.). Default behavior: skip when `data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/` is empty.
   - `test_canon_term_coverage`: every canon term in `canon-terms/{lang}/color.yaml` produces a `.npy` file.
   - `test_pointcloud_shape`: each `.npy` has 2D shape, dtype `float32`, `shape[1] == 768`, `shape[0] == manifest.csv n_samples`.
   - `test_furiolet_kept`: `ru_фиолетовый.npy` exists with `n >= 100`.
   - `test_russian_blues_present`: all four centerpiece cells exist with `n >= 150`: `en_blue.npy`, `es_azul.npy`, `ru_синий.npy`, `ru_голубой.npy`. Verifies the central design survives the pipeline before downstream tasks consume it.
   - `test_pooling_correctness`: small synthetic fixture (mock 1 manifest row, 1 small embedding npy with known WPs) — verify the saved vector equals `np.mean(emb[i, ws:we, :], axis=0)`. Pure-Python; not gated.

## Acceptance

- 34 `.npy` files written to `data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/`.
- `manifest.csv` lists all 34 cells with non-zero `n_samples`.
- `pytest tests/test_draganov_replication_pointclouds.py` green when `PH_REQUIRE_DRAGANOV_POINTCLOUDS=1` and the cache is built; the synthetic test passes regardless.
- `python -m py_compile draganov_replication/pointclouds.py` clean.
- README readable and links to the three epics.

## Example

```python
>>> from draganov_replication.pointclouds import build_pointclouds
>>> manifest = build_pointclouds()
>>> manifest.head()
  lang   term  n_samples                              file
0   en  black        200  data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/en_black.npy
...
>>> import numpy as np
>>> arr = np.load("data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/ru_синий.npy")
>>> arr.shape, arr.dtype
((200, 768), dtype('float32'))
```

title: Compute persistence diagrams per (lang, term) cell
description:
## Summary

Compute Vietoris–Rips persistence diagrams (H_0 + H_1) for each of the 34 per-(lang, term) point clouds produced by `ph-project-inu.1`. Cosine distance metric, max_dim=1. Cache one `.npz` per cell.

This module is an **adaptation of Draganov's A-step pipeline** to our contextual-mBERT substrate. See "Draganov correspondence" below.

## Draganov correspondence

Their A-step (`draganov/A-word_embeddings_to_persistence_diagrams/`) splits the work across three scripts. We collapse them into a single `compute_diagrams()` call because everything is in-process numpy:

| Draganov script | Their action | Our equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `compute_point-cloud_distance_matrix.py --metric cosine` | point cloud → lower-triangular cosine distance SSV | `baselines.distances.cosine_distance_matrix(X)` (returns dense symmetric matrix in memory) |
| `2-point_clouds_to_persistence_bars.sh` (calls `./ripser/ripser`) | distance matrix → ripser bars (stdout text) | `baselines.topology.rips_barcode(D, max_dim=1)` — uses the `ripser` Python package, which wraps the **same C++ ripser binary** Draganov invokes directly. The topology computation is identical; only the invocation surface differs. |
| `ripser_output_to_bars.py` | parse ripser stdout → numpy arrays | handled inside `rips_barcode` (returns structured arrays directly) |

**Faithfulness target: high** — the math is a direct port. The only divergence is in-process numpy vs three-stage shell pipeline, which is purely an ergonomics call and does not change output. Our `.npz` per cell mirrors what Draganov stores at the end of `2-point_clouds_to_persistence_bars.sh`.

**Metric**: locked to cosine to match Draganov's choice for fastText (`--metric cosine`). We do not expose euclidean as an alternative — the choice is locked at planning time, not a configurable knob, since 768-d mBERT embeddings should be compared in cosine space for the same reasons their fastText vectors were.

Reimplement the small functions in our tree; do NOT import or edit `draganov/`.

## Files to create

- `draganov_replication/diagrams.py`
- `tests/test_draganov_replication_diagrams.py`

## Files to read for context

- `draganov_replication/pointclouds.py` (output format from `inu.1`).
- `data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/manifest.csv` (input list of 34 cells).
- `baselines/topology.py` — use `from baselines.topology import rips_barcode`. Read its docstring for the structured-array return format.
- `baselines/distances.py` — use `from baselines.distances import cosine_distance_matrix`.
- `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` — reference usage of `rips_barcode` + `cosine_distance_matrix`.
- `draganov/A-word_embeddings_to_persistence_diagrams/compute_point-cloud_distance_matrix.py` — Draganov's distance-matrix script (read for reference; do NOT import).
- `draganov/A-word_embeddings_to_persistence_diagrams/ripser_output_to_bars.py` — their bars-parsing script (read for reference).

## Implementation steps

1. **Public API**:

   ```python
   def compute_diagrams(
       pointcloud_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds"),
       out_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("data/phase3/draganov_diagrams"),
       max_dim: int = 1,
       overwrite: bool = False,
   ) -> pd.DataFrame:
       """Compute persistence diagrams for each (lang, term) cell.

       Reads {pointcloud_dir}/manifest.csv, loads each .npy, computes cosine
       distance matrix via baselines.distances.cosine_distance_matrix, runs
       baselines.topology.rips_barcode at max_dim, saves an .npz per cell
       with arrays `h0` and `h1` (each shape (n_features, 2), float64).

       Returns a summary DataFrame with columns [lang, term, n_h0, n_h1, file]
       (also written to out_dir/'manifest.csv'). Skips cells whose .npz
       already exists unless overwrite=True.

       Adapted from Draganov A-step (compute_point-cloud_distance_matrix.py +
       2-point_clouds_to_persistence_bars.sh + ripser_output_to_bars.py).
       """
   ```

2. **Per cell** (mirrors their three-stage pipeline in-process):
   - Load `{lang}_{term}.npy` (float32, shape (N, 768)).
   - `D = cosine_distance_matrix(X)` — symmetric, zero-diagonal, in [0, 2]. (← their `compute_point-cloud_distance_matrix.py --metric cosine`.)
   - `bc = rips_barcode(D, max_dim=max_dim)` — returns `{0: structured_array, 1: structured_array}` with `'birth'` and `'death'` fields. Infinite bars are already stripped. (← their `./ripser/ripser` + `ripser_output_to_bars.py`.)
   - Convert each structured array to plain `(n, 2)` float64: `np.column_stack([sa["birth"], sa["death"]])`.
   - `np.savez(out_dir / f"{lang}_{term}.npz", h0=h0, h1=h1, lang=lang, term=term, n_samples=N)`.

3. **Tests** (`tests/test_draganov_replication_diagrams.py`). Real-data tests gated by `PH_REQUIRE_DRAGANOV_DIAGRAMS=1`; synthetic tests are not gated.
   - `test_diagrams_shape`: each `.npz` has `h0` and `h1` keys; both 2D, shape `(n, 2)`, float64; `n` may be 0.
   - `test_h0_no_inf`: assert no `np.isinf` values in any `h0` or `h1` array.
   - `test_birth_le_death`: every `(b, d)` row satisfies `b <= d`.
   - `test_canon_term_coverage`: 34 `.npz` files match the 34 entries in pointclouds manifest.
   - `test_cells_match_pointcloud_manifest`: the `(lang, term)` set in `data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/manifest.csv` exactly matches the set in `data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/manifest.csv` (no skew across the two caches).
   - `test_synthetic_pointcloud`: build a small 10-point cloud (e.g., points on a circle), run `compute_diagrams` on a temp dir, verify H_1 has at least one feature with non-trivial persistence. Not gated.

## Acceptance

- 34 `.npz` files written to `data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/`.
- `manifest.csv` lists per-cell H_0 and H_1 feature counts.
- Total runtime <2 minutes on north (single-threaded ripser at N≈100–200).
- `pytest tests/test_draganov_replication_diagrams.py` green when `PH_REQUIRE_DRAGANOV_DIAGRAMS=1` and the cache is built; synthetic tests pass regardless.
- `python -m py_compile draganov_replication/diagrams.py` clean.
- Module docstring documents the Draganov-A-step correspondence (the table above, summarised).

## Example

```python
>>> from draganov_replication.diagrams import compute_diagrams
>>> summary = compute_diagrams()
>>> summary.head()
  lang   term  n_h0  n_h1                              file
0   en  black   199    47  data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/en_black.npz
...
>>> import numpy as np
>>> d = np.load("data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/ru_синий.npz")
>>> d["h0"].shape, d["h1"].shape
((199, 2), (52, 2))
```

title: Compute 4-way PD-distance grids across (lang, term) cells
description:
## Summary

Compute pairwise persistence-diagram distances between every pair of (lang, term) cells, for each of four distance notions (bottleneck, sliced_wasserstein, persistence_image, bars_statistics) × two homology dims (H_0, H_1). Produces eight 34×34 distance matrices.

This module is a **direct port of Draganov's B-step** (`run_compute_pd_distances.py`) — the most script-faithful subtask in the epic. Function names, distance-notion choices, and vectorisation math should mirror Draganov as closely as the local environment allows. See "Draganov correspondence" below.

## Draganov correspondence

| Draganov function | Source | Our equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| `compare_pds(pd_a, pd_b, distance_name)` | `run_compute_pd_distances.py:111-115` | `compute_pd_distance_grid(...)` (vectorised over the 34×34 grid; per-pair semantics preserved) |
| `vectorise_persistence_image(pd, dim)` | `run_compute_pd_distances.py:59-69` | `vectorise_persistence_image(pd, dim)` — same name, same math, recalibrated `birth_range`/`pers_range` for mBERT cosine distances (see calibration note below) |
| `vectorise_bars_statistics(pd, only_death)` | `run_compute_pd_distances.py:80-108` | `vectorise_bars_statistics(pd, only_death)` — same name, identical 10-statistic vector (mean, std, median, IQR, range, 10/25/75/90 pct, entropy); 10-d for `only_death=True`, 40-d otherwise |
| Bottleneck distance via `gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance` | `run_compute_pd_distances.py` (bottleneck branch) | same library; goes through the shared `replication.giotto_format.to_giotto_format` helper extracted by `inu.5` |
| Sliced Wasserstein via `persim.sliced_wasserstein(M=50)` | `run_compute_pd_distances.py` | same library + same `M=50` parameter |
| Output: per-(distance, dim) matrix on disk | `merge_pd_distances_to_matrix.py` writes the 34×34 (their N=81×81) matrix | `np.savez(out_dir / f'{distance}_d{dim}.npz', matrix, cells, distance, dim)` — same shape, lighter format |

**Faithfulness target: high.** The four distance notions and the two vectorisation helpers should be ported verbatim from `run_compute_pd_distances.py`. Preserve their function names so a reader can diff our module against theirs and see only substrate-driven changes (calibration ranges) and ergonomic ones (in-process numpy vs distributed shell jobs).

The two substrate-driven divergences:

1. **Persistence-image calibration ranges** (see calibration note in step 2). Their ranges are tuned to fastText-300d cosine; ours are tuned to mBERT-768d cosine. The math is the same; only the numerical ranges differ.
2. **Distributed runner**: their `run_compute_pd_distances_distributed.py` parallelises across pairs because their N=81 (3240 pairs per matrix). At our N=34 (561 pairs), single-process is <10 minutes total — distribution is unnecessary overhead.

Reimplement the small functions in our tree; do NOT import or edit `draganov/`.

## Dependencies

- `ph-project-inu.2` (per-cell diagrams must exist).
- `ph-project-inu.5` (the shared `to_giotto_format` helper at `replication/giotto_format.py` must exist; the bottleneck path imports it).

## Files to create

- `draganov_replication/pd_distances.py`
- `tests/test_draganov_replication_pd_distances.py`

## Files to read for context

- `draganov_replication/diagrams.py` — output `.npz` format from `inu.2`.
- `data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/manifest.csv` — list of 34 cells.
- `replication/giotto_format.py` — shared `to_giotto_format` helper landed by `inu.5`. Import this; do NOT reimplement the giotto-tda packing.
- `draganov/B-from_persistence_diagrams_to_language_distances/run_compute_pd_distances.py` — **canonical reference for this module.** Read for `vectorise_persistence_image` (lines 59–69), `vectorise_bars_statistics` (lines 80–108), `compare_pds` (lines 111–115). Do NOT import or edit `draganov/`.
- `draganov/B-from_persistence_diagrams_to_language_distances/merge_pd_distances_to_matrix.py` — their matrix-assembly script (read for output-format reference).
- `replication/diagram_distances.py:541–560` — usage pattern for `gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance(metric='bottleneck', order=None)` and the symmetrisation step.
- `persim` package: `persim.sliced_wasserstein(pd_a, pd_b, M=50)`, `persim.PersistenceImager`.

## Implementation steps

1. **Public API**:

   ```python
   def compute_pd_distance_grid(
       diagrams_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("data/phase3/draganov_diagrams"),
       out_dir: pathlib.Path = pathlib.Path("data/phase3/draganov_pd_distances"),
       distances: tuple[str, ...] = (
           "bottleneck",
           "sliced_wasserstein",
           "persistence_image",
           "bars_statistics",
       ),
       dims: tuple[int, ...] = (0, 1),
       overwrite: bool = False,
   ) -> dict[tuple[str, int], np.ndarray]:
       """Produce 34×34 PD distance matrices for each (distance, dim).

       Adapted from draganov/B-from_persistence_diagrams_to_language_distances/
       run_compute_pd_distances.py (function names and math preserved).

       Loads all diagram .npz files in (lang, term)-sorted order, then for each
       (distance, dim) computes the matrix and writes it to
       out_dir/f'{distance}_d{dim}.npz' alongside the cell labels.

       If out_dir/f'{distance}_d{dim}.npz' exists and overwrite=False, the
       cached matrix is loaded from disk and skipped. The returned dict is
       identical regardless of cache hit vs miss; callers always get a
       (34, 34) float32 matrix per (distance, dim).
       """
   ```

2. **Vectorisation helpers** (port the math from Draganov; preserve their function names and signatures so the diff against `run_compute_pd_distances.py` is minimal):

   ```python
   def vectorise_bars_statistics(pd: np.ndarray, only_death: bool = False) -> np.ndarray:
       """Reproduces draganov/B run_compute_pd_distances.py:80-108.

       10 statistics (mean, std, median, IQR, range, 10/25/75/90th pct, entropy)
       over either just deaths (only_death=True, used for dim 0) or all four
       quantities (deaths, births, lifespans, midpoints; used for dim>=1).
       Output length: 10 (only_death) or 40 (else).
       """

   def vectorise_persistence_image(pd: np.ndarray, dim: int) -> np.ndarray:
       """Reproduces draganov/B run_compute_pd_distances.py:59-69.

       Vectorises via persim.PersistenceImager and flattens to a 1-D vector.
       Birth/persistence ranges recalibrated for mBERT cosine distances; see
       module docstring.
       """
   ```

   **Calibration note for `vectorise_persistence_image`**: Draganov's cosine-metric ranges (lines 156–162 of `run_compute_pd_distances.py`) are calibrated for fastText 300d cosine distances, where H_0 deaths are tiny and `birth_range=(0, 0.1)` makes sense. Our point clouds are mBERT 768d cosine, where H_0 deaths span much of [0, 1]. Default to `birth_range=(0, 1)` and `pers_range=(0, 1)` for both dim 0 and dim 1 (sigma=0.1, pixel_size=0.1) and verify empirically by inspecting `data/phase3/draganov_diagrams/*.npz` H_0 death distributions. If actual deaths cluster < 0.1, fall back to Draganov's narrower range — but the default should match where the data actually lives, not Draganov's calibration. Document the chosen ranges (and their delta from Draganov's) in the function's docstring.

3. **Distance computation per (distance, dim)**:
   - Load all 34 PDs for that dim into a list (order: sorted by `(lang, term)` from the diagrams manifest).
   - **bottleneck**: pack PDs into giotto-tda format using the shared `to_giotto_format` from `replication/giotto_format.py`. The `(0, 0, hom_dim)` padding contract is safe here because `rips_barcode` (used by `inu.2`) strips infinite H_0 bars before saving — no real bar has birth=death=0, so padding rows do not collide with real features. Call `PairwiseDistance(metric='bottleneck', order=None).fit_transform(...)`, slice for the requested dim, symmetrise via `0.5 * (M + M.T)`.
   - **sliced_wasserstein**: nested loop with `persim.sliced_wasserstein(pd_i, pd_j, M=50)` for `i < j`; mirror to `M[j, i]`. (M=50 matches Draganov.)
   - **persistence_image**: vectorise each PD once, then pairwise L2 distance on the matrix of vectors. (Matches Draganov's `compare_pds` semantics for this branch.)
   - **bars_statistics**: same pattern as persistence_image. Use `only_death=True` for dim=0. (Matches Draganov.)

4. **Caching**: `np.savez(out_dir / f'{distance}_d{dim}.npz', matrix=M.astype(np.float32), cells=cell_labels, distance=distance, dim=dim)` where `cell_labels` is a length-34 unicode array of `f"{lang}/{term}"`.

5. **Tests** (`tests/test_draganov_replication_pd_distances.py`). Real-data tests gated by `PH_REQUIRE_DRAGANOV_PD_DISTANCES=1`; synthetic tests are not gated.
   - `test_grid_shape`: each of the 8 output matrices is shape (34, 34), float32.
   - `test_symmetric`: `np.allclose(M, M.T, atol=1e-5)`.
   - `test_diagonal_zero`: `np.allclose(np.diag(M), 0, atol=1e-5)`.
   - `test_finite`: no `inf` or `nan` in any matrix.
   - `test_cells_match_pointcloud_manifest`: the `cells` array in each `.npz` agrees with `data/phase3/draganov_pointclouds/manifest.csv` order.
   - `test_bars_statistics_vector_shape`: synthetic 5-feature PD → vector length 40 (dim ≥ 1) or 10 (dim 0 with `only_death=True`). Not gated.
   - `test_persistence_image_smoke`: synthetic 5-feature PD vectorises to a flat float vector without raising. Not gated.

## Acceptance

- 8 `.npz` files in `data/phase3/draganov_pd_distances/`.
- All matrices symmetric, zero-diagonal, finite, shape (34, 34).
- `pytest tests/test_draganov_replication_pd_distances.py` green when `PH_REQUIRE_DRAGANOV_PD_DISTANCES=1` and the cache is built; synthetic tests pass regardless.
- `python -m py_compile draganov_replication/pd_distances.py` clean.
- Total runtime <10 minutes (bottleneck dominates).
- `vectorise_persistence_image` docstring documents the chosen birth/persistence ranges and the empirical-calibration delta from Draganov's defaults.
- Module docstring documents the per-function correspondence with `run_compute_pd_distances.py`.

## Note on draganov/ frozenness

`draganov/` is upstream code that should not be edited. It is fine — and explicitly encouraged for this subtask — to *read* it line-by-line as the canonical reference. Do not import from `draganov/`; reimplement the small functions in our tree, preserving their names and signatures so the line-by-line correspondence is auditable.

title: Analysis notebook + figures for Draganov contextual mBERT pipeline
description:
## Summary

End-to-end runner and analysis for the Draganov-contextual-mBERT pipeline. Calls subtasks 1–3 to populate caches (no-op if already cached), then produces the analysis figures and CSV outputs. Centerpiece: the Russian-blues zoom test.

## Adaptation faithfulness note

Unlike `inu.2` and `inu.3`, this subtask is **bespoke, not a port**. Draganov's C-step (`draganov/C-tree_construction_and_evaluation/`) builds a phylogenetic-style tree across 81 Indo-European languages and evaluates it against gold language families. At our N=3 languages × 11–12 terms = 34 cells, tree construction is meaningless and family evaluation has no comparison set. Our analysis instead asks the per-(lang, term) cross-linguistic comparison the epic was designed for: the 34×34 distance grid, the Russian-blues zoom, and a cross-distance consistency check.

For style consistency, follow the figure conventions in `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` (the Kushnareva-pipeline analysis notebook) — that's where our project's per-figure conventions live. Do not try to mirror the Draganov C-step notebooks (`4-construct_and_evaluate_trees.ipynb`, `5-summary_figures.ipynb`); they answer a different question.

## Files to create

- `draganov_replication/notebooks/draganov_color_per_term.ipynb`
- (writes) `results/draganov_replication/per_term_cross_language_ranking.csv`
- (writes) `results/draganov_replication/russian_blues_distances.csv`
- (writes) `results/figures/draganov_replication_heatmap_grid.{pdf,png}`
- (writes) `results/figures/draganov_replication_russian_blues_zoom.{pdf,png}`
- (writes) `results/figures/draganov_replication_distance_consistency.{pdf,png}`

## Files to read for context

- `draganov_replication/pointclouds.py`, `diagrams.py`, `pd_distances.py` (the three library modules from inu.1–3).
- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` — figure conventions (figsize, DPI, file naming, CSV write pattern). **This is the style anchor for this notebook.**
- `notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb` — notebook style for this project.
- `canon-terms/ru/color.yaml` — gloss conventions (синий = "dark blue", голубой = "light blue").
- `canon-terms/{en,es}/color.yaml` — for cross-lang label conventions.

## Implementation steps

1. **Setup cells**:
   - Imports (numpy, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn-or-equivalent), paths, `np.random.default_rng(42)`.
   - Import the three library modules:
     ```python
     from draganov_replication.pointclouds import build_pointclouds
     from draganov_replication.diagrams import compute_diagrams
     from draganov_replication.pd_distances import compute_pd_distance_grid
     ```

2. **Pipeline run** (idempotent — uses caches if present):
   ```python
   pc_manifest = build_pointclouds()
   dg_manifest = compute_diagrams()
   grids = compute_pd_distance_grid()  # dict[(distance, dim)] -> (34, 34)
   ```

3. **Figure 1 — 34×34 distance heatmap grid**:
   - Layout: 4 rows (one per distance) × 2 cols (one per dim).
   - Cells sorted by (lang, term), with faint divider lines between en | ru | es blocks.
   - Per-panel colourbar (matrices have very different absolute scales).
   - Save as `results/figures/draganov_replication_heatmap_grid.{pdf,png}`.

4. **Figure 2 — Russian-blues zoom**:
   - Subset cells `{en/blue, es/azul, ru/синий, ru/голубой}` from each of the 8 matrices.
   - 4×4 subset matrix per (distance, dim) — small heatmap or annotated table.
   - Highlight the predicted comparisons: d(en/blue, ru/синий) vs d(en/blue, ru/голубой); d(es/azul, ru/синий) vs d(es/azul, ru/голубой); d(ru/синий, ru/голубой) within-language baseline.
   - Save as `results/figures/draganov_replication_russian_blues_zoom.{pdf,png}`.

5. **Figure 3 — Cross-distance consistency**:
   - Take the upper-triangle of each 34×34 matrix as a 561-vector (34 choose 2).
   - Compute Spearman rank correlation between every pair of (distance, dim) → 8×8 correlation heatmap.
   - Caption: "Do the four distance notions agree on cell ordering?"
   - Save as `results/figures/draganov_replication_distance_consistency.{pdf,png}`.

6. **Table — `per_term_cross_language_ranking.csv`**:
   - For each (lang, term) and each (distance, dim), rank the closest cells in *other* languages (i.e. exclude same-language cells).
   - Columns: `[lang, term, distance, dim, rank, target_lang, target_term, distance_value]`.
   - Top 5 per cell, so 34 cells × 8 (distance, dim) × 5 = 1360 rows.

7. **Table — `russian_blues_distances.csv`**:
   - 6 unique pairs from the 4-cell subset ({en/blue, es/azul, ru/синий, ru/голубой}).
   - 8 (distance, dim) combinations as columns (or as long-format rows).
   - Concretely verifies the centerpiece survives the pipeline: assert in the notebook that the table has exactly 6 rows × 8 (distance, dim) columns and that all values are finite and > 0.

8. **Markdown commentary cells** at the end interpreting the figures:
   - Does the predicted Russian-blues split surface across distances?
   - Is it stronger in H_0 or H_1?
   - Are the four distances consistent with each other (Figure 3)?
   - Caveats:
     - Three of 34 cells are below the n≥200 target: ru/фиолетовый (n=104), ru/коричневый (n=163), es/marrón (n=161). The four Russian-blues centerpiece cells are all at n=200, so the centerpiece test is unaffected, but distances to those three undertarget cells will be slightly noisier than the rest of the grid.
     - Single PD per cell — no permutation test framing here (would require a different design).

## Acceptance

- Notebook runs end-to-end from a clean kernel in <15 minutes.
- Three figures (PDF + PNG each) and two CSVs written to `results/`.
- `russian_blues_distances.csv` contains exactly 6 rows × 8 (distance, dim) columns; all values finite and > 0 (concrete centerpiece signal).
- Markdown commentary addresses the Russian-blues hypothesis explicitly with quoted numbers, AND notes the three undertarget cells (фиолетовый, коричневый, marrón) as a caveat for grid interpretation.
- No `DeprecationWarning` or runtime errors in the executed notebook.
- Figures readable at print scale (matches the `phase3_diagram_distances` figure conventions).

## Out of scope

- Permutation tests on PD distances (a single PD per cell makes Mantel-style tests inapplicable in the same form as `replication/diagram_distances.py`; if a permutation framing is wanted later, file as a follow-up).
- Cross-epic comparison against `ph-project-1iq` (static fastText) outputs — file a follow-up issue once both land.
- Adding all-12-layer support (would require re-extracting embeddings; out of May 6 deadline scope).
- Tree construction or language-family evaluation à la Draganov C-step (inapplicable at N=3 languages; see Adaptation faithfulness note above).

title: Extract to_giotto_format to shared helper module
description:
## Summary

Extract `to_giotto_format` (currently in `replication/diagram_distances.py` lines 384–485) into a shared helper module so that both `replication/diagram_distances.py` (Kushnareva pipeline) and `draganov_replication/pd_distances.py` (this epic) can use it without duplication. Surfaced by plan review on `ph-project-inu`.

## Why

`to_giotto_format` packs persistence diagrams into the giotto-tda PairwiseDistance format `(N, F, 3)` with `[birth, death, hom_dim]` per row. It has a non-obvious correctness landmine: padding rows MUST use `(0, 0, hom_dim)` rather than `(0, 0, 0)`, because giotto-tda misclassifies `hom_dim=0` padding rows as real H_0 features and silently corrupts distance matrices. Reimplementing this from scratch in two places risks one diverging.

## Files to create

- `replication/giotto_format.py`
- `tests/test_giotto_format.py`

## Files to modify

- `replication/diagram_distances.py` — replace inline `to_giotto_format` (lines 384–485) with `from replication.giotto_format import to_giotto_format`. The only existing caller is `compute_per_head_distances` (line 543).

## Files to read for context

- `replication/diagram_distances.py:384–485` (current implementation; preserve docstring — it is the source of truth on the padding gotcha).
- `replication/diagram_distances.py:541–560` (only current caller).
- `tests/` — search for any existing tests of `to_giotto_format` so they move to `tests/test_giotto_format.py`.
- `gtda.diagrams.PairwiseDistance` documentation (input contract: shape `(N, F, 3)`).

## Implementation steps

1. Create `replication/giotto_format.py` with `to_giotto_format` moved verbatim. Keep the full docstring.
2. Update `replication/diagram_distances.py`:
   - Remove the inline definition.
   - Add `from replication.giotto_format import to_giotto_format` at the top.
3. If existing tests reference `to_giotto_format` (search `tests/`), move them to `tests/test_giotto_format.py`.
4. Add minimal coverage if not present:
   - `test_padding_uses_hom_dim`: H_1 padding rows have `arr[..., 2] == 1.0`, not `0.0`.
   - `test_shape`: output shape is `(N, sum(max_features per dim), 3)`.
   - `test_keyerror_on_missing_layer_head`: passing unknown `(layer, head)` raises `KeyError`.

## Acceptance

- `replication/giotto_format.py` exists and is importable.
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` no longer defines `to_giotto_format`.
- `pytest tests/test_giotto_format.py` green.
- `pytest tests/` green (no regressions in `replication/diagram_distances.py` consumers).
- `python -m py_compile replication/giotto_format.py replication/diagram_distances.py` clean.

## Note

Small task (~60 lines moved + ~30 lines of test). It exists so that `draganov_replication/pd_distances.py` (subtask `ph-project-inu.3`) does not need to copy this function. Independent of the rest of the draganov_replication tree — this can land before any other `inu.*` task.

title: test_vectors validation tests assume vector files missing; fail when data is present
description:
TestValidation.test_valid_langs_are_en_ru_es and
test_valid_kinds_are_cc_and_aligned in tests/baselines/test_vectors.py
expect a FileNotFoundError when load_fasttext is called with valid
(lang, kind). They assume the vector .bin/.vec files do NOT exist on
disk — but on a dev machine where data/fasttext/{cc,aligned}/ has
real or symlinked vector files, load_fasttext succeeds and the test
fails with "DID NOT RAISE".

Discovered while running pytest tests/baselines/ in a worktree where
.venv and data/ were symlinked to the main checkout for ssa.4.

Fix options:
1. Mock or monkey-patch the lookup path so the test always sees a
   missing file.
2. Use a tmp path (pytest tmp_path) and point load_fasttext at it,
   so the test's assumption holds regardless of dev-machine state.
3. Drop these tests — the validation behavior they target (no
   ValueError on valid lang/kind) is implicitly covered by the
   smoke tests that actually load real files.

Repro
-----
cd ph-project (or any worktree where data/fasttext/cc/cc.en.300.bin
exists)
.venv/bin/pytest tests/baselines/test_vectors.py::TestValidation -v

Expected: pass.
Actual: 2 failures — "DID NOT RAISE FileNotFoundError".

Not a regression caused by ssa.4 — ssa.4 doesn't modify test_vectors.py.
The failures only become visible to a CI/dev environment with the
data present, which is the normal case after ssa.1 lands. Likely
just hasn't been caught because nobody runs the full test suite
locally with the data present.

title: Replace circular test_per_head_slice_matches_compute_per_head with meaningful coverage
description:
## Summary

`tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py::TestComputeFullDistanceTensor::test_per_head_slice_matches_compute_per_head` is algebraically circular: `compute_full_distance_tensor` is implemented as a loop over `compute_per_head_distances`, so asserting that each slice matches the per-head call is guaranteed to be true given the implementation. It contributes zero regression protection.

## Files to modify

- `tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py` (around the `TestComputeFullDistanceTensor` class).

## Implementation steps

1. Either delete the test, or replace it with something the loop wrapper is responsible for INDEPENDENTLY:
   - Layer/head index mapping: `layers=range(3, 5)` should map to tensor axis-0 indices 0 and 1, with the (3, 0) and (4, 0) per-head slices, NOT axis-0 indices 3 and 4. Verify this mapping.
   - Heads/layers iteration order: assert that the result of `compute_full_distance_tensor` with non-default layers/heads has shape `(len(layers), len(heads), N, N, n_dims)`.
2. Existing shape coverage in `test_output_shape_12x12_N_N_ndims` and `test_subset_layers_heads` already covers the basic shape case, so the new test should focus on the index-mapping semantics.

## Why this matters

A useless test is worse than none — it gives false confidence and adds runtime to every CI run. The coordinator's `bd close` requires test gates to pass, so removing or replacing it tightens the meaning of those gates.

## Acceptance criteria

- The replacement test would fail under a deliberately broken indexing in `compute_full_distance_tensor` (e.g., swapping layer/head iteration order).
- Total test count in the file changes by 0 or +1, not -1.

title: Substrate cell 14 bare except swallows interrupts
description:
The substrate notebook `replication/notebooks/features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb` has a bare `except:` in cell 14 that swallows `KeyboardInterrupt`, `MemoryError`, `SystemExit` along with the intended `FileNotFoundError`. The adapted notebook `notebooks/phase3_thresholds.ipynb` cell 14 inherits this verbatim per the cell-faithful adaptation (mwk.2 / Option A).

Surfaced by the correctness review on PR for ph-project-mwk.2.

The bare except is unreachable in normal flow given the SCHEMA contract (`data/kwic/SCHEMA.md` guarantees the CSVs exist), so this is a latent paper-cut not an active bug. Fix would be `except (FileNotFoundError, pd.errors.ParserError):` at minimum, or drop the TSV fallback entirely now that SCHEMA is contractual.

Caveat: editing `replication/notebooks/` requires going through patch_notebook.py since the substrate notebooks are derived. Confirm the build flow before touching.

title: MUSE OOV in ru/kinship: 6 archaic affinal terms missing (18%)
description: Sub-baseline C (ssa.6) flagged ru/kinship as tier-2 OOV (17.6%, 28/34 found). Missing terms (all archaic/rare affinal): свёкор, деверь, золовка, свояченица, сноха, свояк. These are traditional Russian in-law terms that may not appear in modern wiki text used for MUSE. Options: (1) verify which forms appear in MUSE wiki.multi.ru.vec and substitute living variants where possible (e.g., свёкор may appear as свекор without the ё diacritic); (2) keep the canon as literature-grounded and accept the tier-2 marker. Investigate ё-vs-е normalization in MUSE Russian vocabulary.

title: Tighten test assertions in test_diagram_distances_loader.py
description:
## Summary

Pre-PR test review on blr.1 surfaced 4 loose/missing test assertions that should be fixed before merge.

## Files to modify

- `tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py`

## Issues to fix

1. **`test_empty_features_allowed` (line ~212)** — assertion uses a disjunction where the last arm (`arr.shape[1] == 2`) is trivially true for any 2-column array. Replace with `assert arr.shape == (0, 2)`.

2. **`sentence_idx_within_term` correctness never tested (lines ~259, ~635)** — only column presence is checked. Add a unit test with a known synthetic KWIC CSV asserting per-term indices are `[0, 1, 2, ...]` (and that this holds for non-contiguous terms in the KWIC CSV ordering).

3. **`test_parts_loaded_in_numeric_order` (lines ~263–298) doesn't verify order** — only checks `len(metadata) == 6`. Add an assertion that part1 samples come first: e.g. `metadata["source_part"].tolist() == [1]*4 + [2]*2`.

4. **`pytest.raises(Exception)` too broad (line ~324)** — production raises `ValueError` on count mismatch. Change to `pytest.raises(ValueError)`.

5. (optional, trivial) Add tests for `FileNotFoundError` paths in `load_lang_barcodes` (missing barcode dir, missing KWIC CSV).

## Acceptance

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py -v` passes after changes
- All 4 listed assertions tightened
- Item 5 optional but encouraged

title: Cross-linguistic adaptation atop the replication pipeline
description:
The replication/ tree (built under ph-project-4zo and follow-ups: nmh, xyo, wz1.1-.3, 1za, a9s, 3nh) is the working Kushnareva pipeline, hardened for the 2026 software stack and north's hardware. This epic covers the adaptation layer: feeding cross-linguistic KWIC contexts (from ph-project-5f9) through that pipeline and replacing the binary classifier with cross-linguistic comparison.

SCOPE NOTE (2026-05-04)
-----------------------
The COSI 115a final analysis (May 2026) is scoped to the COLOR domain
only. mwk.2 / mwk.3 / mwk.4 will run against en/ru/es color KWIC inputs
ONLY; the emotion and kinship CSVs that exist on disk under
data/kwic/{en,ru,es}/{emotion,kinship}.csv are out of scope for this
study and should be skipped by the (lang × domain) iteration loops.
Emotion + kinship remain available for follow-up work after May 6.

This rescoping was triggered by ph-project-bmb (corpus sparsity in
emotion/kinship even for ru/news 1M) and resolved via ph-project-i6q
(re-extract color from Leipzig 3M news, achieving n ≥ 150 per term).

title: Toy-input equivalence test for grab_weights_compat
description:
The port itself shipped as replication/grab_weights_compat.py (under ph-project-4zo + follow-ups). What's missing is a formal toy-input equivalence test: feed a fixed mBERT model + tokenizer + ~3 short sentences through grab_weights_compat.grab_attention_weights and assert the output shape (n_layers, batch, n_heads, MAX_LEN, MAX_LEN), dtype float16, and that values are bounded [0, 1] per attention head row.

title: Rebase feature/replication-4zo onto main — resolve ripser notebook conflict
description:
Context
-------
feature/replication-4zo has diverged from origin/main. An attempted
straight `git rebase origin/main` (during the ph-project-1za /merge
flow on 2026-05-02) hit a CONFLICT (content) on
replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb
and was aborted.

The conflict is non-trivial — both sides intentionally modified the
same notebook for different reasons. Other touched files
(.beads/issues.jsonl, features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb,
patch_notebook.py) auto-merged cleanly.

Conflicting commits
-------------------
On the branch:
  c613533 ph-project-xyo: extend patch_notebook.py for ripser/template notebook
          - re-patched the ripser notebook via the extended patcher
  8803339 ph-project-xyo: bump number_of_splits to 20 in ripser notebook
          - parameter bump in the same notebook

On main:
  ad60a62 ph-project-wz1.2: refactor ripser cell 29 — fork+mmap, fix OOM (#12)
          - rewrote cell 29 to use fork+mmap to fix an OOM in the
            ripser computation

Both changes are wanted: Anna's branch needs the patcher-driven
notebook regeneration AND the wz1.2 OOM fix. They overlap in cell
content, not in intent.

Approach
--------
1. Spawn the rebase subagent with full context (it will read both
   sides of the conflict, including the parent commits, and propose
   a reconciliation):

     ROLE: Rebase Agent (Conflict Resolution)
     SKILL: Read and follow .claude/skills/rebase/SKILL.md

     SOURCE: feature/replication-4zo
     TARGET: origin/main
     WORKTREE: <create a fresh worktree, do NOT do this in the live
                main checkout where Anna has uncommitted work>
     CLEANUP: false

2. The rebase agent should:
   - Inspect cell 29 of the ripser notebook on both sides
   - Identify whether the patcher (c613533) or the wz1.2 refactor
     (ad60a62) should be the source of truth for cell 29 — likely
     the wz1.2 version, with the patcher's other-cell changes
     reapplied on top
   - Re-run the patcher on the post-rebase notebook if appropriate,
     so the patcher remains the source of truth
   - Propose the resolved notebook for Anna's sign-off, NOT
     auto-commit

3. Whatever resolution is chosen, document it in the rebase commit
   message so future archaeology is clean.

Pre-flight
----------
- Anna's main checkout (/home/anna/ph-project) currently has
  uncommitted work: rename of features_calculation_by_thresholds.executed.ipynb
  to archive/, staged add of launch_jupyter.sh, modified
  .beads/issues.jsonl, untracked install_kernel_spec.py and
  launch_kernel.sh in replication/scripts/. Do NOT do the rebase
  in that checkout — use a separate worktree.

- After successful rebase, the launch_kernel.sh and
  install_kernel_spec.py untracked files in Anna's main checkout
  will become tracked (they're committed on main). Git should
  recognize them as identical and not flag conflicts. If it does,
  rm the untracked copies first.

Acceptance
----------
- feature/replication-4zo's HEAD is a descendant of origin/main.
- The ripser notebook contains both Anna's patcher-driven changes
  (number_of_splits=20, etc.) AND the wz1.2 cell-29 fork+mmap
  refactor.
- Rebase commit message documents the conflict resolution choice.
- Force-push origin/feature/replication-4zo with the rebased
  history (--force-with-lease).
- Anna's working tree changes survive the rebase (use stash +
  pop pattern, handle .beads/issues.jsonl conflict if it occurs).

Out of scope
------------
- Resolving any other branches' divergence from main.
- Changes to either patcher or wz1.2 logic — only reconciliation.
close_reason: Rebase complete. feature/replication-4zo is now a descendant of origin/main (last common ancestor: 42b7c10). Two ripser notebook conflicts resolved in a worktree: (1) setup cell — kept main's UUID, took xyo's np.int shim; (2) cell 0 — kept the wz1.1 kernel-cap validation cell, then main's setup cell. Cell 29 has wz1.2's fork+mmap helpers; cell 30 has number_of_splits=20 from xyo. Force-pushed with --force-with-lease. Safety tag pre-rebase-20260502-182642 retained on local pre-rebase tip.

title: Replication: handle ripserplusplus dependency for barcode notebook
description:
The barcode notebook (replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb)
imports `ripserplusplus`, the GPU-accelerated ripser the Kushnareva
paper used. The project venv has CPU `ripser` (0.6.14) but not
`ripserplusplus`. The notebook is currently unrunnable as-is.

Three viable paths (pick one, justify the choice):

A. Install ripserplusplus
   - https://github.com/simonzhang00/ripser-plusplus
   - Requires Pascal+ GPU and a CUDA matching the system.
   - We have CUDA 13.0 and an RTX 5070 Ti (Blackwell). May or may
     not build cleanly — try and see.

B. Write a CPU shim
   - Create replication/ripser_count_compat.py that mirrors
     reference/ripser_count.py's API but calls scikit-learn's
     ripser.ripser() under the hood.
   - Slow but portable.

C. Skip barcode features entirely for the replication
   - The threshold features (Section 4.1) already give a working
     classifier (per ph-project-4zo). Barcode + template features
     (Sections 4.2 + 4.3) are additional richness, not core proof.
   - Document this scope choice and move on.

Recommendation if compute time matters: A first, fall back to C.

Out of scope here: the cross-linguistic mBERT epic (ph-project-mwk).
Whether the main project uses ripser vs. ripserplusplus is a separate
decision and may be informed by what we learn here.
close_reason: Resolved: ripserplusplus master compiles cleanly with CUDA 13 / RTX 5070 Ti via 'pip install git+https://github.com/simonzhang00/ripser-plusplus.git'. PyPI is stale; upstream master has the cuda12 thrust fix (PR #14, merged 2023). Smoke test on 4-point unit square produces correct H0/H1 barcodes.

title: MUSE OOV in en/kinship: 6 in-law compounds missing (22%)
description: Sub-baseline C (ssa.6) flagged en/kinship as tier-2 OOV (22.2%, 21/27 found). Missing terms (all hyphenated affinal compounds): father-in-law, mother-in-law, son-in-law, daughter-in-law, brother-in-law, sister-in-law. The MUSE wiki vocabulary tokenizes around hyphens and these forms are absent. Options: (1) revise canon to use unhyphenated multi-word forms (which already get head-word extraction → 'law' which is wrong) or use different surface forms; (2) accept the tier-2 marker as the noted limitation. Investigate which English in-law surface forms appear in MUSE wiki.multi.en.vec and update canon-terms/en/kinship.yaml accordingly.

title: phase1_kwic.matchers: SpacyMatcher should batch via nlp.pipe() — current per-sentence calls make full extraction infeasible (~5h, not ~5min)
description:
Summary
-------
SpacyMatcher.lemmatize calls self._nlp(sentence) per sentence in a loop
(phase1_kwic/matchers.py:211). On the Leipzig 1M news corpus this is
the dominant cost in extract_kwic. Empirical: en/color extraction was
still on its first (lang, domain) pair after >16 minutes of CPU at 99%.
At ~3 ms per sentence × 1M sentences = ~50 minutes per (lang, domain).
Across 6 spaCy runs (en × 3, es × 3) that's ~5 hours, plus another
~1.5 hours for pymorphy3 — total ~6.5 hours for the full 5f9.5 run.
The 5f9.5 spec estimated 30-60s per pair, off by ~50x.

Root cause
----------
spaCy is designed for batched processing via nlp.pipe(sentences,
batch_size=...). nlp.pipe avoids per-call setup, batches internal
work, and yields documents lazily. Typical speedup on en_core_web_md:
10x-20x over per-sentence calls. After this change, total 5f9.5
extraction should drop to ~30-60 minutes — matching the spec's
spirit if not its literal estimate.

Files to modify
---------------
- phase1_kwic/matchers.py
- phase1_kwic/extract.py (the call site that consumes lemmatize results)
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_matchers.py (add a batched-API test)

Recommended approach
--------------------
1. Extend the Matcher Protocol with a streaming method:
       def lemmatize_many(
           self, sentences: Iterable[str]
       ) -> Iterator[list[tuple[str, str]]]:
           '''Yield (token, lemma) lists in input order, one per sentence.'''
   Keep lemmatize() as the single-sentence convenience that internally
   delegates to lemmatize_many for consistency.

2. SpacyMatcher.lemmatize_many uses nlp.pipe with batch_size=128 (tune
   if needed) and disables the parser/NER as today:
       for doc in self._nlp.pipe(sentences, batch_size=128):
           yield [(t.text, t.lemma_) for t in doc if not t.is_space]

3. PymorphyMatcher.lemmatize_many can simply iterate (pymorphy3 has
   no batched API), but adding the method preserves the Protocol
   contract.

4. extract_kwic: change the per-sentence loop to consume the streaming
   API. Practically:
       lines = (s.split('\t', 1)[1] for s in stream if '\t' in s)
       for ws_tokens, lemmatized in zip(
           ws_token_stream(...),
           matcher.lemmatize_many(lines),
       ):
           ...
   Be careful to preserve sentence-index alignment if needed for
   target_idx computation.

5. Add a benchmark test: 1000 synthetic sentences via lemmatize_many
   should be at least 5x faster than the equivalent lemmatize loop
   (use time.perf_counter; assert speedup > 5x to guard regression).

Acceptance
----------
- After the change, `python -m scripts.extract_kwic --lang en --domain color`
  on the real Leipzig en corpus completes in under 10 minutes.
- pytest tests/phase1_kwic/test_matchers.py passes (incl. new
  batched-API test and benchmark assertion).
- 5f9.5 (full 9-pair extraction + validation notebook) becomes feasible
  in one sitting.

Why this surfaced now
---------------------
5f9.4 unit tests use a StubMatcher with simple per-sentence behavior,
so the per-sentence cost was invisible until 5f9.5 attempted real-corpus
runs. This is exactly the kind of integration regression that the
spec's "synthetic corpus avoids the ~150-300MB Leipzig file dependency
for CI" trade-off was always going to defer to first real run.

title: Unify _bh_correction between replication/diagram_distances.py and phase3_comparison.ipynb
description:
## Summary

`_bh_correction` is now defined in two places:
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` (added in blr.3) — module-level, importable.
- `notebooks/phase3_comparison.ipynb` cell 11 — inline copy.

The module's docstring says it lives there "so notebook code can import it rather than redefining it inline" — but `phase3_comparison.ipynb` was not updated to import it. Headline analysis notebooks now use parallel implementations of the same FDR step.

## Resolution options

- (preferred) Update `phase3_comparison.ipynb` cell 11 to import `_bh_correction` from `replication.diagram_distances`. Re-execute the cell and verify outputs are identical.
- (fallback) If `phase3_comparison.ipynb` should remain self-contained, instead update the module docstring to say the canonical version lives in the notebook.

## Acceptance

- Only one canonical implementation of `_bh_correction` is treated as source of truth.
- Either both notebooks import from the module, or the module docstring no longer claims to be the canonical version.

## Discovered from

blr.3 architecture review.

title: phase1_kwic.canon: load_canon should use lemmatize_many for symmetry with extract_kwic
description:
Summary
-------
After ph-project-o93 (PR #28) lands, the corpus-side lemma path uses
SpacyMatcher.lemmatize_many on full sentences (which gives spaCy
context for tagging), while load_canon still calls matcher.lemmatize
per ws-token in isolation (no context). For canonical color / emotion
/ kinship vocabulary this is fine in practice, but the architectural
drift means the two paths can produce silently different lemmas for
the same surface word on context-sensitive homographs (en: "saw"
noun vs verb, "rose" noun vs verb, "leaves" noun vs verb). Term.lemmas
(canon side) and corpus ws_lemmas would then disagree and a hit would
be silently missed.

Why this matters now
--------------------
1. The two lemma paths must agree for KWIC matching to be correct.
2. The current canon list is mostly context-stable (concrete nouns
   and basic adjectives), but emotion-domain words like "love" /
   "fear" already have homograph noun-vs-verb forms in English.
3. Even if the canon never trips a homograph, the architecture should
   not depend on the canon being lucky.

Files to modify
---------------
- phase1_kwic/canon.py
- phase1_kwic/matchers.py (extract a shared lemma-pick helper if not
  already done in o93's PR)
- tests/phase1_kwic/test_canon.py

Recommended approach
--------------------
1. Refactor load_canon to call matcher.lemmatize_many([surface_form])
   per term and use the same character-offset reconciliation as the
   corpus side. The Term.lemmas tuple is then computed by exactly the
   same code path that produces corpus ws_lemmas, eliminating drift
   by construction.
2. Alternatively (simpler but less symmetric), keep load_canon on the
   per-ws-token path and add an explicit regression test pinning the
   contract: for every term in canon-terms/{en,es}/*.yaml, assert
   matcher.lemmatize_many([term.surface])[0] yields the same lemmas
   tuple as the per-ws-token computation.
3. Document in matchers.py whichever decision is made: canon and
   corpus MUST share a lemma path. State the contract explicitly.

Acceptance
----------
- canon-side and corpus-side lemma computation use the same code path
  for SpacyMatcher (PymorphyMatcher is already symmetric).
- No silent canon misses on context-sensitive surface forms.
- pytest tests/phase1_kwic/ passes.

title: Cross-disciplinary textbook overview of ph-project
description:
Goal: build a textbook-style overview of ph-project as docs/overview.pdf, covering the humanities anchor (color/emotion/kinship), mBERT and attention, persistent homology, the Kushnareva pipeline + our adaptation, the codebase, tooling, and current results / open questions.

Why: the project is largely agent-written. A unified overview document forces synthesis across the linguistics → ML → algebraic topology seam and gives Anna a learning artifact she can read as ground-truth for what was built and why. May later feed ph-project-vdh (Phase 5 paper writeup), but is its own deliverable.

Scope:
- LaTeX, document class report, built from docs/
- 9 chapters (motivation, humanities, attention, attention→graphs, persistent homology, Kushnareva pipeline + adaptation, codebase tour, tooling, results)
- 3 TikZ diagrams (filtration, persistence diagram, project pipeline)
- Style guide and BibTeX bibliography
- Consistency review at the end as a punch-list at docs/REVIEW.md

Out of scope (file as discovered-from later if needed):
- Final paper for Phase 5 (separate, ph-project-vdh)
- Index/glossary, HTML build, translations

Voice: rigorous-but-dialogical, in Anna's eventual edit voice. The textbook poses questions and answers them, builds intuition before formalism, and treats the reader as someone deep in linguistics meeting topology fresh.

Slop prevention: every factual claim must cite a BibTeX entry. STYLE.md (built in the Setup task) makes this concrete with banned constructions and examples.

Subtasks (filed separately):
- Setup (kit) — scaffolding + style guide
- Ch 1 Motivation (cal)
- Ch 2 Humanities anchor (cal) — consolidates the 3 canon-terms reports
- Ch 3 mBERT and attention (cal)
- Ch 4 From attention to graphs (cal)
- Ch 5 Persistent homology (cal)
- Ch 6 Kushnareva pipeline + adaptation (cal)
- Ch 7 Codebase tour (kit)
- Ch 8 Tooling and infrastructure (kit)
- Ch 9 Results and open questions (cal)
- Diagrams (kit)
- Consistency review (bea)

Setup blocks all chapter and diagram tasks. Consistency review is blocked by all chapter and diagram tasks.

title: Setup: LaTeX scaffolding + style guide for textbook overview
description:
Summary
Lay down the LaTeX scaffolding and style guide for the textbook overview. After this task, every chapter agent has a clear file to write into and a style guide to anchor on.

Files to modify (create)
- docs/overview.tex — main file, document class report
- docs/refs.bib — seed BibTeX bibliography (substantial seed list, see step 3)
- docs/Makefile — `make pdf` via latexmk
- docs/STYLE.md — voice, terminology, citation, and conventions guide
- docs/README.md — short orientation for anyone opening docs/
- docs/01-motivation.tex through docs/09-results.tex — empty stubs containing only `\chapter{Title}` and `% TODO: chapter body`
- docs/figures/ — empty directory; the diagrams task fills it

Files to read for context
- README.md (project root) — pre-registered predictions, project framing
- AGENTS.md — bd workflow conventions
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md — citation short-form conventions to mirror
- canon-terms/color_report.md — example of the prose voice the textbook should match

Implementation steps
1. Create docs/ and docs/figures/ directories.
2. Write docs/overview.tex:
   - \documentclass{report}
   - Required packages: amsmath, amssymb, amsthm, tikz (with libraries positioning, calc, fit), hyperref, biblatex (style=authoryear, backend=biber), graphicx, fontspec for Cyrillic + Spanish accents (compile with lualatex or xelatex)
   - Title page (Author: Anna Brezgis; subtitle "A textbook-style overview of ph-project"; \date{\today})
   - \tableofcontents
   - \listoffigures
   - \input{01-motivation} ... \input{09-results} (no .tex extension)
   - \printbibliography at the end
3. Write docs/refs.bib with real entries (look up DOIs / ACL anthology IDs / publisher info), including ALL of:
   - Kushnareva et al. 2021 (EMNLP)
   - Berlin & Kay 1969; Kay & Maffi 1999; Lillo et al. 2018; Paramei 2005; Winawer et al. 2007; Davidoff et al. 1999
   - Wierzbicka 1999, Wierzbicka 1992; Goddard & Wierzbicka 2002; Ekman 1992; Pavlenko 2005
   - García Lorca 1933; Maurer (ed.) 2004
   - Murdock 1949; Friedrich 1964
   - Vaswani et al. 2017; Devlin et al. 2019; Pires et al. 2019 ("How multilingual is multilingual BERT?", ACL); Clark et al. 2019
   - Carlsson 2009 ("Topology and data"); Edelsbrunner & Harer 2010 (Computational Topology); Cohen-Steiner, Edelsbrunner, Harer 2007 (Stability of persistence diagrams); Bauer 2021 (Ripser)
4. Write docs/Makefile: `pdf:` target runs `latexmk -lualatex -bibtex overview.tex`; `clean:` removes auxiliary files.
5. Write docs/STYLE.md covering all of the following sections:

   ## Voice
   - Dialogical, rigorous-but-conversational. Pose questions and answer them. Build intuition before formalism.
   - First-person plural ("we predict", "we choose mBERT because"). Not third-person ("the author predicts"); not passive ("it is predicted").
   - Treat the reader as someone deep in linguistics meeting the topology fresh.

   ## Banned constructions (with examples)
   - "It's worth noting" / "It is important to note" → state the thing directly
   - "Furthermore" / "Moreover" / "Additionally" → use plain conjunction or paragraph break
   - "Let me explain" → just explain
   - Three-bullet summary at the end of every section → don't
   - Hedging: "may potentially", "could possibly", "arguably" → commit or omit
   - "In conclusion" / "To summarize" → don't telegraph; let the prose conclude

   ## Citation rule
   - Every factual or empirical claim cites a BibTeX entry. Use \parencite{} parenthetical, \textcite{} for inline-by-name.
   - If you need a bib entry not in refs.bib, add it in your chapter's task. The consistency review will dedupe; do not coordinate manually.

   ## Math typesetting
   - $...$ inline, \[...\] or align for displays
   - $H_0$ and $H_1$ in math mode, never unicode 𝐻₀
   - Use \varepsilon (ε) for filtration parameter consistently
   - Distance d, similarity / attention W (lowercase d, capital W)

   ## Terminology consistency
   - "Filtration parameter ε" or "threshold ε" — pick one per chapter and stay consistent
   - "Persistence pair" or "(birth, death) pair" preferred over "barcode bar"
   - "Barcode" = the multiset of pairs; "persistence diagram" = the (birth, death) plot view
   - "Layer × head" for attention head identification (e.g., "layer 4 head 7")
   - Russian terms: Cyrillic with inline gloss on first use — "тоска (toska, 'longing')" — then either form
   - Spanish terms: original orthography, glossed inline on first use — "duende ('a dark, telluric force')"

   ## Section titles
   - Prose, not numeric. \section{Color}, not \section{2.1 Color}. LaTeX numbers automatically.

   ## Notation
   - A brief notation table belongs in Ch 5 (persistent homology). Other chapters introduce notation inline as needed.

   ## Cross-references
   - Use \autoref liberally
   - Label conventions: \label{fig:...}, \label{sec:...}, \label{ch:...}

   ## Length
   - Chapters target 3–5 pages of body text
   - Ch 5 (persistent homology) and Ch 6 (pipeline+adaptation) likely longer (6–8 and 5–7 pages)

6. Write 9 empty chapter stubs containing only:
     \chapter{<title>}
     % TODO: chapter body
   Titles:
   - 01-motivation: "Motivation and hypothesis"
   - 02-humanities: "The humanities anchor"
   - 03-attention: "mBERT and attention"
   - 04-graphs: "From attention to graphs"
   - 05-persistent-homology: "Persistent homology in plain language"
   - 06-pipeline: "The Kushnareva pipeline and our adaptation"
   - 07-codebase: "Codebase tour"
   - 08-tooling: "Tooling and infrastructure"
   - 09-results: "Results and open questions"
7. Write docs/README.md: short note on what docs/ is, how to build, where STYLE.md is.

Acceptance
- `cd docs && make pdf` succeeds and produces overview.pdf with title page, TOC, list of figures, and 9 chapter pages (each empty except for chapter heading)
- STYLE.md is concrete and example-driven, includes ALL listed sections (voice, banned constructions, citation rule, math typesetting, terminology consistency, section titles, notation, cross-references, length)
- BibTeX entries are real (DOIs / ACL anthology IDs / publisher info), not stub placeholders
- All entries listed in step 3 are present in refs.bib

Owner: kit (implementer for code/structure)

title: Ch 9: Results and open questions
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 9 of the textbook: results and open questions. Honest accounting of what's done, what's in flight, what's not yet started, and what would change the project's story.

Files to modify
- docs/09-results.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- README.md
- bd ready and bd list output (run `bd ready` and `bd list --status open` before drafting; also `bd list --status closed` for what's done)
- Recent git log (git log --oneline -30)
- replication/outputs/ — actual current output location (results/ is planned but not yet populated)
- The five Phase epics (5f9, ssa, 0cm, bt5, vdh) and the mwk epic — read with `bd show <id>`

Implementation steps
1. Open with where the project actually is, not where it was meant to be. Be honest. The five-phase structure is in flight; describe each phase's current state.
2. By phase:
   - Phase 1 — KWIC extraction (5f9): completion status (check at draft time)
   - Phase 2 — fastText baseline (ssa): in flight; ssa.1 done, ssa.2-7 in various states
   - Phase 3 — mBERT + PH (0cm): blocked on mwk; mwk.1 in progress
   - Phase 4 — comparison and prediction tests (bt5): not started; depends on Phase 3
   - Phase 5 — writeup (vdh): the formal paper, separate from this textbook
3. Discovered work / known issues: wz1 family (ripser OOM), c9c (brittle pattern), ybh (incomplete RIPSER_PATCHES), and any other significant open issues at draft time. Note that `results/` is planned but not yet populated; current outputs live at `replication/outputs/`.
4. Open questions / what would change the story:
   - If the four pre-registered predictions all fail, what does that mean?
   - If they all succeed, are there confounds we haven't addressed?
   - Tokenization caveat (multi-word terms; canon-terms head-word extraction per ph-project-ssa.3)
   - mBERT-specific vs. multilingual-model-general findings
5. Close: what Anna would do next if she had more time.

Length target: 3–5 pages.

Acceptance

- replication/outputs/ named as the actual current output location
- Open issues honestly named
- Open questions section names what would falsify or bolster the work
- STYLE.md voice followed
- Compiles cleanly

Owner: cal (synthesis task)

title: Diagrams: 3 TikZ figures (filtration, persistence diagram, pipeline)
description:
Summary
Draft three TikZ figures referenced by the chapters: a small filtration sequence (Ch 5), an example persistence diagram (Ch 5), and the project pipeline (Ch 6). Each figure is its own .tex file, included via \input{figures/<name>}.

Files to modify
- docs/figures/filtration.tex
- docs/figures/persistence-diagram.tex
- docs/figures/pipeline.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- replication/Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf — for the pipeline shape
- README.md — for the project pipeline (KWIC → mBERT → graph → PH → comparison)

Implementation steps
1. docs/figures/filtration.tex
   - Three or four panels showing a Vietoris-Rips filtration of a small point cloud (5–8 points) at increasing thresholds ε
   - Panel 1: just points (ε small)
   - Panel 2: some edges added (a few pairs within ε)
   - Panel 3: more edges, a triangle filled
   - Panel 4: all edges in
   - Label "ε increases →" and individual ε values per panel
   - \begin{figure}[H] ... \caption{...} \label{fig:filtration}
2. docs/figures/persistence-diagram.tex
   - Plot of birth (x-axis) vs. death (y-axis) for a few labeled features
   - Diagonal y = x line drawn
   - Two or three H_0 features (color-coded), one or two H_1 features
   - Mark the longest-living feature explicitly
   - Caption explaining how to read it. \label{fig:persistence-diagram}
3. docs/figures/pipeline.tex
   - Block diagram: [Corpus] → [KWIC extraction] → [mBERT attention] → [Graph filtration] → [Persistent homology features] → [Cross-linguistic comparison]
   - Each block is a TikZ node; arrows show data flow
   - Bracket above the right half labeled "topology"; bracket above the left half labeled "linguistic input"
   - Caption and \label{fig:pipeline}
4. If overview.tex's preamble lacks any required TikZ libraries (positioning, calc, fit), add them.

Acceptance

- Figures look readable and have proper captions
