title: Consistency review of textbook overview
description:
Summary
Read the assembled docs/overview.pdf (or the .tex files) and produce a punch-list of issues for Anna to apply. Don't edit chapter files; this preserves Anna's editorial control.

Files to modify (create)
- docs/REVIEW.md — the punch-list

Files to read for context
- docs/overview.tex and all 9 chapter files (.tex)
- docs/figures/ — the three diagrams
- docs/STYLE.md — the standard to check against
- docs/refs.bib — to verify citations resolve

Implementation steps
1. Compile the document and read it from top to bottom as a reader would.
2. For each issue found, add an entry to REVIEW.md with:
   - Severity: blocker / major / minor
   - Location: chapter and section / line
   - Issue: what's wrong
   - Suggested fix (one or two lines, no full rewrite)
3. Categories to check explicitly:
   - Voice drift: does each chapter follow STYLE.md? Any banned constructions?
   - Internal consistency: do chapters contradict each other? Do cross-refs resolve?
   - Citation completeness: every empirical/theoretical claim has a \cite{}.
   - Citation correctness: BibTeX entries exist for every \cite{} key.
   - Math errors: H_0 / H_1 used consistently; no obvious mistakes in the persistent homology chapter.
   - Figure references: \autoref{fig:filtration} etc. resolve and the figure numbering is sane.
   - Length: any chapter dramatically out of target? (Ch 5 long is fine; a chapter shorter than 2 pages is probably underdone.)
   - Coverage gaps: anything from STYLE.md systematically missed.

Acceptance
- REVIEW.md exists and contains a structured punch-list
- Punch-list groups issues by severity
- Suggested fixes are concrete, not "rewrite for clarity"
- No edits to chapter .tex files

Owner: bea (reviewer)

title: Ch 1: Motivation and hypothesis
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 1 of the textbook: motivation and hypothesis. Frames what the project is asking and why it matters, ending with the four pre-registered predictions.

Files to modify
- docs/01-motivation.tex (replace stub with chapter body)

Files to read for context
- README.md (project root) — the four pre-registered predictions are listed there and are the spine of this chapter
- CLAUDE.md — project framing
- canon-terms/color_report.md — Russian blue split is the central motivating case
- docs/STYLE.md — voice and citation conventions to follow
- replication/Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf — for Kushnareva's task framing

Implementation steps
1. Open with the central question: do distributional patterns in language encode culturally-specific attentional structures, and if so, do they show up as different shapes — measurable topology — in the attention graphs of a multilingual language model?
2. Walk through why this question is interesting. Linguistic relativity has been studied behaviorally (Winawer et al. 2007, Russian blues) but rarely directly in the internal representations of language models. mBERT learns shared multilingual representations and is a natural test bed.
3. Bridge to topology: differences between languages may not be detectable as distance shifts (which mean-collapse) but as shape changes (extra holes, extra connected components, different persistence). Cite Carlsson 2009 for the "topology of data" framing.
4. State the hypothesis (one or two sentences, plain language).
5. State the four pre-registered predictions verbatim from README.md, with one paragraph of unpacking each:
   a. Russian blue split (extra H_0 component)
   b. Emotion divergence > color divergence
   c. Russian kinship complexity
   d. Head specialization (Clark et al. 2019)
6. Close with what would falsify each prediction. (Important for pre-registration and for the rigor reviewer.)

Length target: 3–5 pages.

Acceptance
- All four predictions stated and unpacked
- Russian blue split case made cleanly using canon-terms/color_report.md as the source
- Every empirical or theoretical claim cites a BibTeX entry in refs.bib (add new entries if needed)
- STYLE.md voice followed; no banned constructions
- Compiles cleanly with the existing Makefile

Owner: cal (researcher)

title: Ch 2: The humanities anchor
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 2 of the textbook: the humanities anchor. Consolidates the three canon-terms reports into one chapter that explains what a "semantic domain" is in this project, why color / emotion / kinship were chosen, and how the canonical term lists were built from cited literature.

Files to modify
- docs/02-humanities.tex (replace stub with chapter body)

Files to read for context
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md — schema and citation conventions
- canon-terms/color_report.md — Berlin & Kay + Russian blue split
- canon-terms/emotion_report.md — Ekman/Wierzbicka, тоска, duende
- canon-terms/kinship_report.md — Murdock Eskimo system, Russian affinal richness, Spanish consuegro/concunado
- canon-terms/{en,ru,es}/{color,emotion,kinship}.yaml — actual term lists (read at least one per domain to get a feel)
- docs/STYLE.md

Implementation steps
1. Open with what a "semantic domain" means and why the project picks three. The choice is methodological: color is universal-ish but with one famous exception (Russian blue), emotion is culturally variable, kinship has clean typological framing. Together they span a spectrum from cross-linguistically convergent to divergent.
2. Section 2.1 — Color. Berlin & Kay (1969) Stage VII; the Russian blue split (синий vs голубой) using Paramei 2005 and Winawer et al. 2007; Spanish as a control (no blue split).
3. Section 2.2 — Emotion. Ekman 1992 as the comparability spine. Wierzbicka NSM — define what an NSM prime is (Goddard & Wierzbicka 2002). Centerpiece terms: Russian тоска (Wierzbicka 1999), Spanish duende (García Lorca 1933, Maurer 2004 ed.).
4. Section 2.3 — Kinship. Murdock (1949) Eskimo system; all three languages share the type. Russian affinal richness (Friedrich 1964): свёкор/тесть, etc. Spanish extended affinal terms (consuegro, concunado).
5. Close with the term-count table (English / Russian / Spanish per domain) and a note on why these specific divergences are what we'd expect persistent homology to detect.

Length target: 5–7 pages (the longest chapter, given three domains).

Acceptance
- All three domains covered with their anchor citations
- Russian blue split, тоска/duende, and Russian affinal richness called out as the central cross-linguistic divergences
- Term counts table included
- Every claim cites a BibTeX entry
- STYLE.md voice followed
- Compiles cleanly

Owner: cal (researcher)

title: Ch 3: mBERT and attention
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 3 of the textbook: mBERT and attention. Explains what attention computes inside a transformer, why mBERT specifically is the right model for cross-linguistic comparison, and what Clark et al. 2019 tell us about head specialization.

Files to modify
- docs/03-attention.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- replication/Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf — Section 4 has the relevant attention framing
- README.md — the project's specific use of mBERT

Implementation steps
1. Open with the transformer attention mechanism. Define query, key, value; scaled-dot-product softmax formula; multi-head attention. Cite Vaswani et al. 2017.
2. BERT (Devlin et al. 2019): encoder-only stack, masked language modeling, bidirectional context. Keep this short — not the focus.
3. mBERT specifically: 12 layers, 12 heads, 110M params, trained on concatenated Wikipedia of 104 languages with shared WordPiece vocabulary. Why a single shared model rather than language-specific BERTs: cross-lingual representations align without explicit supervision (Pires et al. 2019, "How multilingual is multilingual BERT?"; add to refs.bib).
4. What attention heads specialize in (Clark et al. 2019): syntactic relations, coreference, attention to [SEP] / [CLS], etc. This motivates the project's prediction (4) about head specialization.
5. Bridge: an attention matrix at one head is an N×N tensor at one (layer, head). Each row says how much one token attends to each other token. This is the object the rest of the pipeline turns into a graph.

Length target: 3–4 pages.

Acceptance
- Vaswani, Devlin, Pires, Clark cited
- mBERT's 12L / 12H / shared-vocab structure explicitly stated
- Attention matrix object explicitly named (sets up Ch 4)
- STYLE.md voice followed

Owner: cal (researcher)

title: Ch 4: From attention to graphs
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 4 of the textbook: attention → graph. Explains Kushnareva's construction for converting an attention matrix into a weighted graph and why that is the natural object for topological analysis.

Files to modify
- docs/04-graphs.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- replication/Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf — Section 4.1 in particular
- reference/grab_weights.py — how attention matrices are extracted in their pipeline
- reference/ripser_count.py — how matrices are passed to ripser

Implementation steps
1. Recap from Ch 3: an attention head produces an N×N matrix W where W[i,j] is how much token i attends to token j.
2. Treating W as a graph: tokens are nodes, attention weights are edge weights. Discuss directed vs. undirected (Kushnareva symmetrize via max), self-loops, normalization.
3. Thresholding: define the family of graphs G_t obtained by keeping edges with weight > t. As t decreases, the graph fills up. This is exactly a filtration in the topological sense.
4. Distance vs. similarity: ripser wants distances, but attention is similarity. Standard move: invert via d(i,j) = 1 - W[i,j] or some monotone transform. Kushnareva's exact choice goes here (read Sec 4 of the paper for confirmation).
5. Why this object (a filtered graph) is rich enough for topology to be informative: as the threshold decreases, components merge (H_0) and cycles form and fill (H_1). The persistence of these features reveals structure not visible at any single threshold.

Length target: 3–4 pages.

Acceptance
- Construction described concretely enough that a reader could implement it
- Filtration concept introduced (handed off to Ch 5)
- Cites Kushnareva 2021 throughout for the specific design choices
- STYLE.md voice followed

Owner: cal (researcher)

title: Ch 5: Persistent homology in plain language
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 5 of the textbook: persistent homology. Builds intuition for simplicial complexes, filtrations, and persistence diagrams from the ground up, then connects to the H_0 / H_1 features the project actually uses.

Files to modify
- docs/05-persistent-homology.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- replication/Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf — Sections 4.1–4.3 for the specific features used
- requirements.txt — for the ripser/ripserplusplus/giotto-tda toolchain (with pinned-commit comment)

Implementation steps
1. Open with the data → topology bridge. Cite Carlsson 2009 ("topology of data") and Edelsbrunner & Harer 2010 (the standard textbook).
2. Simplicial complexes informally: 0-simplex is a point, 1-simplex is an edge, 2-simplex is a triangle. **Insert `\input{figures/filtration}` at the point in the prose where the small example is introduced.** Reference it via \autoref{fig:filtration}.
3. Filtrations: a sequence of nested simplicial complexes parametrized by a threshold ε. As ε grows, simplices appear and never disappear.
4. Homology in plain language: H_0 counts connected components; H_1 counts independent loops (1-cycles that are not boundaries of 2-simplices). Higher H_k counts higher-dimensional voids; rarely informative for attention graphs (typically just H_0 and H_1).
5. Persistence: a feature has a birth ε (when it appears) and a death ε (when it merges or fills in). Plot (birth, death) pairs to get the persistence diagram. **Insert `\input{figures/persistence-diagram}` near this explanation.** Reference it via \autoref{fig:persistence-diagram}.
6. Stability theorem (one sentence): persistence diagrams are stable under small perturbations of the input. Cite Cohen-Steiner et al. 2007 (already in the seed refs.bib).
7. Practical computation: Ripser (Bauer 2021) for the algorithm; giotto-tda for the Python wrapper. Mention the GPU variant ripserplusplus and the pinned-commit story (forward-reference to Ch 8 tooling).
8. What features Kushnareva extract — at conceptual level only: barcodes (counts, lifetimes, etc.), features by threshold (#edges, #components at fixed ε), template features (distance to known motifs). Stay at conceptual level here; Ch 6 connects them to our pipeline.
9. Include a brief notation table at the start of the chapter (per STYLE.md): ε for filtration parameter, $H_0$/$H_1$ for the homology groups we use, d for distance, W for attention matrix.

Length target: 6–8 pages (longest chapter; rigor required).

Acceptance
- Simplicial complex, filtration, persistence diagram defined cleanly
- H_0 and H_1 explained with the connected-components / loops intuition
- Stability theorem mentioned (without proof)
- Ripser / giotto-tda named as the actual tools
- Both `\input{figures/filtration}` and `\input{figures/persistence-diagram}` appear in the chapter prose at sensible points
- Cross-references to figures use \autoref
- Notation table present at chapter start
- STYLE.md voice followed; no banned constructions

Owner: cal (researcher)

title: Ch 6: The Kushnareva pipeline and our adaptation
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 6 of the textbook: the Kushnareva pipeline as designed for binary text classification, and our adaptation to cross-linguistic semantic comparison. This is the chapter where the methodology threads come together.

Files to modify
- docs/06-pipeline.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- replication/Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf (full)
- replication/README.md — the replication subtree's purpose
- README.md (project root) — our adaptation, the four predictions
- reference/ — directory listing, to reference the actual notebook names
- notebooks/ — directory listing, to reference current adapted state
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md — for how canon term lists feed the pipeline
- AGENTS.md — for the bd-tracked epic structure (5f9, mwk, ssa, 0cm, bt5, vdh)

Implementation steps
1. Open with Kushnareva's original task: binary classification of human-written vs. machine-generated text. Architecture: BERT attention → graphs → topological features → logistic regression classifier.
2. Their three feature families. **Note: Ch 5 introduces these concepts; your job here is to describe how each family is *computed in our pipeline* and what each one tells us about a specific (layer, head) attention matrix. Do not redefine the families from scratch.**
   - Section 4.1 thresholds: how we compute counts (edges, weakly/strongly connected components) per (layer, head, threshold) and what those counts tell us
   - Section 4.2 barcodes: how we compute persistence diagrams from the filtered attention graph and which summary statistics we extract (lifetimes, sums, longest-bar timing)
   - Section 4.3 templates: which template motifs we measure distance to (attention to previous token, to punctuation, to CLS) and why
3. What we keep:
   - Same feature families
   - Same model class (mBERT, where they used BERT — already adapted in our notebook port)
   - Same per-(layer, head) extraction
4. What we change:
   - Inputs: canonical terms × KWIC sentences from Leipzig Corpora × three languages, instead of WebText / GPT-2 sentences
   - Comparison: Mann-Whitney U / permutation tests / bottleneck and Wasserstein distances between persistence diagrams across languages, instead of binary logistic regression
   - Targets: testing the four pre-registered predictions, not maximizing classification accuracy
5. Why the same features generalize: Kushnareva's features describe the *shape* of attention. Different inputs produce different shapes. If different languages produce different shapes for the same semantic domain, the same features will catch it.
6. Section: in-flight status. Reference the bd epic structure honestly:
   - Phase 1 — KWIC extraction (5f9): completion status at draft time
   - Phase 2 — fastText baseline (ssa): partial; ssa.1 done, others in flight
   - Phase 3 — mBERT + PH (0cm): blocked on mwk; mwk.1 in progress
   - Phase 4 — comparison and prediction tests (bt5): not started; depends on Phase 3
   - mwk (Kushnareva rewrite) — partial; mwk.1 (grab_weights port) and mwk.2-4 (notebook adaptations) ongoing
   - replication/ — Kushnareva's pipeline run end-to-end here for sanity / intuition; deliberately not a project deliverable
7. Pipeline-level diagram. **Insert `\input{figures/pipeline}` at the appropriate point in the prose.** Reference via \autoref{fig:pipeline}.

Length target: 5–7 pages.

Acceptance
- Kushnareva's three feature families named and described at the pipeline-implementation level (not redefined conceptually — Ch 5 does that)
- "What we keep" / "what we change" sections clearly delineated
- Statistical comparison story (Mann-Whitney, permutation, bottleneck/Wasserstein) named, not glossed
- bd epics referenced by ID: 5f9, ssa, mwk, 0cm, bt5; replication subtree mentioned
- `\input{figures/pipeline}` present in the chapter prose at a sensible point
- STYLE.md voice followed

Owner: cal (researcher)

title: Ch 7: Codebase tour
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 7 of the textbook: codebase tour. Walks the reader through every top-level directory, with particular care to distinguish reference/ (frozen Kushnareva), replication/ (Kushnareva's task run end-to-end here), and notebooks/ (the adapted cross-linguistic pipeline).

Files to modify
- docs/07-codebase.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- README.md (project root)
- CLAUDE.md
- AGENTS.md
- reference/KUSHNAREVA_README.md
- replication/README.md
- canon-terms/SCHEMA.md
- Directory listings of each top-level subtree (use `ls`)

Implementation steps
1. Open with the project layout (a tree diagram). Verify against actual `ls` output before drafting — do not invent files.
2. Section by section, top-level directories:
   - reference/ — frozen Kushnareva originals; Hard Rule (no AI edits, see CLAUDE.md). Sanity-check filenames against `ls reference/` before listing them. Currently: 3 notebooks (features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb, features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb, features_prediction.ipynb), grab_weights.py, ripser_count.py, stats_count.py, utils.py, KUSHNAREVA_README.md.
   - replication/ — separate subtree for running Kushnareva's pipeline end-to-end here for sanity check / feature intuition / hardware baseline. Per its README, "this is not a project deliverable." Notable files: scripts/launch_jupyter.sh, launch_kernel.sh, install_kernel_spec.py for the cgroup-capped kernel (forward-ref to Ch 8); grab_weights_compat.py is the four-line shim around the modern transformers tokenizer API that lets the frozen reference/ code run unchanged; editable copies of the three reference notebooks; the Kushnareva-2021-EMNLP.pdf.
   - notebooks/ — currently 4 baseline notebooks (descriptive, topological, aligned, comparison) for the fastText baseline (Phase 2). The Kushnareva adaptations land here later (mwk epic).
   - canon-terms/ — SCHEMA.md, three reports (color/emotion/kinship), 9 YAML term files (3 languages × 3 domains).
   - baselines/ — Python source modules: clustering.py, distances.py, topology.py, vectors.py.
   - data/ — gitignored. Corpora, KWIC extracts, attention matrices.
   - results/ — currently does not exist as a populated directory; the project's actual experiment outputs live at `replication/outputs/{attentions,barcodes,features}/`. Note this in the chapter.
   - tests/ — pytest tests (replication features, mmap regressions, kernel spec, etc.).
   - scripts/ — utility shells (download_fasttext, GitHub app token).
   - .beads/ — bd issue tracking; auto-syncs to .beads/issues.jsonl on git.
3. Section: bd workflow. Cite AGENTS.md. Briefly explain epics (Phase 1–5 = 5f9, ssa, 0cm, bt5, vdh), `/plan` and `/work` slash commands, ready-work and dependency model.
4. Section: hard rules from CLAUDE.md (frozen reference/, notebook-faithful adaptation, citations on canon term lists, bd for all task tracking).

Length target: 4–5 pages.

Acceptance
- Tree diagram included, generated from actual `ls` output
- reference/ vs replication/ vs notebooks/ distinction made explicitly
- Filenames listed in reference/ subsection match actual on-disk files
- `replication/grab_weights_compat.py` mentioned with its purpose
- `results/` is described accurately (not yet populated; outputs live at replication/outputs/)
- Hard rules called out
- bd workflow named with epic IDs (5f9, mwk, ssa, 0cm, bt5, vdh)
- STYLE.md voice followed

Owner: kit (implementer; knows code structure)

title: Ch 8: Tooling and infrastructure
description:
Summary
Draft Chapter 8 of the textbook: tooling and infrastructure. Explains the package stack, the CUDA-pinned ripserplusplus story, the cgroup-capped kernel wrapper, and the GPU on north.

Files to modify
- docs/08-tooling.tex

Files to read for context
- docs/STYLE.md
- requirements.txt — full file, including the ripserplusplus pinned commit comment block
- CLAUDE.md — hardware (north: RTX 5070 Ti, 16GB VRAM, 64GB RAM)
- replication/README.md — for the kernel wrapper section
- replication/scripts/launch_jupyter.sh, launch_kernel.sh, install_kernel_spec.py — the cgroup-capped kernel implementation

Implementation steps
1. Open with the package stack at a high level: torch (CUDA 12.4), transformers, ripser/ripserplusplus, giotto-tda, gensim (fastText), sklearn / scipy / numpy, networkx.
2. The ripserplusplus pinned-commit story:
   - Why it matters: ripserplusplus is the GPU ripser Kushnareva used.
   - The breakage: PyPI's 1.1.2 doesn't compile against CUDA 12+ (thrust API changed).
   - The fix: pinned to a specific commit on upstream master that has the fix (PR #14, merged 2023, never re-released).
   - Build prerequisite: nvcc + CUDA toolkit matching torch's CUDA major version.
   - The kind of detail that, undocumented, costs days of debugging.
3. The cgroup-capped kernel wrapper:
   - Problem: ripser barcode loops can OOM the host.
   - Solution: launch the kernel inside a `systemd-run --user --scope` cgroup v2 with MemoryMax and MemorySwapMax set. A runaway cell kills the kernel, not north.
   - Implementation: launch_jupyter.sh, launch_kernel.sh, install_kernel_spec.py.
   - Defaults: 48 GB RAM, 4 GB swap; tunable via env vars.
   - Requires cgroup v2 (north satisfies).
4. North hardware: RTX 5070 Ti (16 GB VRAM), 64 GB RAM. The 16 GB VRAM constraint shapes design decisions (e.g., batch sizes for mBERT forward passes; bd issue ph-project-wz1 documents an OOM that drove splits=20).
5. Reproducibility note: requirements.txt pin format, the --extra-index-url for CUDA torch wheels.

Length target: 3–4 pages.

Acceptance
- ripserplusplus pinned-commit story told completely
- cgroup-capped kernel wrapper explained with file references
- north hardware specified
- bd issue ph-project-wz1 cited in the OOM context (or equivalent if more accurate)
- STYLE.md voice followed

Owner: kit (implementer)

title: ssa.5 review-fixes: key ordering, std-vs-var golden, inf-bar assertion
description:
Six review-flagged fixes against the ssa.5 implementation. Three non-trivial,
three trivial. All fixes land in the feature/ssa-5-baseline-B worktree at
/home/anna/ph-project-ssa-5-baseline-B and ship in the same PR as ssa.5.

NON-TRIVIAL FIXES
=================

(1) baselines/topology.py:225-227 — key-insertion-order inconsistency in
    barcode_features() empty branch.

    The empty branch interleaves n_d_m and n_b_l keys by threshold:
        n_d_m_t0.25, n_b_l_t0.25, n_d_m_t0.5, n_b_l_t0.5, ...
    The non-empty branch groups them:
        n_d_m_t0.25, n_d_m_t0.5, n_d_m_t0.75, n_b_l_t0.25, n_b_l_t0.5, ...

    The docstring promises "deterministic order" because ssa.7 baseline_comparison
    will iterate keys to build a flat feature vector. Several real (lang, domain)
    rows have empty H1 (e.g. en/color, ru/color), so this divergence is already
    visible in results/baseline_B/features.csv.

    Fix: split the empty branch into two loops:
        for t in _THRESHOLDS:
            features[f"{prefix}_n_d_m_t{t}"] = 0.0
        for t in _THRESHOLDS:
            features[f"{prefix}_n_b_l_t{t}"] = 0.0

    Add a test asserting the key order is identical across empty and non-empty
    barcodes (e.g. compare list(features.keys()) for both cases).

(2) tests/baselines/test_topology.py — golden regression test (Test 4) cannot
    distinguish std from variance.

    The current _GOLDEN_D ladder produces 4 H0 bars all with identical length
    0.2; std == var == 0.0, so a regression that flipped std to var would not
    be caught.

    Fix: add a dedicated test (test_barcode_features_v_is_std) that builds a
    barcode with non-uniform bar lengths (e.g. 3 bars of lengths 0.1, 0.2, 0.5)
    and asserts:
        - h0_v ≈ np.std(lengths)         # passes
        - h0_v != approx(np.var(lengths))  # would fail if v silently meant var

    Either as a new test, or as a second golden case alongside _GOLDEN_D.

(3) tests/baselines/test_topology.py:94-98 — Test 1 (two-cluster sanity) does
    not assert that infinite bars are stripped.

    The assertion `h0[h0["death"] > 0.5]` passes trivially even if an inf bar
    slips through (np.inf > 0.5 is True). Test 5 (all-zero) covers the
    all-infinite case but no test explicitly checks the mixed finite+infinite
    case.

    Fix: in test_rips_barcode_two_cluster_sanity, before the inter_cluster_bars
    check, add:
        assert np.all(np.isfinite(h0["death"])), \
            "rips_barcode must strip the infinite H0 bar"

TRIVIAL FIXES
=============

(4) baselines/topology.py:252 — h{d}_e returns -0.0 for single-bar barcodes
    (IEEE 754 quirk: -np.sum([0.0]) == -0.0). Wrap with max(0.0, ...) to
    return clean +0.0.

(5) baselines/topology.py module docstring line 45 — stale "0.95" reference.
    _THRESHOLDS = (0.25, 0.5, 0.75); remove 0.95 from the docstring.

(6) tests/baselines/test_topology.py:143-144 — test_non_2d_raises lacks a
    `match=` regex on pytest.raises(ValueError, ...). The other five validation
    cases all use `match=`. Add one (e.g. match=r"2-D|ndim").

POST-FIX
========

After all six fixes:
- Run pytest tests/baselines/test_topology.py -v — all tests must pass, including
  the two new ones (key-order parity test and v-is-std test).
- Re-run the notebook end-to-end: features.csv should now have grouped column
  order in all rows (no interleaving in the H1 columns of empty-H1 rows).
- Verify results/baseline_B/features.csv H1 columns are in the documented
  grouped order.
close_reason: All 6 review fixes implemented; 13/13 tests pass; ships in same PR as ssa.5

title: Add NaN check to phase3 attention test files (parity gap)
description:
Both tests/test_mbert_attention_thresholds_features.py and tests/test_mbert_attention_ripser_features.py have a `_no_inf` test that asserts `np.sum(np.isinf(arr)) == 0` but does NOT check for NaN. Several feature paths (ripser barcode division-based features, threshold normalization) could in principle produce NaN from degenerate inputs.

Files to modify
---------------
- tests/test_mbert_attention_thresholds_features.py (test_features_no_inf)
- tests/test_mbert_attention_ripser_features.py (test_ripser_features_no_inf, test_template_features_no_inf)

Implementation
--------------
Add an assertion symmetric to the existing isinf check in each `_no_inf` test:

    n_nan = np.sum(np.isnan(arr))
    assert n_nan == 0, f"({lang!r}, {domain!r}): found {n_nan} NaN values in <feature> tensor."

Trivial follow-up; flagged in the mwk.3 pre-PR test-quality review as a parity gap rather than a regression — the thresholds test had the same gap and the ripser test mirrored it. Fixing both keeps them in sync.

title: Patch and run features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb for replication
description:
The barcode notebook (replication/notebooks/features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb)
is not yet runnable in the replication setup. Surfaced by reviewers on
ph-project-3nh and ph-project-nmh: the prediction notebook's np.load
calls for ripser_*.npy and templ_*.npy files depend on this notebook
producing them, and the shim from ph-project-nmh is not yet wired in.

Three layers of work:

1. Extend replication/scripts/patch_notebook.py with a BARCODE_PATCHES
   set covering: input_dir / output_dir paths, subset rename (\"test_5k\"
   to \"train\" / \"test\"), attention_name / attention_dir cell repointing,
   and any tokenizer-API patches the notebook needs.
2. Swap the bare `import ripser_count` lines (cells 23 and 31 per the
   review) to use replication.ripser_count_compat as ripser_count
   (or whatever final name nmh ships under).
3. Run the patched notebook for subset=\"train\" then subset=\"test\" to
   produce the ripser_*.npy and templ_*.npy files in
   replication/outputs/features/.

Performance note: CPU ripser is 10-50x slower than the GPU original.
500/class smoke run is fine; full subsets would be slow.

DISK NOTE: f93 ran out of disk because it persists ~4.7GB attention
.npy per subset. Re-running this notebook on top of that risks the
same. Either make sure f93's persisted attention dumps are gone first,
or rework the pipeline to stream attention through the feature
extraction without saving full tensors.

DISK CLEANUP (added 2026-05-02): ph-project-a9s shipped a disk-budget
markdown + cleanup cell injection for the threshold notebook, hardcoded
under `if name == \"features_calculation_by_thresholds.ipynb\":` in
patch_notebook.py:patch(). The same pattern needs to extend to this
notebook. When picking it up:
- The current conditional only matches the threshold name; widen it to
  include features_calculation_ripser_and_templates.ipynb, OR refactor
  to a data-driven per-notebook injection table parallel to _SUPPORTED.
- The cleanup cell SOURCE references `adj_filenames` and `subset` —
  threshold-notebook-local symbols. The ripser notebook uses
  `attention_dir` + `attention_name` per the xyo branch's RIPSER_PATCHES.
  Either reuse adj_filenames if the ripser notebook builds it the same
  way, or factor a per-notebook CLEANUP_CELL_SOURCE.

Soft-blocked by:
- ph-project-nmh PR landing (the shim must exist on main)
- ph-project-3nh PR landing (the dispatcher refactor in patch_notebook.py)

Verification: after running, the prediction notebook (ph-project-3nh)
can finally execute end-to-end.
close_reason: Superseded by wz1 (rewritten 2026-05-02 with corrected diagnosis) and its subtasks wz1.1/wz1.2/wz1.3. Most of qrs is already done in main: patch_notebook.py has the dispatcher refactor (_SUPPORTED), RIPSER_PATCHES (paths/subset/tokenizer/Pool→gc/splits=20), and the np.int + networkx-3.x compat shims in the setup cell. The ripser_count_compat shim qrs referenced never shipped — ph-project-nmh pinned ripserplusplus to upstream master with the cuda12 fix instead, so we run GPU ripser++ directly. Remaining 'actually run the notebook' bits are now wz1.3. Disk-cleanup-cell injection (a9s pattern) for the ripser notebook is a separate concern, not OOM-related; file as a fresh issue if needed.

title: Reconsider domain= log-context arg on extract_term_vectors
description:
Summary
-------
extract_term_vectors currently takes a domain: str | None = None argument
that has NO effect on computation — it only labels OOV log messages.
This mixes a computational concern (head_position override) with a
logging concern (domain label) on the same public signature.

Two options to consider once ssa.4/5/6 callers exist:

  1. Drop domain= entirely. Callers can use logging.LoggerAdapter or
     a contextvars-based scope to inject (lang, domain) context into
     warnings without adding a positional arg. This keeps the public
     surface tight.

  2. Keep domain= but document it explicitly as logging-only. The
     current docstring already says "Has no effect on computation"
     but the parameter still appears in tab-completion and IDE hints
     as if it were a normal argument.

Recommendation: revisit after ssa.4 lands and we see how callers
actually pass context. If every callsite passes (lang=, domain=) as
literals, option 2 is fine. If callers want richer per-call context
(e.g., file path, term-list version), option 1 wins.

Discovered during architecture review of ssa.3 (commit 9e6b348).

title: Consolidate phase3_comparison flatten functions into single dispatch
subsample_per_term: support groupby_columns=[...] for cross-language sampling
description:
## Summary

`replication/diagram_distances.py::subsample_per_term` groups by the single `term` column. The blr.2 CLI script needs to subsample per (lang, term) and currently works around this by temporarily mutating `combined_metadata["term"]` to `"<lang>/<term>"`, calling the subsampler, then restoring the column. This is a brittle pattern and any future script needing cross-language subsampling will repeat the workaround.

## Files to modify

- `replication/diagram_distances.py`: extend `subsample_per_term` to accept `groupby_columns: list[str] = None`. When `None`, default to `["term"]` for backward compatibility. When given, use `metadata_df.groupby(groupby_columns, sort=True)` instead of `metadata_df.groupby("term", sort=True)`.
- `scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py::_load_combined_barcodes`: replace the `_lang_term` workaround with `subsample_per_term(combined_metadata, n_per_term=..., seed=..., groupby_columns=["lang", "term"])`.
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py`: extend `TestSubsamplePerTerm` with a `groupby_columns=["lang", "term"]` test that asserts cross-language groups are subsampled independently.

## Why this matters

The blr.2 `_lang_term` workaround is a code smell that already showed up once. Generalizing the subsampler removes the smell at the source and gives blr.3+ a clean API.

## Acceptance criteria

- `subsample_per_term(metadata, n_per_term=2, groupby_columns=["lang", "term"])` returns indices that have at most 2 samples per `(lang, term)` combo, deterministic given the seed.
- Backward-compatible default (`groupby_columns=None` -> `["term"]`) means no other call sites break.
- `_load_combined_barcodes` is simplified — the `_lang_term` mutation is removed.
- All existing tests in `test_diagram_distances_loader.py` and `test_diagram_distances_compute.py` still pass.

title: Phase 2 — fastText baseline
description:
Phase 2 — static embedding baselines (fastText).

Three complementary sub-baselines that together test the project hypothesis
(shape of semantic domains differs across languages) using only static word
embeddings, before introducing mBERT attention:

A. Language-specific descriptive. Per-language fastText CC-300 vectors.
   Pairwise cosine distance matrix per (language, domain). Agglomerative
   clustering with dendrograms and k-means with silhouette sweeps.

B. Language-specific topological. Same distance matrices as A, passed through
   persistent homology (Vietoris-Rips / Ripser). Full Kushnareva-style barcode
   feature set (sum/mean/variance/entropy/threshold counts for H0/H1). Same
   topology pipeline as the main mBERT experiment — this is the primary foil.

C. Cross-lingual aligned. MUSE supervised aligned fastText. Cross-lingual
   cosine distances, joint clustering, joint PH on combined term clouds.
   Tests prediction #2 (emotion divergence > color divergence) descriptively.

All three consume the canon term lists from ph-project-5m7. Output: baseline
figures and feature tables for direct comparison with the main mBERT result
in Phase 4.

Hybrid implementation: computation in importable Python modules under
baselines/ (testable with pytest); narrative analysis in notebooks/ that
import from those modules. Mirrors the Kushnareva reference/ layout
(modules + notebooks).

Multi-word canon terms: take the head word (Anna's call — e.g., "maternal
uncle" → "uncle"; in English the non-head modifier is not salient, the
meaning sits in the head).
close_reason: All sub-baselines (A descriptive, B topological, C MUSE) plus comparison notebook complete and merged. Phase 2 done.

title: Download and cache fastText vectors (CC-300 + MUSE aligned)
description:
Summary
-------
Acquire and cache the static embedding vectors used throughout Phase 2 baselines.
Plumbing task only — no analysis yet. Runnable immediately; does not depend on
canon terms.

Vectors to download
-------------------
* fastText CC-300 (Common Crawl, 300-dim, .bin) for English, Russian, Spanish.
  Source: https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/crawl-vectors.html
  Files: cc.en.300.bin, cc.ru.300.bin, cc.es.300.bin
  Used by: sub-baselines A and B (language-specific).

* MUSE supervised aligned fastText for English, Russian, Spanish.
  Source: https://github.com/facebookresearch/MUSE#download (aligned wiki vectors).
  Files: wiki.multi.en.vec, wiki.multi.ru.vec, wiki.multi.es.vec (~600MB each).
  Used by: sub-baseline C (cross-lingual aligned).

PH library pin
--------------
Phase 2 commits to `ripser` (pure Python, CPU). Rationale: simpler install,
CPU is fine for ~30-term distance matrices, and the reference code's use of
`ripserplusplus` is only for the import line — the feature-extraction code
is library-agnostic (operates on already-computed barcodes). Add `ripser` to
requirements.txt if it isn't already confirmed at the expected version.
If Phase 3 mBERT attention PH is slow, file a follow-up to swap in
ripserplusplus (GPU) — that is a Phase 3 decision, not Phase 2.

Files to create
---------------
* baselines/__init__.py — empty package marker
* baselines/vectors.py — module exposing
    load_fasttext(lang: str, kind: str) -> KeyedVectors
  where kind ∈ {"cc", "aligned"}. For "cc", use gensim's
  load_facebook_model(...).wv; for "aligned", use
  KeyedVectors.load_word2vec_format(..., binary=False).
* scripts/download_fasttext.sh — reproducible download script with sha256 checks.
* data/fasttext/README.md — source URLs, file list, sha256s.

Data layout (gitignored)
------------------------
data/fasttext/cc/cc.{en,ru,es}.300.bin
data/fasttext/aligned/wiki.multi.{en,ru,es}.vec

.gitignore update
-----------------
Current .gitignore excludes data/fasttext/ and *.bin/*.npz/*.pt but NOT *.vec.
MUSE files are large .vec text files. Add `*.vec` to .gitignore (belt-and-
suspenders for any stray .vec files outside data/). Confirm existing
data/fasttext/ rule is in place (it is as of 2026-04-16).

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* README.md — pipeline overview
* CLAUDE.md — project conventions (notebook-faithful, no reaching into prior project)
* requirements.txt — gensim and ripser are already listed
* .gitignore — current state

Testing notes
-------------
tests/baselines/test_vectors.py — smoke test that loads each language for each
kind and asserts:
  - vector dim == 300
  - a known in-vocab word (e.g., "cat" / "кот" / "gato") returns a finite,
    non-zero vector

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Verify .gitignore covers data/fasttext/ (it does). Add `*.vec` as a
   belt-and-suspenders rule.
2. Pin PH library: ensure `ripser` is in requirements.txt at a known-good
   version. Do NOT add `ripserplusplus` in Phase 2.
3. Write scripts/download_fasttext.sh; verify sha256 of each file on download.
4. Create baselines/ package with __init__.py and vectors.py.
5. Implement load_fasttext with clear error message if file missing.
6. Write smoke tests.
7. Confirm CUDA / GPU is not required for this step (pure file IO + CPU PH).
close_reason: All six fastText vector files downloaded and SHA-256 verified. CC-300 binaries (en/ru/es) under data/fasttext/cc/, MUSE aligned vectors (en/ru/es) under data/fasttext/aligned/. Both data/fasttext/README.md table and EXPECTED_SHA256 dict in scripts/download_fasttext.sh populated with hashes.

title: Term vector extraction module (shared by A/B/C)
description:
Summary
-------
Extract (term × dim) matrices per (language, domain, embedding-kind) from
canon term lists and loaded fastText vectors. Shared input layer for
sub-baselines A, B, and C.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.1 (vectors downloaded and load_fasttext works)
* ssa.2 (module stubs exist)
* ph-project-5m7.{1,2,3} (canon term lists exist under canon-terms/)

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/distances.py — implement extract_term_vectors(terms, vectors, strategy)

Behavior
--------
Input: list of canon term strings, a KeyedVectors (or FastText model with
subword support), a strategy flag.

Strategies for multi-word terms (Anna's decision: head-word):
  * "head"  (default) — take the head word per language NP typology:
        EN: head-final in descriptive NPs: "maternal uncle"   → "uncle"     (right)
        RU: head-final with adjective modifier: "двоюродный брат" → "брат" (right)
        ES: head-INITIAL (Spanish NPs are head-initial):
              "tío materno"  → "tío"         (left)
              "hermano mayor" → "hermano"   (left)
        Implementation: per-language head-position lookup
          HEAD_POSITION = {"en": "right", "ru": "right", "es": "left"}
        Override-able via an optional per-call argument.
        If a term has no space, return as-is.
  * "mean"  — average vectors of component words (fallback strategy).
  * "skip"  — drop multi-word terms entirely.

OOV handling:
  * CC .bin (FastText) — subword composition returns a vector for any
    string; no miss. Verify via model.wv.has_index_for or equivalent and
    note that fastText `.vec` (text) files would NOT have this property.
  * MUSE aligned .vec — no subword fallback. Log misses to stderr and to
    a returned boolean mask. Dropped terms do NOT get a zero vector; they
    are excluded and flagged in the mask.

Return: tuple (matrix: np.ndarray of shape (n_found, dim),
               found_mask: np.ndarray[bool] of shape (n_terms,))

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* canon-terms/<lang>/<domain>.yaml — term list format (exists after ph-project-5m7)
* baselines/vectors.py — loader signatures
* gensim fastText docs (if needed for subword API questions)

Testing notes
-------------
tests/baselines/test_distances.py — unit tests for extract_term_vectors:
  * head strategy picks correct word for EN ("maternal uncle" → "uncle")
  * head strategy picks correct word for RU ("двоюродный брат" → "брат")
  * head strategy picks correct word for ES ("tío materno" → "tío")   [CRITICAL:
    confirms the left-head rule for Spanish; if this test fails the lookup
    is inverted]
  * OOV in MUSE-style vectors flags mask=False and drops the row
  * OOV in FastText CC-style vectors returns a finite vector (no drop)
  * output shape matches mask.sum()
Use a small synthetic gensim KeyedVectors fixture so tests don't load real vectors.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Implement the per-language HEAD_POSITION lookup:
     {"en": "right", "ru": "right", "es": "left"}
2. Implement the three strategies with explicit dispatch on the strategy arg.
3. Log OOV misses; include lang + domain + term in the log line.
4. Unit-test with synthetic KeyedVectors (build with gensim KeyedVectors(dim)
   and add_vectors to inject a tiny vocabulary). Include the three
   language-specific head-word tests above.
5. Smoke-run on one real (lang, domain) pair to confirm end-to-end.

title: Sub-baseline A: descriptive (clustering + dendrograms)
description:
Summary
-------
Compute cosine distance matrices and clustering structure per
(language, domain) on language-specific fastText CC-300 vectors.
This is the human-readable descriptive baseline.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.3 (term vector extraction)

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/distances.py — implement cosine_distance_matrix(X) -> np.ndarray
  Use scipy.spatial.distance.pdist(X, metric="cosine") + squareform.
* baselines/clustering.py —
    agglomerative_linkage(D, method="average") -> linkage matrix (scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage on a condensed distance matrix)
    kmeans_silhouette_sweep(X, k_range) -> dict mapping k -> silhouette score
* notebooks/baseline_A_descriptive.ipynb — full analysis

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load canon terms for each (language, domain) — 9 combinations.
2. Load fastText CC-300 for EN, RU, ES via baselines.vectors.load_fasttext.
3. Extract term vector matrices via baselines.distances.extract_term_vectors.
4. Compute per-(lang, domain) cosine distance matrices.
5. Agglomerative clustering + dendrogram per (lang, domain) — 9 dendrograms.
   Use matplotlib; label leaves with the canon term strings.
6. k-means silhouette sweep (k = 2..8) — silhouette plots per (lang, domain).
7. Summary table: cluster count suggested by silhouette peak per (lang, domain).
8. Save figures and summary CSV to results/baseline_A/.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* canon-terms/*.yaml
* baselines/distances.py, baselines/clustering.py, baselines/vectors.py
* reference/utils.py (style)

Testing notes
-------------
tests/baselines/test_distances.py —
  * test cosine_distance_matrix against scipy.spatial.distance.cosine on
    handpicked pairs
  * symmetric, zero diagonal, bounded [0, 2]
tests/baselines/test_clustering.py —
  * on synthetic 2-cluster data (well-separated), silhouette at k=2 > 0.5
  * agglomerative_linkage returns a (n-1, 4) linkage matrix

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Implement cosine_distance_matrix and the two clustering functions.
2. Write unit tests.
3. Walk through notebook with one (lang, domain) first as a smoke test.
4. Produce all 9 dendrograms + silhouette plots.
5. Save results/baseline_A/{dendrograms/, silhouette/, summary.csv}.

title: Sub-baseline B: topological (PH on static embeddings)
description:
Summary
-------
Compute persistent homology on the language-specific cosine distance
matrices. Reuse the full Kushnareva barcode feature set for direct,
apples-to-apples comparison with the main mBERT result. This is the
primary foil for the whole project.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.3 (term vector extraction)

PH library
----------
Use `ripser` (CPU) per ssa.1's Phase 2 pin. Do NOT introduce
`ripserplusplus` here.

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/topology.py —
    rips_barcode(D, max_dim=1) -> dict
        Wraps ripser.ripser on a precomputed distance matrix
        (distance_matrix=True). Validates D is square, symmetric,
        non-negative, finite, non-NaN. Returns
          {0: structured_array, 1: structured_array}
        where each structured array has 'birth' and 'death' float fields.
    barcode_features(barcode) -> dict
        Ports the full reference/ripser_count.py feature set, preserving
        function and feature names:
          h0_s, h0_m, h0_v, h0_e,
          h0_n_d_m_t<t>, h0_n_b_l_t<t>, h0_t_d, h0_t_b,
          plus the h1_* equivalents.

        NAMING CAVEAT — preserved for faithfulness:
        In reference/ripser_count.py, `h{d}_v` is computed by
        `barcode_std` (standard deviation), NOT by variance, despite the
        letter 'v'. Preserve this behavior and the naming exactly. Add
        a one-line docstring to barcode_features noting this:
        "h{d}_v computes std (not variance), matching Kushnareva naming."
        Do NOT silently "fix" the name to match variance — downstream
        comparison requires exact feature-value parity with the main
        mBERT result, which will use the same convention.

        Threshold values: match reference (0.25, 0.5, 0.75) unless a
        domain-specific rationale for changing them is documented.

* notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb — analysis notebook
* tests/baselines/test_topology.py — tests (see below)

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load term vectors and cosine distance matrices (reuse A's outputs).
2. Compute Ripser barcode per (language, domain).
3. Plot H0 and H1 barcodes side by side across languages per domain.
4. Extract full Kushnareva feature set per (language, domain).
5. Save barcodes (.npz) and feature table (.csv) to results/baseline_B/.
6. Early qualitative look at prediction #1: does Russian color H0 show
   more persistent structure (extra connected component) than EN or ES?

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* reference/ripser_count.py — source of barcode features; preserve naming
  AND the _v-is-std convention
* reference/utils.py — cutoff_matrix style
* baselines/distances.py — where cosine_distance_matrix lives
* ripser API docs

Testing notes
-------------
CRITICAL. The prior tda-project attempt had a silent ripser zero-output
bug (see ph-project-mwk.3). Tests must cover FIVE cases:

  1. Sanity — two well-separated clusters: distance matrix with two
     tight blocks produces exactly 2 persistent H0 bars (one infinite,
     one persistent above threshold).
  2. Input validation — non-square / non-symmetric / NaN / negative
     input raises a clear ValueError BEFORE calling ripser.
  3. Empty barcode — barcode_features on {0: empty, 1: empty} returns
     zeros for all features, never NaN.
  4. Regression — on a fixed synthetic distance matrix, features match
     precomputed expected values (golden numbers in test file).
  5. Degenerate input — all-zero distance matrix (all points coincident)
     produces the documented behavior: a single infinite H0 bar and
     empty H1. NOT a silent empty barcode. This is the exact prior-
     project failure mode.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Port feature-extraction functions from reference/ripser_count.py into
   baselines/topology.py, preserving names and signatures — INCLUDING
   the _v-is-std convention (document in docstring).
2. Implement rips_barcode with input validation (reject silently-bad input
   BEFORE calling ripser — this is the zero-output-bug firewall).
3. Add all five tests above; include the degenerate-input test as the
   explicit firewall against the prior bug.
4. Walk the notebook end-to-end on one (lang, domain).
5. Run on all 9 (lang, domain) combinations; save artifacts.

title: Sub-baseline C: cross-lingual aligned (MUSE)
description:
Summary
-------
Compute cross-lingual semantic distance using MUSE supervised aligned
fastText vectors. Supports prediction #2 (emotion divergence > color
divergence) descriptively. Formal tests happen in Phase 4.

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.3 (term vector extraction)

Files to modify
---------------
* notebooks/baseline_C_aligned.ipynb — cross-lingual analysis notebook

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load MUSE aligned vectors for EN, RU, ES via load_fasttext(..., kind="aligned").
2. Extract term matrices per (language, domain) with extract_term_vectors.
   Expect OOV to be higher than CC-300 case — MUSE has no subword fallback.
3. OOV acceptance gate (see below).
4. Cross-lingual cosine distance matrices per domain (rows: EN canon terms,
   columns: RU canon terms; same for EN-ES and RU-ES).
5. Joint distance matrix per domain: all three languages stacked into one
   term × term matrix. Label each row with (lang, term).
6. Joint agglomerative clustering per domain; dendrogram colored by language.
7. Joint Ripser PH per domain — what does the union cloud look like?
   Report H0 count (does Russian cluster separately from EN and ES in color?).
8. Prediction #2 descriptive check: mean cross-lingual cosine distance in
   EMOTION domain vs COLOR domain, per language pair. Table + bar chart.
9. Save to results/baseline_C/.

OOV acceptance gate
-------------------
For each (language, domain), record the OOV rate = (1 - found_mask.mean()).

Behavior by rate (hard-coded, not configurable — this is a correctness gate):
  * OOV ≤ 10%  — proceed. Log the misses in the notebook output.
  * 10% < OOV ≤ 25% — proceed with an explicit warning cell in the
      notebook. Report downstream results with a ⚠ marker. File a
      discovered-from issue linked to ssa.6 flagging the weak coverage
      for canon-terms followup (e.g., "Russian kinship MUSE OOV = 18%").
  * OOV > 25% — STOP. Skip the affected (lang, domain). Record it in
      results/baseline_C/oov_skipped.csv. Downstream joint analysis
      excludes the skipped combinations. ssa.7 (comparison notebook)
      must read this file and mark skipped cells as "insufficient
      coverage — baseline C" in its output tables.

File a discovered-from issue under ssa.6 for any (lang, domain) that
triggers the second or third tier, so term lists can be revisited.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* baselines/vectors.py, baselines/distances.py, baselines/clustering.py,
  baselines/topology.py
* notebooks/baseline_A_descriptive.ipynb — mirror its plotting patterns
* notebooks/baseline_B_topological.ipynb — mirror its PH plotting patterns

Testing notes
-------------
No new module code under test (notebook reuses existing functions).
Do log MUSE OOV rates prominently per (lang, domain) — they are the
primary reproducibility signal for this sub-baseline.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Walk the notebook end-to-end on the color domain first as smoke test.
2. Apply the OOV acceptance gate; file discovered-from issues as needed.
3. Produce cross-lingual distance heatmaps, joint dendrograms, joint
   barcodes for all (lang, domain) that pass the gate.
4. Save results/baseline_C/{heatmaps/, dendrograms/, barcodes/, pred2.csv,
   oov_skipped.csv}.

title: Baseline comparison notebook (writeup-ready figures)
description:
Summary
-------
Tie sub-baselines A, B, and C together into a cohesive comparison notebook
with paper-ready figures. Qualitative sanity-check of the four
pre-registered predictions from README.md. Formal statistical tests
happen in Phase 4 (ph-project-bt5).

Dependencies
------------
* ssa.4 (sub-baseline A results)
* ssa.5 (sub-baseline B results)
* ssa.6 (sub-baseline C results)

Files to modify
---------------
* notebooks/baseline_comparison.ipynb — cross-cutting analysis
* results/baseline_summary.csv — flat table of all baseline feature values
  (one row per (language, domain) with all of B's Kushnareva features +
  A's silhouette / cluster counts + C's cross-lingual distances)
* results/figures/ — paper-ready figure exports (.pdf and .png)

Notebook structure
------------------
1. Load artifacts from results/baseline_A/, results/baseline_B/,
   results/baseline_C/.
2. Side-by-side figures per domain:
     (A dendrograms) || (B barcodes) for EN, RU, ES.
3. Cross-lingual view from C adjacent to intra-lingual view from B per
   domain.
4. Pre-registered predictions — qualitative sanity check table:
     P1 (Russian blue split) — does B's Russian color H0 show extra
         structure (bar count, persistence) vs EN/ES?
     P2 (emotion > color divergence) — does C's mean cross-lingual
         distance table show emotion > color?
     P3 (Russian kinship complexity) — does B's Russian kinship have
         higher h0_s or h1_s than EN?
     P4 (head specialization) — OUT OF SCOPE for baseline; noted as
         requiring mBERT (Phase 3).
5. Export paper-ready figures to results/figures/.

Files to read for context
-------------------------
* results/baseline_A/, results/baseline_B/, results/baseline_C/
* README.md — pre-registered predictions list
* reference/features_prediction.ipynb — for figure-styling reference

Testing notes
-------------
No new module code. This is analysis + figure export.

Implementation steps
--------------------
1. Load all three sub-baseline result artifacts.
2. Assemble baseline_summary.csv with all features flattened.
3. Produce side-by-side comparison figures.
4. Write a markdown cell per prediction with qualitative verdict
   ("signal present in baseline" / "not present — awaits mBERT").
5. Export final figures to results/figures/ in PDF (vector) and PNG.

title: Test coverage for _load_combined_barcodes cross-language alignment
description:
## Summary

`scripts/compute_diagram_distances.py::_load_combined_barcodes` has zero test coverage despite being the most fragile logic in the distance-compute CLI. It concatenates en/ru/es per-(layer, head) sample lists with `list.extend()` in `LANGS` order, then uses `pd.concat(..., ignore_index=True)` for metadata, then temporarily mutates `combined_metadata["term"]` to `"<lang>/<term>"` so `subsample_per_term` groups within languages.

If diagram-list ordering ever diverges from metadata-row ordering (different reset_index behavior in `load_lang_barcodes`, future refactor of part-file globbing, etc.), distance-tensor entries would silently associate with the wrong (lang, term) — corrupting the downstream permutation tests in blr.3 / blr.4 without raising any exception.

## Files to modify

- `tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py` (extend with new test class).
- May also need to import `_load_combined_barcodes` directly from the script — the script already adds repo root to sys.path, so a `from scripts.compute_diagram_distances import _load_combined_barcodes` should work.

## Implementation steps

1. Add a `tests/data/synthetic_barcodes/` fixture builder OR generate fixtures in a tmp_path fixture inline. Build 3 minimal barcode JSONs (en/ru/es color, 1 layer × 1 head × ~20 samples each) where each language's H_0 features have a distinguishable signature (e.g. en in [0.0, 0.1], ru in [1.0, 1.1], es in [2.0, 2.1]).
2. Build matching minimal KWIC CSVs with 2-3 terms per language, deterministic row order.
3. Test that:
   - The combined diagram dict's per-key list length equals `sum(per_lang_n)`.
   - The combined metadata has the right total row count and row order (en first, then ru, then es).
   - For random sample-index `i`, `all_diagrams[(0, 0)][i]` has H_0 features matching the language at `combined_metadata.iloc[i]["lang"]` (using the per-lang signature).
   - After subsampling at `n_per_term=2`, the returned indices group into (lang, term) buckets of at most 2 each.
   - The combined_metadata `term` column is restored (NOT `lang/term`) on return.

## Why this matters

Subtasks 3-7 read `load_distance_tensor` and trust the embedded metadata. A silent misalignment here means every downstream statistical test runs on shuffled labels — invalid results without any error.

## Acceptance criteria

- `pytest tests/test_diagram_distances_compute.py::TestLoadCombinedBarcodes -v` passes (new test class).
- A deliberately broken concat order (e.g. iterate LANGS in reverse for diagrams but not metadata) makes at least one of the new tests fail.

title: Centralize supported-language list (used by vectors.py and distances.py)
description:
Summary
-------
The set of supported languages ({"en", "ru", "es"}) is currently encoded
in two places:

  * baselines/vectors.py:45 — _VALID_LANGS = frozenset({"en", "ru", "es"})
  * baselines/distances.py — HEAD_POSITION = {"en": "right", "ru": "right", "es": "left"}
    (keys are the supported language set)

Adding a fourth language means updating both. This is low-risk now (only
two call sites, three languages) but worth fixing before more callers
(ssa.4, ssa.5, ssa.6, and Phase 4 mBERT) lock in on the same hardcoded
literal.

Suggested fix
-------------
Move SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES to baselines/__init__.py (or a new
baselines/_constants.py) and have vectors.py and distances.py import
from there. HEAD_POSITION can stay in distances.py (it is distances-
specific), but its keys should equal SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES — add an
assertion at module import time to enforce that invariant.

Files to modify
---------------
* baselines/__init__.py (or new baselines/_constants.py) — define
  SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES
* baselines/vectors.py — import and use it for _VALID_LANGS
* baselines/distances.py — import and assert HEAD_POSITION.keys() ==
  set(SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES) at module import

Discovered during architecture review of ssa.3 (commit 9e6b348).

title: wz1.2 review-fix: tighten ripser mmap tests to actually guard regression
description:
Discovered during wz1.2 test-quality review. Two non-trivial gaps in the
new tests that would let a regression slip through silently.

Issue 1 (tests/test_ripser_mmap.py)
-----------------------------------
The parent-RSS test fixture is shape (8, 2, 2, 8, 8) float16 = ~4 KB on
disk. Threshold is 10 MB. Reviewer confirmed experimentally: even with
the OLD broken behavior (parent np.load(allow_pickle=True) + 20
fancy-index copies), the parent RSS delta is ~0.07 MB — two orders of
magnitude below the threshold. The test passes with the broken behavior
on this fixture, so it provides zero regression protection for its
load-bearing invariant.

Fix: bump fixture to ~25 MB on disk (shape (50_000, 2, 2, 8, 8) float16
suffices and still runs in under a second). Confirm experimentally that
the OLD pre-load pattern would now exceed the 10 MB threshold while the
new mmap path stays under.

Issue 2 (tests/test_patch_notebook.py)
--------------------------------------
test_barcode_loop_passes_filename_and_indices checks the substring
"filename, indices,". This passes even if someone wrote
(filename, indices, ntokens, ...) instead of the required
(filename, indices, ntokens[indices], ...). The critical semantic
difference vs. the old code is that ntokens is now sliced; if a future
patch accidentally passed the full array, no test would fail.

Fix: add an assertion that "ntokens[indices]" appears in the loop cell
source.

Acceptance
----------
- Parent-RSS test fails on the OLD pattern, passes on the NEW pattern,
  on a fixture large enough that this is meaningful.
- test_barcode_loop_passes_filename_and_indices asserts ntokens is
  sliced, not passed whole.
- Full test suite remains green.

title: Refactor sig_mat construction in phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb cells 12 and 23

Cells 12 and 23 of `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` each build a

This duplication is pre-existing from blr.3 (cell 12) and was carried into
blr.5 (cell 23) because the new `effect_heatmap_data` helper only covers
the float effect-size matrix, not the boolean BH-significance mask.

## Files to modify

- `notebooks/phase3_diagram_distances.ipynb` cells 12 and 23 — replace the manual `sig_mat` iterrows loop with a single call to the new helper.
- `tests/test_diagram_distances_aggregation.py` — add tests mirroring the existing `effect_heatmap_data` tests (shape, default value, missing cells default False, OOB raises).

## Why deferred

The architecture reviewer flagged this on blr.5; the scope of blr.5 was
specifically the effect-size pivot, and refactoring cell 12 (a closed
subtask's output) would have widened the PR. Filing this so the cleanup
isn't lost.

## Acceptance criteria

- New helper has unit tests.
- Cells 12 and 23 each call the new helper instead of duplicating the loop.
- All existing tests still pass.

title: Refactor to_giotto_format to dim-agnostic samples-list API
description:
## Summary

`to_giotto_format` (in `replication/giotto_format.py`) is currently shaped around the Kushnareva pipeline's per-(layer, head) packing — it takes `per_layer_head_diagrams: dict[tuple[int, int], list[dict[int, np.ndarray]]]` plus `layer`, `head`, and `dims` arguments. The draganov-pipeline caller in `draganov_replication/pd_distances.py:_bottleneck_matrix` has to fake a Kushnareva-shaped wrapper (`{(0, 0): [{dim: pd_arr} for pd_arr in pds]}`) to satisfy this signature even though it has nothing per-(layer, head) to express.

This wrapper-fiction works (the function only inspects the `(layer, head)` key and the resulting list of dicts), and it is documented in both the call site and the module docstring. But it is a code smell: the helper's API is shaped around one caller and the other caller has to lie about its data structure.

## Proposed refactor

Refactor `to_giotto_format` to a flatter, dim-agnostic signature:

```python
def to_giotto_format(
    samples: list[dict[int, np.ndarray]],
    dims: tuple[int, ...] = (0, 1),
) -> np.ndarray:
    """Pack a list of per-sample PDs into giotto-tda (N, F, 3) format."""
```

Then update the two callers:
- `replication/diagram_distances.py:compute_per_head_distances` — extract the `per_layer_head_diagrams[(layer, head)]` list and pass directly.
- `draganov_replication/pd_distances.py:_bottleneck_matrix` — pass `[{dim: pd_arr} for pd_arr in pds]` and `dims=(dim,)` directly. Drop the wrapper dict.

The `(0, 0, hom_dim)` padding contract (the load-bearing landmine) stays exactly as-is.

## Files to modify

- `replication/giotto_format.py` — change signature, update docstring.
- `replication/diagram_distances.py` — update the single existing caller.
- `draganov_replication/pd_distances.py` — drop the wrapper dict.
- `tests/test_giotto_format.py` — update tests to the new signature; the `test_keyerror_on_missing_layer_head` test gets retired (no more layer/head args) and replaced with a `test_empty_samples_list` test.

## Acceptance

- `pytest tests/test_giotto_format.py tests/test_diagram_distances_loader.py tests/test_draganov_replication_pd_distances.py` all green.
- The padding-contract test (`test_padding_uses_hom_dim`) still passes — the contract is unchanged, only the call shape moves.

## Note

Surfaced by the architecture reviewer on `ph-project-inu.3` (see PR for `inu.5` + `inu.3`). Non-blocking; the wrapper-fiction is self-documenting and does not regress correctness. Priority 3.
